This is because the greater the will to receive that a vessel acquires, the more it is distanced from the Creator and His light and the more material and dark it becomes.

keter1.bio: *Keter* is the name of the first and highest [sefira](/topics/sefira2) of the ten [sefirot](/topics/sefirot). However, *Keter* is not always counted as one of the ten [sefirot](/topics/sefirot). Just as a crown, a *keter*, on a person’s head encompasses him but is not part of his body, so too the [sefira](/topics/sefira2) of *Keter*, due to its lofty status, can be considered above the ten [sefirot](/topics/sefirot). When *Keter* is regarded as part of the system, it is the highest part of the [head](/topics/head2), which is comprised of the first three [levels](/topics/level): *Keter*, [Ḥokhma](/topics/hokhma1), and [*Bina*](/topics/bina3). When *Keter* is not included in the list of [sefirot](/topics/sefirot), it is substituted by *Da’at*, a structure that can be considered a pseudo-*sef

haggadah.bio: The haggadah is the classic ritual text that serves as the base and script of the Passover seder. Its text was compiled during the periods of the Mishnah and the Talmud, with later medieval additions. It is the most printed Jewish book as new editions and new commentaries are published every year. Different communities around the world have also developed their own variations and ritual practices, often recorded in the editions produced in their own communities.

miracles.bio: Miracles feature throughout the Jewish textual tradition. Supernatural events appear throughout the Bible, including the [ten plagues](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/the-ten-plagues?sort=Relevance&tab=sources), the [splitting of the sea](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/splitting-of-the-red-sea?tab=sources), and when the walls of [Jericho](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/jericho?tab=sources) fell, among others. Miracles feature in later rabbinic texts as well, and ancient through contemporary works analyze the nature and significance of miracles.

fasting.bio: There are assorted fast days scattered across the Jewish calendar. During the two major fast days, Yom Kippur and Tisha B'Av, one abstains from food, drink, washing, and sexual relations for 25 hours. During the other, minor fast days, one abstains from food and drink from sunup to sundown.

mar-ukva.bio: Mar Ukva was among the first of the exilarchs - administrative leaders of the Jewish community in Babylonia who claimed descent from the House of David. He was an important judge and scholar and a student of Shmuel. Known for his piety and wealth, he was famous for his discretion and generosity.

rachel.bio: Rachel is one of the four matriarchs of the Jewish people. The beloved and favored wife of [Jacob](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/jacob?sort=Relevance&tab=sources), who was tricked into first marrying her older sister [Leah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/leah?tab=sources), Rachel suffered from infertility before giving birth to Jacob's two favored sons, Joseph and Benjamin. She died giving birth to Benjamin.

yom-tov.bio: Yom Tov (literally: "good day") refers to any Jewish holiday marked by ritual blessings over the wine and abstention from work: the beginning and end of Passover, Shavuot, Rosh HaShanah, Yom Kippur, the beginning and end of Sukkot, Shemini Atseret, and Simchat Torah.

jerusalem.bio: Jerusalem — Yerushalayim in Hebrew — is considered the holiest city in the Jewish textual tradition. Although it is not mentioned explicitly in the Torah, the book of Deuteronomy refers numerous times to "the place that God will choose," a phrase later sources understood to refer to Jerusalem. In the book of Samuel, [King David](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/king-david?tab=sources) captured Jerusalem from the Jebusites, and the book of Kings recounts that [King Solomon](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/king-solomon?tab=sources) built the Temple there. Jerusalem is a central focus of many Jewish prayers, customs, and texts.

tzaraat.bio: Tzara'at is a biblical disease that involved discoloration of one's skin, home, or clothing. Once confirmed by a priest's inspection, it renders the subject ritually impure, requiring them to leave the camp for a period, undergo further inspection, and then a purification process. Tzara'at is the subject of [Leviticus 13–14](https://www.sefaria.org/Leviticus.13?lang=bi&aliyot=0). Several biblical stories have characters inflicted with tzara'at. And tzara'at is the subject of the Mishnah's tractate [Negaim](https://www.sefaria.org/Mishnah_Negaim?tab=contents).

peace.bio: The Hebrew word for "peace," *shalom,* is related to the Hebrew word that means whole or perfect, *shalem.* This suggests that there is a relationship between peace and wholeness. Judaism values peace very highly, prioritizing it over nearly everything else, as is reflected in biblical, rabbinic, liturgical, ethical, and legal texts.

mishnah.bio: The [Mishnah](https://www.sefaria.org/texts/Mishnah) is the first major work of rabbinic literature, consisting of teachings transmitted over hundreds of years and compiled around 200 CE. It covers agricultural, ritual, civil, criminal, and Temple-related laws, presenting a multiplicity of legal opinions and incorporating occasional stories. It is a foundation of the Jewish oral tradition, which continues with the [Talmud](https://www.sefaria.org/texts/Talmud/Bavli), a work that is structured as commentary on the Mishnah.

aliases: Yitro, the priest of Midian, Moses' father-in-law
yitro.bio: Yitro was the father of Moses's wife [Tzipporah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/tzipporah1?tab=sources) and a Midianite priest. He served as Moses's advisor in the desert.

aliases: Rabbi Yehudah b. Shimon b. Pazi
Rav Yehuda b. Rabbi Shimon b. Pazi
Rabbi Yehudah ben Shimon ben Pazi
Rav Yehuda ben Rabbi Shimon ben Pazi
rabbi-yehudah-b-pazi.bio: R. Yehudah b. Pazi was the son and student of R. Shimon b. Pazi. As he grew into an outstanding scholar and judge, other scholars would inquire about his father's teachings from him.

yesod1.bio: The [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of *Yesod* is the ninth of the ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot). When the [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) are divided into the five primary [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) – [*Keter*](/topics/keter1), [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1), [*Bina*](/topics/bina3), [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1), and [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) – *Yesod* is encompassed in the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1).  *Yesod* is the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) that represents the end of the middle line or column, the line of [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1). Accordingly, *Yesod* is a unique aspect of the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1) and also exists in parallel in the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1), which receives

golden-calf.bio: Shortly after the giving of the Torah at Mount Sinai, despairing that their leader Moses had died atop the mountain, the Israelites built and began to worship a golden calf — in direct violation of the first commandments of the [Ten Commandments](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.20.3?lang=bi&aliyot=0). The "golden calf" may refer to either the idol the Israelites built or [the narrative](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.32?lang=bi&aliyot=0). Upon Moses' discovery of the Israelites' grievous transgression, he smashed the tablets of the law, ground up the golden calf, and made the Israelites drink the mixture.

wine.bio: Wine (yayin, in Hebrew) is widely discussed in biblical and rabbinic sources, with the focus being on its enjoyment as well as its potential for abuse. On Shabbat, as well as on Jewish holidays, special prayers are said over wine in order to fulfill the biblical commandment of sanctifying the day.

rabbi-hama-b-r-hanina.bio: Rav Hama b. Hanina was a teacher in the time of R. Yochanan and possibly the son of R. Hanina b. Hama. Not much is known about him, but we are told that he used his wealth to build a synagogue in Sepphoris.

tisha-bav.bio: Tisha B’Av, the ninth day of the Hebrew month of Av, is a fast day and the saddest day on the Jewish calendar. Among other historical tragedies, it commemorates the destruction of the First and Second Temples in Jerusalem in 586 BCE and 70 CE, respectively. One fasting abstains from food and other physical pleasures, reads the biblical book of Lamentations, and observes mourning customs.

tefillin.bio: Tefillin are black leather boxes that contain small parchment scrolls on which specific passages from the Torah are written. Tefillin are worn on the upper arm and forehead, usually during morning prayers, except on Shabbat and holidays, as the means of fulfilling the commandment to "bind them as a sign on your hand and as a symbol between your eyes" [(Deuteronomy 6:8)](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.6.8?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en).

light-of-giving.bio: The light of giving (*or ḥassadim*) is the type of [*shefa*](/topics/shefa1)  that is revealed as a result of the will or desire of a created being to give. This light originates in the second [level](/topics/level) of the [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot), the [level](/topics/level) of [*Bina*](/topics/bina3). This is in contrast to the other type of [*shefa*](/topics/shefa1), the light of [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1), which is the light of the Creator that emanates in and from the first [level](/topics/level) of the [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot), the [level](/topics/level) of [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1). Although the light of giving is not the main lifeforce of the created beings, it contains an important quality related to the [constriction](/topics/constriction) (*tzimtzum*). Constriction inv

head1.bio: The head refers to the highest part of a [level](/topics/level). For example, in each [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1), the first three [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) are considered the head of that [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1). Additionally, the root of every [level](/topics/level) may also be defined as its head. This might occur when the [returning light](/topics/returning-light1) resulting from a [*fusion through collision*](/topics/fusion-through-collision) with a [partition](/topics/partition2) [enclothed](/topics/enclothe) the supernal light. While this process ultimately allows for the [returning light](/topics/returning-light1) to serve as a complete vessel for the supernal light, initially it only serves as the conceptual root of that actualized vessel. This initial stage of [encloth

blessings-(halakhah).bio: Blessings are declarations of acknowledgement to God about His greatness, His gifts or His connection with the Jewish people via the commandments. While they follow several formats, they all include the key phrase, "Blessed are You, O Lord!"

amalek.bio: Amalek was the first nation to attack the Israelites in the desert, attacking them from behind so as to target the weak and weary stragglers. As a result of this, paradoxically, the Torah commands the Israelites simultaneously to remember what they did and to blot out even the memory of them.

rabbi-yitzhak-nappaha.bio: R. Yitzchak Nappacha was a student of R. Yochanan. When his academy moved to Caesarea, he moved to Babylonia, but eventually rejoined his colleagues. He was a renowned authority in both legal and homiletical matters.

kindness.bio: Treating others with kindness is a foundational Jewish practice and is considered one of [three pillars on which the world stands](https://www.sefaria.org/Pirkei_Avot.1.2?lang=bi). Biblical characters like Abraham exemplify kindness in their generous treatment of strangers, and [rabbinic texts](https://www.sefaria.org/Sotah.14a.4?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) instruct people to follow God’s example of behaving with kindness.

lashon-hara.bio: Evil speech — *lashon hara,* in Hebrew — refers to the prohibition against speech that is harmful to another person, even though it may be true. The parameters, laws, and spiritual implications of *lashon hara* are discussed throughout the Jewish textual tradition and are a focus of Rabbi Yisrael Meir Kagan's [Chafetz Chaim](https://www.sefaria.org/Chafetz_Chaim?tab=contents), the first work to organize the laws of proper speech comprehensively.

vows.bio: A neder is a vow taken voluntarily. A vow can forbid one from deriving pleasure from particular objects or actions, obligate one to perform an action in the future, or declare that an individual is becoming a [Nazirite](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/nazir?tab=sources), among other functions. Stories of vows feature throughout the Bible, and many laws relating to vows are derived from [Numbers 30](https://www.sefaria.org/Numbers.30.3?ven=The_Contemporary_Torah,_Jewish_Publication_Society,_2006&lang=en&aliyot=0).

kashrut.bio: Kashrut means “fitness” and refers to the dietary laws governing what is permitted and forbidden for consumption. These laws further prescribe how kosher food should be prepared and by whom. While the Torah dictates which animals may be consumed and under what conditions, the rabbis further derived principles to ensure that one does not violate the Torah and discuss the value of these practices.

rabbi-helbo.bio: R. Helbo was a student of Rav Huna in Sura, and then moved to Israel. His understanding of homiletics and ethics, however, was formed by R. Shmuel b. Nachmani.

time.bio: Time is a concept that appears from the very beginning of the Torah, as the world's creation unfolds over seven days, each of which is divided into evening and morning. Sources throughout the Jewish textual tradition discuss how God and people sanctify time, different ways of relating to the passing of time, how time governs the performance of commandments, and how to make the most of one's time.

prophets.bio: Traditional Jewish sources [describe](https://www.sefaria.org/Megillah.14a.4?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) how 48 prophets and seven prophetesses prophesied on behalf of the people of Israel. Many of their teachings are recorded in the Prophets section of the Bible, which consists of collections of prophetic speeches and narrative accounts of history attributed to prophets. These prophets covered the period from the people of Israel's entrance into the land of Israel through the beginning of the Second Temple. In the Torah, [Abraham](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.20.8?lang=bi&aliyot=0) and [Moses](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.34.10?lang=bi&aliyot=0) are also called prophets, and the Torah warns against listening to a [false prophet](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy

justice.bio: The Torah commands [“Justice, justice you shall pursue”](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.16.20?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) as a condition for inhabiting the land of Israel. The Torah further commands specific measures to be put in place in order to establish just societies, including just courts and fair and honest weights and measures. Several biblical prophets critique those who prioritize ritual practice but ignore the Torah’s imperatives to act justly, and sources throughout the rabbinic tradition discuss and analyze the requirements and parameters of justice.

humility.bio: The Jewish tradition emphasizes that humility is a desirable character trait. This trait is exhibited by biblical characters — most notably [Moses](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/moses?tab=admin) — analyzed by commentators who discuss the balance between humility, self-worth, and self-recognition, and celebrated by works of musar that offer guidance on cultivating humility.

ahasuerus.bio: Ahasuerus was the king of Persia in the [book of Esther](https://www.sefaria.org/Esther?tab=contents). He agreed to his advisor Haman's plans to eradicate the Jews and confiscate their property, but his Jewish wife Esther and her cousin Mordekhai foiled the plot when Esther revealed her true identity as a Jew.

yoav.bio: Yoav was King David's nephew and general, who led his forces against the rebellion of David's son Avshalom. After later joining the rebellion of David's son Adoniah, David ordered Solomon to kill him.

hannah.bio: Hannah is a biblical character from the book of Samuel who struggles with infertility. She visits the [Tabernacle](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/mishkan?tab=admin) and prays verbally for a child rather than simply offering a sacrifice. God takes note of her, and she gives birth to a son, the prophet Samuel, who would lead the Israelites. In Jewish tradition, Hannah becomes a model for verbal prayer and for audacity in beseeching God.

zedekiah.bio: Zedekiah was the last king of Judah before it fell to the Babylonians. He was taken captive and brought to Babylon where he died.

seder.bio: The seder is a ritual dinner and discussion held on the first or first two nights of Passover. Attendees retell the story of the exodus from Egypt and eat special foods to mark the occasion.

light.bio: Light, both physical and metaphorical, features prominently throughout the Jewish textual tradition. Among other instances, light is the first thing God creates in the book of Genesis, is a focus of the holiday of [Chanukkah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/chanukkah?tab=sources), and is a central concept in kabbalastic literature, representing divine emanations.

rabbi-shimon-b-abba.bio: Rav Shimon b. Abba studied under R. Chaninah b. Chamah and was admired by R. Yochanan as someone worthy of ordination. Though he moved to Damascus, R. Abahu convinced him to come back to Israel.

tzitzit.bio: Tzitzit are the ritual fringes added to four-cornered garments. Four strings are threaded through each corner and folded over and knotted in order to produce eight fringes. Ideally, three of the strings should be white and the fourth should be azure, which is known as tekhelet in Hebrew.

water.bio: Water features prominently from the opening chapter of the Torah, as God separates between the waters of the sky and the waters below. Water plays a role in many other biblical narratives, like the splitting of the Red Sea or the miraculous supply of water provided to the people of Israel in the desert. Later Jewish sources discuss water extensively, acknowledging its physical necessity as a life-sustaining element and spiritual significance, and delving into details about its usage in Temple rituals, as a metaphor for Torah, and more.

the-spies.bio: The 12 spies feature in the books of Numbers and Deuteronomy. They were a group of leaders – one from each tribe – sent by Moses to scout the land of Canaan ahead of the people of Israel's arrival there. They came back reporting that the land was good but filled with giants and fearsome enemies. This prompted the Israelites to cry and complain that they did not want to enter the land. Two of the 12 spies, Joshua and Caleb, attempted to assuage their fears.

arikh-anpin1.bio:
The [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1) of [*Keter*](/topics/keter1) is called *Arikh Anpin*, or “the long face,” on account of the essence of the [light of *Ḥokhma*](/topics/light-of-hokhma) within it. [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1) is called [*Ze’er Anpin*](/topics/zeer-anpin1), or “the small face,” because it contains only a slight illumination from the [light of *Ḥokhma*](/topics/light-of-hokhma). The names *Arikh Anpin* and [*Ze’er Anpin*](/topics/zeer-anpin1) correspond to one another, for they refer only to the [light of *Ḥokhma*](/topics/light-of-hokhma) in the [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1), since the [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1) is called the [light](/topics/light2) of the face, as stated. This must be understood (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 3, Q & A 13).  
The [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkh

mourning.bio: Jewish customs and practices surrounding the death of an immediate family member – like burial and tearing one’s clothing – often have roots in biblical texts. Later Jewish works elaborate on how to act during different stages of mourning – before burial, the week following the burial, the month following death, and the year following death – and how practices may differ depending on the mourner's relationship to the deceased. Many Jewish mourning practices embody values such as  [honoring the dead](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/laws-of-honor-for-the-dead?tab=admin) and elevating their souls.

edom.bio: Edom, meaning “red,” is the name of the country founded by Jacob’s brother Esau, who the Bible says was born “red all over.” Edom was a rival of Israel and later became associated with the Romans and the West. Edom is located in present-day Jordan.

caleb.bio: Caleb (Kalev ben Yefuneh, in Hebrew) was one of the twelve spies sent to scout out Canaan soon after the Exodus. While most of the spies were intimidated by the Canaanites and their walled cities and returned discouraging reports, Caleb gave a more favorable account and was allowed to go into the promised land.

splitting-of-the-red-sea.bio: The splitting of the Red Sea is an event described in [Exodus 14:1–15:21](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.14.1?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=he&aliyot=0), in which God miraculously splits the sea, and the Israelites cross through on dry land. When the Egyptians try to follow the Israelites, the sea closes in on them, and they drown.

free-will.bio: Jewish texts have explored the concept of free will for thousands of years. A paradigmatic narrative through which the topic is often discussed is God’s [hardening of Pharoah’s heart](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/the-hardening-of-pharaohs-heart?sort=Relevance&tab=admin) in the Exodus narrative. Jewish thinkers analyze the interplay between free will and God’s omnipotence and consider the practical implications of free will in daily life.

shofar.bio: The shofar is made from the horn of an animal, usually a ram. In the Bible, the shofar was blown as a trumpet in battle and, today, is used primarily during Rosh Hashanah services and at the end of Yom Kippur as a “wake up call” to repentance or to announce God the King.

yirat-shamayim.bio: *Yirat Shamayim* — translated as “fear of heaven,” “awe of heaven,” or “reverence of heaven” — refers to an attitude toward God and is often considered an essential commandment and the basis of religious observance. In the Torah, “fear of God” connotes general ethical conduct, as may be the case in the Torah’s first use of the phrase in the story of [Abraham’s visit to Gerar](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.20.11?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). Rabbinic literature and later Jewish texts expand on the concept of fearing God — claiming it is the one thing that God [does not control](https://www.sefaria.org/Berakhot.33b.23?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) — analyze how fear of God intersects with [love of God](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/love-of-god1?tab=admin), and discuss how to ac

birth.bio: Childbirth is discussed throughout the Jewish textual tradition in a variety of contexts. The [charge to procreate](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.1.28?ven=The_Koren_Jerusalem_Bible&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) is the first commandment in the Torah, and [painful childbirth](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.3.16?ven=The_Koren_Jerusalem_Bible&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) features as a punishment for Eve's role in eating from the tree of knowledge. Rabbinic sources discuss permitting otherwise prohibited actions for the sake of aiding childbirth, and sources of Jewish thought analyze the symbolic significance of the birthing process.

leah.bio: Leah is one of the matriarchs of the Jewish people and the first wife of [Jacob](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/jacob). Her father tricked Jacob into marrying her and she suffered from the knowledge that she was unloved, in contrast to Jacob's beloved second wife, her sister [Rachel](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/rachel). She was blessed with six sons, who each became tribes in their own right, and one daughter.

hillel.bio: Hillel was one of the most influential of the early sages, active toward the end of the first century BCE and the beginning of the first century CE. Coming from Babylon to the land of Israel, he became famous for his scholarship and his patient and tolerant manner. Bolstered by his Davidic lineage, he served as *nasi* (president) of the [*Sanhedrin*](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/sanhedrin?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) (judicial assembly).

haftarah.bio: A haftarah is a section from the Prophets section of the [Bible](https://www.sefaria.org/texts/Tanakh), commonly read aloud in synagogues following the Torah reading on Shabbat and holidays. Each Torah portion has a corresponding and generally thematically connected haftarah portion, though the exact section of Prophets can vary by communal custom.

rabbi-shimon-b-yehudah.bio: R. Shimon b. Yehudah was a devoted student of R. Shimon b. Yochai and lived near Acre.

terumah.bio: Terumah is the tithe, or heave offering, taken from produce grown in the land of Israel and given to the [kohanim, or priests](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/priesthood?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources).

rambam.bio: Rabbi Moses ben Maimon (Rambam, also known as Maimonides) was perhaps the greatest intellectual and spiritual figure of post-talmudic Judaism. He wrote indispensable works of philosophy, halakhah, commentary, and responsa. Born in Spain, his family fled while he was still a youth. After an extended period in Morocco, he settled in Egypt, where he became the *naggid* (leader) of the Jewish community. His works were all foundational in their field. He was the first to produce a comprehensive commentary on the entire Mishnah. His great work of philosophy, *Moreh Nevukhim,* spawned an entire discipline and had incalculable influence upon enthusiastic promoters and vehement opponents alike. His code of law, *Mishneh Torah,* is the first and unsurpassed comprehensive code of Jewish law and pract

mount-sinai.bio: Mount Sinai (Har Sinai) is famously where Moses and the Israelites heard the Ten Commandments and received the Torah. Although it was the location of this historic event, the mountain in the middle of the Sinai Desert was sacred only as long as God present.

aliases: Jonah b. Amittai, the prophet from Gat-Hepher
Jonah b. Amittai the prophet from Gat Hepher
jonah.bio: Jonah is the protagonist of the Book of Jonah. A reluctant prophet, he tried to avoid his instruction to prophecy destruction to Nineveh. He fleed by boat but was tossed overboard during a storm and swallowed by a great fish. After being saved by God, he went to Nineveh, whose people heeded his warnings and repented.

rav-hiyya-b-yosef.bio: [Rav] Hiyya b. Yosef was a student of Rav who would exchange Rav's teaching with Shmuel's responses. When Rav died, he studied under Shmuel and then went to Israel, where he taught and studied with R. Yochanan, again bringing the teachings of Rav.

poverty.bio: Jewish texts acknowledge the existence of poverty and greatly value attempts to alleviate it. Caring for the poor is a commandment in the Torah, and later halakhic (legal) authorities detail practical applications of the obligation. In sources of Jewish thought, poverty has been held up as a positive, refining quality as well as a potential punishment for sins. In any case, one is required to treat a poor person with respect and dignity.

six-extremities.bio: The six extremities refers to the six directional edges or limits of every physical being. They are: Above, below, right, left, front, and back. These six extremities are paralleled by the six [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) that represent the [body](/topics/body3) of a [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1): [*Ḥesed*](/topics/hesed), [*Gevura*](/topics/gevura), [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1), [*Netzaḥ*](/topics/netzah), [*Hod*](/topics/hod1), and [*Yesod*](/topics/yesod1). .

nature.bio: Nature is discussed throughout Jewish literature in a variety of contexts. Many Jewish sources point to nature as a manifestation of godliness on earth, while others describe the importance of caring for our surroundings .

rabbi-pinchas.bio: R. Pinchas was a student of R. Yosi of Tiberias and a colleague of R. Huna and R. Hizkiyah. He lived in Tiberias and later moved to Tzippori.

parashat-bereshit.bio: Bereshit ("In the Beginning”) is the first parashah in the annual Torah reading cycle, which restarts at the beginning of the book of Genesis. The reading begins with God’s creation of the world. The first people, Adam and Eve, eat from the Tree of Knowledge and are then banished from the Garden of Eden. Their elder son, Cain, kills their younger son, Abel, and Cain is destined to a life of wandering.

dreams.bio: Dreams are discussed throughout Jewish literature in a variety of contexts. The Bible features several dreams, such as those in the narrative of Joseph in Genesis. Generations of Jewish sources have analyzed the significance and role of dreams.

giving-of-the-torah.bio: The giving of the Torah is an event described in [Exodus 19–20](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.19?lang=bi&aliyot=0) and [Deuteronomy 5](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.5.2?lang=bi&aliyot=0), in which God gives the Torah to the Israelites at Mount Sinai. Accompanied by fire, smoke, a cloud, thunder, and lightning, God commands the Ten Commandments. The event is considered a foundational moment in Jewish tradition and is celebrated on the holiday of [Shavuot](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/shavuot?tab=sources).

abba-and-imma-ilain.bio: *Abba* and *Ima Ila'in* (literally "supernal father and mother") is the name for the [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1) formed out of the first three [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) of the entire [level](/topics/level) of [*Bina*](/topics/bina3) that was ejected from the [head](/topics/head2) of [*Arikh Anpin*](/topics/arikh-anpin1) as a result of the [second constriction](/topics/second-constriction) and the ascent of [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1). This [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1) [enclothes](/topics/enclothe) [*Arikh Anpin*](/topics/arikh-anpin1) from the [mouth](/topics/mouth1) to the chest.

atonement.bio: Atonement, or kapparah in Hebrew, is the process of bringing about forgiveness for a sin. In the Jewish textual tradition, atonement may be achieved through repentance, prayer, sacrifice, punishment, the rituals of [Yom Kippur](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/yom-kippur?sort=Relevance&tab=sources), and other methods.

aliases: Simeon b. Shetah President of the Court
shimon-b-shetach.bio: During the tumultuous reign of Alexander Yannai, the third pair of the Zugot, Yehudah ben Tabbai and Shimon ben Shetach, faced significant challenges. Despite being Yannai's brother-in-law, Shimon had to go into hiding during Yannai's persecution of the Pharisees, while Yehudah fled to Egypt. Nonetheless, Shimon successfully wrested control of the Sanhedrin from the Sadducees.

rabbi-yishmael-b-yohanan-b-berokah.canonical: Rabbi Yishmael b. Yohanan b. Berokah
rabbi-yishmael-b-yohanan-b-berokah.canonicalHe: רבי ישמעאל בנו של רבי יוחנן בן ברוקה
aliases: Rabbi Yishmael b. Yohanan b. Berokah
Rabbi Yishmael b. Rabbi Yohanan b. Beroka
Rabbi Yishmael ben Yohanan ben Berokah
Rabbi Yishmael ben Rabbi Yohanan ben Beroka
Rebbi Ismael b. Rebbi Johanan b. Beroqa
Rebbi Ismael b. Rebbi Johanan b. Baroqa
rabbi-yishmael-b-yohanan-b-berokah.bio: R. Yishmael b. Yohanan b. Berokah was a contemporary of R. Shimon b. Gamliel and is often cited together with him.

parashat-shemini.bio: Shemini (“Eighth”) opens with the consecration of the Tabernacle (portable sanctuary). Two of [Aaron’s](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/aaron?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) sons are consumed by a fire sent from God when they attempt to offer an “alien fire.” God describes the animals, birds, and fish that are permissible and prohibited for consumption, as well as some laws of ritual purity.

rain.bio: Rain features prominently throughout the Jewish textual tradition. In the Torah, it is repeatedly described as a marker of God's blessing in the land of Israel. Jewish liturgy incorporates special prayers for rain, and rabbinic and philosophical sources are replete with discussions of the significance of rain and the laws and customs associated with asking for rain.

rebecca.bio: Rebecca is one of the four matriarchs of the Jewish people. Known for her acts of kindness and hospitality, she married Abraham's son Isaac. She favored her younger son Jacob and helped him to get the blessing meant for her older son Esau.

josiah.bio: Josiah was the sixteenth king of Judah and considered to be a righteous king, unlike many of his forefathers. He renovated the Temple and discovered a lost book of Torah, perhaps the book of Deuteronomy.

machloket.bio: A machloket is a dispute between two different interpretations of Torah law. Although these disagreements can be incredibly destructive, disputes like those between Hillel and Shammai are considered constructive and known as “disagreements for the sake of heaven.”

head2.bio:
The [first three](/topics/first-three1) [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) are the lights that came before the vessels, which are [enclothed](/topics/enclothe) in the [returning light](/topics/returning-light1) that ascends to them from the [partition](/topics/partition2) that is located from them and above. That is, the first three [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) – [*Keter*](/topics/keter1), [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1), and [*Bina*](/topics/bina3) – which are also known as the head of the [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1) (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 2, Q & A 13).
Those first three and the head are the ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) of [direct light](/topics/direct-light1), which are [enclothed](/topics/enclothe) in the [returning light](/topics/returning-light1) that ascends from the [partition](

nazir.bio: A Nazirite, or nazir in Hebrew, is an individual who vows abstinence from wine, haircuts, and ritual impurity generated from contact with corpses. Laws relating to Nazirites are described in [Numbers 6:1-21](https://www.sefaria.org/Numbers.6.1?ven=The_Koren_Jerusalem_Bible&lang=en&with=all&lang2=en) and are the focus of tractate Nazir in the [Mishnah](https://www.sefaria.org/Mishnah_Nazir?tab=contents) and [Talmud](https://www.sefaria.org/Nazir?tab=contents).

food.bio: Food is an essential element of human life and a significant subject in Jewish texts. In addition to narratives of eating and cooking in biblical stories, there are many commandments related to food and cooking. These include the laws of what Jews may and may not eat, known as [kashrut](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/kashrut?tab=admin), the offering of sacrifices, the prohibition against eating [chametz](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/chametz?tab=admin), the obligation to eat [matzah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/matzah?tab=admin) during Passover, and the separation of [terumot](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/terumah?tab=sources) and [ma'aserot](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/maaser?tab=sources), among others.

the-ten-commandments.bio: The "Ten Commandments" or "Ten Utterances" are the group of the commandments given to the Israelites at Mount Sinai in a constitutive moment of divine revelation to all of the children of Israel. The first set of five commandments establish the rules of God's relationship with humanity, and the second set of five are between people and other people. The Ten Commandments appear twice in the Torah: first, when they are given at Sinai, in [Exodus 20](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.20?lang=bi&aliyot=0) and again in [Deuteronomy 5](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.5.7?lang=bi&aliyot=0), in Moses' retelling of the earlier narrative.

thoughts.bio: The significance of a person's thoughts is discussed throughout Jewish sources in a range of contexts. The Torah, for example, instructs the individual not to harbor hatred in one's heart, rabbinic texts discuss under what conditions thoughts are rendered legally significant, and chasidic texts offer guidance on maintaining positive thinking.

sukkah.bio: A sukkah is a temporary dwelling — sometimes called a hut — covered by organic material, like tree branches or bamboo. A sukkah (plural: sukkot) serves as the means of fulfilling the biblical commandment to live in a sukkah for a week on the holiday of Sukkot. For the duration of the holiday, as long as it is not raining, those celebrating typically eat, socialize, and sleep in the sukkah rather than in their permanent homes.

aliases: Rabbi Shimon b. Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi
Rabbi Shimon ben Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi
rabbi-shimon-b-rabbi.bio: R. Shimon b. Rabbi was R. Yehudah haNasi's younger son. Though he was more learned than his older brother, their father decided that the position should go to the eldest, who excelled in his fear of God. R. Shimon was also known for his outstanding character and was made the Chacham of the academy.

rebbi-samuel.bio: R. Shmuel was a student of R. Abahu and of R. Zeira and a fourth generation talmudic sage of the Land of israel.

the-patriarchs.bio: Abraham, his son Isaac, and Isaac's son Jacob (also known as Israel) are the patriarchs (or forefathers) of the Jewish people. In [Genesis 12](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.12?lang=bi&aliyot=0), God makes a covenant with Abraham and promises him that he will be a great nation through whom the nations will be blessed and that his children will inherit the land of Canaan. Throughout the rabbinic tradition, the concept of the merit of the patriarchs, zekhut avot, is invoked, typically as an appeal to God's covenant with the forefathers for the forgiveness of their descendants, the Jewish people.

dan.bio: Dan was the fifth son of Jacob and the first with his concubine Bilhah. He was the founder of the Tribe of Dan.

trees.bio: As part of creation, humans are charged with the protection of trees. Those trees that produce fruit in the land of Israel are subject to the various Torah regulations regarding Temple gifts and tithes. Trees are also likened to the Torah itself and serve as a popular metaphor in Jewish tradition.

burial.bio: The concept that a body returns to the earth after death is rooted in one of the opening narratives of the Torah, as God tells Adam that he will [return to the dust](https://www.sefaria.org.il/Genesis.3.19?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he). Stories of people burying loved ones feature throughout the Torah--as when [Abraham buries his wife Sarah](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.23.19?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en)--and rabbinic sources extrapolate that there is an obligation to bury the dead from [Deuteronomy 21:23](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.21.23?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). Sources throughout the Jewish textual tradition discuss the importance of burial, its laws and customs, and its spiritual significance.

rabbi-zerika.bio: Rav Zerika cane from Babylonia, where he was a student of Rav Zerika; to Israel. where he studied under R. Abahu and other rabbis of the period in Israel. He was a colleague of Rabbi Yirmiayah.

parashat-lech-lecha.bio: Lech Lecha (“Go Forth”) recounts Abraham’s (here known as Abram) first encounter with God, his journey to Canaan, the birth of his son Ishmael, the covenant between him, his descendants, and God, and God’s commandment to circumcise the males of his household.

parashat-shoftim.bio: Shoftim (“Judges”) discusses guidelines of leadership, opening with a command to appoint judges and continuing to detail laws of kings, priests, and prophets. It also describes laws relating to cities of refuge for accidental killers, false witnesses, warfare, and the rite performed in a case of unsolved murder.

lot.bio: Lot was Abraham's nephew. As he grew wealthy, his household separated from Abraham's and he settled in Sodom. Angels rescued him and his family when Sodom was destroyed. His daughters thought they were the last living people on earth and had children with him, becoming the ancestors of the Moabites and the Ammonites.

shimon-b-zoma.bio: Much of [Shimon] Ben Zoma's career paralleled that of his namesake, Shimon ben Azzai. He too studied under R. Yehoshua before becoming one of R. Akiva's greatest students. He too was overwhelmed when "entering the orchard."

parashat-chayei-sarah.bio: Chayyei Sarah (“The Life of Sarah”) opens as Sarah dies and Abraham buys the Cave of Machpelah to bury her. Abraham sends his servant to find a wife for Isaac. The servant meets Rebecca at a well, and Rebecca returns with the servant to marry Isaac. Abraham remarries, has more children, and dies at age 175.

ecclesiastes.bio: [Ecclesiastes](https://www.sefaria.org/Ecclesiastes?tab=contents) (“Kohelet”) is one of the five megillot (scrolls), part of the section of the Hebrew Bible called Writings, and is often read publicly on the holiday of Sukkot. Attributed in the book’s opening verse to a son of King David (traditionally identified as [King Solomon](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/king-solomon?tab=sources)) the book explores the meaning of life, reckoning with death, futility, and purpose. The book often suggests that “all is vanity” and “nothing is new under the sun,” but also concludes by asserting that life’s purpose can be found in fear of God and adherence to God’s laws.

pirkei-avot.bio: This [tractate of the Mishnah](https://www.sefaria.org/Pirkei_Avot?tab=contents) contains ancient wisdom and ethics of the rabbinic sages. Traditionally, the work is studied between Passover and Shavuot. Perhaps because of the tractate’s place in the liturgical calendar, it has spawned many commentaries over the centuries.

truth.bio: The value of truth is discussed throughout the Jewish textual tradition, with roots in the biblical command to [distance oneself from lying.](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.23.7?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). Jewish texts also make room for bending the truth or lying in certain circumstances, like for the sake of [peace](https://www.sefaria.org/Yevamot.65b.7?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en).

high-holidays.bio: The High Holidays refer to the Jewish New Year (Rosh HaShanah) and the Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur) that follows 10 days later. These days are a time for repentance, introspection, prayer, and good deeds.

body2.bio: The term *guf*, body, essentially has three meanings in kabbalistic literature. The first is the vessel for the supernal light. Just as the physical body is a vessel for the soul, so too vessels of all kinds and on all [levels](/topics/level) of reality are called bodies (while the lights within them are called *neshamot*, souls). Second, “body” can refer to the central and important aspect that defines each [level](/topics/level). The third meaning of “body” is in the context of the structure of a [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1): the [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) of [*Ḥesed*](/topics/hesed), [*Gevura*](/topics/gevura), and [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1), or sometimes [*Ḥesed*](/topics/hesed), [*Gevura*](/topics/gevura), [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1), [*Netzaḥ*](/topics/netzah), [*Hod*](/topic

parashat-vayera.bio: Vayera (“He Appeared”) opens as guests inform Abraham that Sarah will give birth. Despite Abraham’s attempts to convince God otherwise, God destroys the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah. Sarah gives birth to Isaac, and Abraham sends away Hagar and Ishmael. At God’s command, Abraham prepares to sacrifice Isaac, but an angel of God tells Abraham to offer a ram instead.

tu-bishvat.bio: Tu Bishvat, or the 15th of Shevat, is the New Year (or birthday) for trees and planting. It is often celebrated by eating specific fruits.

rabbi-shimon-b-nannas.bio: An interlocutor of R. Akiva and R. Yishmael, (R. Shimon) Ben Nannas was known for his great expertise in monetary law.

shechem.bio: Shechem, a Canaanite town located north of Jerusalem, is first mentioned in Genesis when Abraham offered a sacrifice and built an altar there. However, it is perhaps most infamously known as the place where Jacob’s daughter, Dinah, was tragically raped.

parashat-balak.bio: Balak tells the story of the Moabite king Balak, who hires Balaam to curse the Israelites. Balaam’s donkey speaks along the way, and Balaam ends up blessing the people of Israel. The portion ends with a story about Israelite men sinning with Moabite women and the stabbing of an Israelite and a Midianite.

parashat-behaalotcha.bio: Beha’alotekha (“When You Raise”) opens with God instructing Moses to inaugurate the Levites for service in the Tabernacle. It also recounts the stories of people who request a second chance to offer the Passover sacrifice, the people of Israel's complaints and punishments, and a disease that affects Miriam.

parashat-naso.bio: Naso (“Take A Census”), the longest portion in the Torah, opens by detailing the responsibilities of the Levites. It also describes the laws of a woman suspected of adultery (*sotah*), the [Nazirite](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/nazir?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources), and the [priestly blessing](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/priestly-blessing?tab=admin). The portion ends by listing the gifts that heads of tribes bring to the [Tabernacle](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/mishkan?tab=admin).

nadav-and-avihu.bio: Nadav and Avihu were Aaron's two oldest sons. Soon after being invested as priests, they brought an offering of “strange fire that had not been commanded” before the Lord. As a result, they were consumed by fire and succeeded by their younger brothers, Elazar and Itamar. It is not clear from the biblical text exactly what Nadav and Avihu did wrong, which has led biblical interpreters to offer many different explanations for their punishment.

boaz.bio: Boaz was a wealthy man in Bethlehem who was generous with the poor, including Ruth who came to glean from his fields. He married Ruth and with her had a son from whom King David was descended.

cain.bio: Cain was the oldest child of Adam and Eve, the first human being born, and a farmer. After his own fruit and vegetable offerings to God were rejected, he killed his brother, the shepherd Abel, whose animal offerings had been accepted. He was then cursed to wander the earth, protected by God from being killed in revenge.

fusion-through-collision.bio: Fusion through collision refers to the clash between the supernal light, which shines continuously, and a [partition](/topics/partition2), which is the force of resistance of a created being, or [*vessel*](/topics/vessel1), to receiving the supernal light. When the supernal light is rebuffed by the created entity, the fusion through collision produces the [returning light](/topics/returning-light1), which initiates the process through which a new vessel or entity is created. In this context, “fusion” denotes the merging of two entities, which leads to the formation of a new entity, while “collision” means opposition and rejection. These concepts express the two opposing forces that act upon the [partition](/topics/partition2): [opacity](/topics/opacity) ([*ovyut*](/topics/ovyut)), which co

parashat-shemot.bio: Shemot (“Names”) is the first Torah reading in the book of Exodus. It opens describing the enslavement of the Israelites in Egypt. Moses is born, placed in a basket on the Nile, and adopted into Pharaoh’s household. He later encounters God at a burning bush and begins his mission of demanding that Pharaoh let the Israelites go.

parashat-ki-tisa.bio: Ki-Tisa (“When You Elevate”) opens as God tells Moses to collect a half-shekel donation from all Israelite adult males and to anoint the Mishkan (Tabernacle), its vessels, and the priests. The Israelites worship the golden calf, and Moses breaks the tablets. Moses beseeches God to forgive, returning with a second set of tablets.

matzah.bio: Matzah is the unleavened bread mentioned in the Torah that can be made from any of these five grains: wheat, barley, spelt, rye, or oats. In the Torah, God commands the people of Israel to eat matzah before leaving Egypt, a practice still observed and commemorated yearly, as Jews eat matzah at the Passover seder and throughout Passover.

tyre.bio: Tyre (Tzor) was a Phoenician port city located in present-day Lebanon. Built on an island, it was secure from invading armies, which helped it become prosperous. Although Tyre was allied with Israel under King Hiram, it was more frequently viewed as a rival to Israel.

conquest-and-division.bio: Initial actions in the Land of Israel.

the-ten-plagues.bio: In the context of the [Exodus story](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/the-exodus-from-egypt?tab=sources), in which God liberates the Israelites from slavery in Egypt, God wreaks a process of ten plagues on [Pharaoh](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/pharaoh?tab=sources) and the Egyptians as Pharaoh repeatedly refuses to let the Israelites go. The Ten Plagues, which appear in Exodus [chapters 7](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.7.14?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) through 12, are: water turned to blood, frogs, lice, wild animals, pestilence of livestock, boils, hail, locusts, darkness, and death of the firstborn. They are recounted and discussed as part of the Passover seder.

kavanah.bio: Kavanah is the word for the focused mindset recommended for prayer, i.e. of being before God and of what one is saying. While Jewish Law recognizes the legitimacy of prayer with minimal awareness, it requires knowledge of what one is saying in the first blessing of the Amidah.

demons.bio: While demons appear in a few episodes in the Hebrew Bible, they are more prominent in talmudic and kabbalistic literature. Jewish texts tell stories of demons, analyze their nature, discuss how to avoid them, and explain how fear of demons shapes some Jewish practices.

eliezer.bio: Eliezer was Abraham's steward and, according to Midrash, the servant he sent to find Isaac a wife. The servant prayed for a woman who would show him kindness and hospitality and before he finished praying, Rebecca was there to greet him.

memory.bio: Variations of the Hebrew word *zakhor* — translated as "remember" — appear multiple times throughout the Torah: The people of Israel are instructed to actively remember pivotal events in their history, like the [Exodus from Egypt](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/the-exodus-from-egypt?tab=admin), and God is described as remembering individuals [like Noah](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.8.1?ven=english|The_Koren_Jerusalem_Bible&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) and God's covenant with the patriarchs. Later sources in the Jewish textual tradition discuss the role of communal memory in Judaism, the value of committing Torah teachings to memory, and how to preserve historical memory through daily actions and prayers.

niddah.bio: In biblical terms, a *niddah* is a woman experiencing a menstrual flow who is thus required to abstain from intimacy with her husband and who renders impure people and items that are susceptible to ritual impurity. After the destruction of the Second Temple, the latter concern fell away, and the term came to refer to a category of Jewish ritual purity law that governs the intimate relations between spouses, also sometimes known as the laws of family purity.

sennacherib.bio: Sennacherib was an Assyrian king who sought to conquer Jerusalem. Isaiah prophesied his defeat. He laid siege to the city, but King Hezekiah withstood the siege. An angel defeated the Assyrian army and Sennacherib withdrew his forces.

shemoneh-esrei.bio: The Amidah — ”standing prayer” in English — is a central part of each of the three daily Jewish prayer services. It is also called *Shemoneh Esrei,* which means “18,” a reference to the fact that  the prayer originally consisted of 18 blessings, though the rabbis later added an additional one. The Amidah can be broken down into three parts: praise, national and personal requests, and thanksgiving.

parashat-emor.bio: Emor (“Say”) opens with laws regulating priestly behavior, working in the Tabernacle, and consuming sacrifices and priestly food. It describes the biblical holidays of Passover, Shavuot, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, and Sukkot, and ends with a story about a blasphemer and his punishment.

ephraim.bio: Ephraim was the second son of Joseph and Asenath. Despite being the younger son, he was given the primary blessing by Joseph's father, Jacob.

flood.bio: The biblical flood is described in the book of Genesis, chapters [6-9](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.6.9?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). In this account, God decides to destroy humanity with a flood. At God’s command, the righteous Noah builds an ark, where Noah, his family, and select animals survive the flood. Later sources analyze the story's themes, messages, and laws derived from it.

health.bio: Health is a value discussed throughout Jewish literature in a variety of contexts.

heavens.bio: The Heavens (Shamayim) appear in the first lines of Genesis and are mentioned frequently in the Jewish sources as representing everything above Earth. The Heavens are also understood as the place where God and the angels reside.

rabbi-yehudah-b-bava.bio: R. Yehudah b. Bava was an important scholar who lived at the same time as R. Akiva. He was so exceptional in his piety, that when the Talmud mentions a certain pious man, the reference is almost always to him. He defied the persecutory Roman laws by ordaining students in a hidden location in the mountains. Caught in this act, he was killed by the Romans.

rabbi-hanina-b-pappa.bio: R. Chanina b. Pappa was also a contemporary of R. Ami, and a student of R. Shmuel  b. Nachmani. He is known for the miraculous stories associated with him.

parashat-shelach.bio: Shelach (“Send”) tells the story of Israelite spies journeying into Canaan, the negative report they bring back, and the resulting punishment decreed upon the Israelites: to wander and die in the desert over forty years. It ends with laws about sacrifices, the story of a man who desecrates Shabbat, and the commandment to wear ritual fringes.

yisrael-sabba-and-tevuna1.bio: The [seven lower ones](/topics/seven-lower-ones) of [*Abba* and *Imma*](/topics/abba-and-imma), that is, their ear nose and mouth, are called *Yisrael* *Sabba* and *Tevuna*. Initially, at the time of the [fusion](/topics/fusion-through-collision) of the [gaze](/topics/gaze1) of the [eyes](/topics/eyes4) of [*Abba* and *Imma*](/topics/abba-and-imma) towards one another, *Yisrael* *Sabba* and *Tevuna* and [*Abba* and *Imma*](/topics/abba-and-imma) are united in a single [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1). Subsequently, however, when [*Abba* and *Imma*](/topics/abba-and-imma) return to a back-to-back [fusion](/topics/fusion-through-collision), *Yisrael* *Sabba* and *Tevuna* separate from them and emerge as a special [head](/topics/head2) at the level of the ear nose and mouth, becoming a distinct

returning-light.bio: The returning light is formed when the [partition](/topics/partition2) of a particular [level](/topics/level) of existence rebuffs the supernal light, returning it to its source in the manner of a light refracting off a mirror. This returning light is generated through the resistance of the receiving entity to receiving the supernal light. The returning light does not disappear but rather [enclothes](/topics/enclothe) the supernal light, which is then manifest as a new vessel for the supernal light. All the [levels](/topics/level) and [*partzufim*](/topics/partzuf) of creation are formed through [enclothing](/topics/enclothe) supernal, or “direct light” in the receiving entity’s returning light.

the-tablets.bio: The tablets (luchot) are the two tablets upon which God wrote the [Ten Commandments](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/the-ten-commandments?tab=sources) and which were taken down by [Moses](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/moses?tab=sources) to be placed in the [ark of the covenant](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/aron-habrit?sort=Relevance&tab=sources). Moses broke the first set when he saw the people worshipping the [golden calf](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/golden-calf?tab=sources), but they were replaced by a second set.

gratitude.bio: Gratitude is the theme of the first prayer uttered upon waking up in the morning, [Modeh Ani](https://www.sefaria.org/Siddur_Edot_HaMizrach%2C_Preparatory_Prayers%2C_Modeh_Ani.4?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) (“I give thanks”) and drives many other Jewish practices and customs: the recitation of [Hallel](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/hallel?tab=admin) on Jewish holidays is an expression of thanks for God’s miracles, many understand the obligation of [honoring one’s parents](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/honoring-parents?tab=admin) to be an embodiment of gratitude, and countless Jewish blessings, prayers, and psalms give voice to feelings of thankfulness.

forgiveness.bio: Forgiveness is a value discussed throughout the Jewish textual tradition. It is particularly associated with the holidays of [Rosh Hashanah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/rosh-hashanah?sort=Relevance&tab=sources) and [Yom Kippur](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/yom-kippur?tab=sources), and the days that lead up to them.

sodom.bio: In the Bible, the city of Sodom became synonymous with evil in a story about Abraham’s nephew, Lot, his guests, and the mob of Sodomites that rose against them. Rabbinic sources elaborate on Sodom’s many evils, including using the death penalty to punish hospitality to strangers.

mussar-(מוסר-).bio: Mussar refers to the spiritual, educational, and cultural movement that arose in 19th-century Lithuania. Instead of taking a traditional, rules-based approach to Judaism, Mussar uses virtue-based ethics to offer guidance for living a meaningful life.

rashi.bio: Rabbi Shlomo ben Yitzchak, best known by the acronym "Rashi," was an influential medieval Torah and Talmud commentator. Born in Troyes, France, he studied in the yeshivot of Worms and Mainz. At the age of 25, he returned to Troyes, where he later opened his own yeshiva. Rashi's commentaries on the Bible and Talmud are considered indispensable tools for Torah study. His biblical commentary incorporates literal explanations of the text alongside midrashic and rabbinic interpretations.

tribes.bio: The sons of Jacob, founders of the tribes.

parashat-vayeshev.bio: Vayeshev (“He Settled”) begins the story of Joseph, describing his rivalry with his brothers, slavery in Egypt, and imprisonment after his master’s wife frames him in response to Joseph’s refusal of her advances. It also contains the story of Tamar, her husbands, and her father-in-law, Judah.

bikkurim.bio:
Bikkurim (firstfruits) refers to the commandment, detailed in Deuteronomy 26:1–11, to bring firstfruits and grains to the Temple and give them to a priest next to the altar.
Details of the process are discussed in tractate Bikkurim, which describes the festive procession that accompanied the offering. This ritual is often associated with the holiday of Shavuot, since the holiday marked the beginning of the period during which bikkurim were brought.

garden-of-eden.bio: The Garden of Eden features in the opening chapters of the book of [Genesis](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis?tab=contents). God plants the garden, causes trees to grow there, and places Adam in the garden to work and preserve it. After [Eve and Adam](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/adam-and-eve?tab=sources) eat forbidden fruit, God drives them out. The Garden of Eden has been the subject of analysis and discussion throughout thousands of years of the Jewish textual tradition.

parashat-beshalach.bio: Beshalach (“When He Let Go”) describes the splitting of the Red Sea and the song the Israelites sing upon crossing through. In the desert, God sweetens bitter water and provides manna and quail. The portion ends recounting the victory of the Israelites against an attack by the Amalekites.

old-age.bio: Biblical and rabbinic texts often talk of living into old age as a [blessing](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.15.15?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he), a [reward](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.6.2?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) for fulfilling the commandments, and a stage of life marked by wisdom. The Torah instructs people to show [deference to the old](https://www.sefaria.org/Leviticus.19.32?lang=he&aliyot=0), a commandment many see as a central Jewish value. Some texts also discuss [difficult](https://www.sefaria.org.il/Shabbat.151b.11?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) experiences that can accompany old age, like a [sense of futility](https://www.sefaria.org/II_Samuel.19.36?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he), frustration at not being able to enjoy life’s pleasures, and [f

feminine-waters2.bio: It is known that as a result of the expansion of the [points](/topics/points) of [*Sag*](/topics/sag) to [*Mah*](/topics/mah1) and [*Ban*](/topics/ban) of the inner [*Adam Kadmon*](/topics/adam-kadmon2), [traces](/topics/traces) of two levels are joined in the [partition](/topics/partition2). These are the first *heh* in [*Sag*](/topics/sag), and the bottom *heh* in the inner [*Adam Kadmon*](/topics/adam-kadmon2). Thus, the [partition](/topics/partition2) is incorporated from two females, [*Bina*](/topics/bina3) and [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1). For this reason the [partition](/topics/partition2) was given the name feminine waters, since from this point onwards it incorporates these two females in every [fusion](/topics/fusion-through-collision) it performs with the supernal light (*Talmu

rav-hanan-b-rabbah.bio: Rav Hanan b. Rava was the son-in-law of Rav and the father-in-law of Rav Chisda.

hesed.bio: The [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of *Ḥesed* is the fourth of the ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot). When the [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) are divided into the five primary [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) – [*Keter*](/topics/keter1), [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1), [*Bina*](/topics/bina3), [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1), and [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) – *Ḥesed* is encompassed in the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1) as the [*Keter*](/topics/keter1) [level](/topics/level) of [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1) (see [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1)). The term *sefirat* *Ḥesed* is also a reference to different [levels](/topics/level) and *sefirot* in lower structures of reality that are all derivatives of the root *sefira* of *Ḥesed* found in the [*direct light*](/topics/direct-lig

parashat-tazria.bio: Tazria (“She Bears Seed”) opens by describing the purification process for a woman after childbirth. It then describes different forms of *tzara’at*, a discoloration condition on skin or clothing, and the requirement of an infected person to dwell alone outside the camp and be inspected by a priest.

rabbi-assi.bio: R. Assi is one of the names mentioned most frequently in the Babylonian and Jerusalem Talmud. R. Assi studied under Shmuel in Babylonia before coming to study together with R. Ami in the academy of R. Yochanan in Tiberias. The Babylonian Talmud reports that when he died, all trees were uprootsd (Moed Katan 25b), while the Jerusalem Talmud records that on that day a fountain of Tiberias fell (Avodah Zarah 3:1).

honoring-parents.bio: The commandment to honor one’s parents is the fifth of the Ten Commandments [(Exodus 20:12)](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.20.12?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). Rabbinic sources describe the commandment as on par with revering God: just as we acknowledge the gifts that God gives us, we must also show gratitude for the gift of life that our parents gave us.

zechariah.bio: The prophet Zechariah, active during the post-exilic period, emphasized themes of repentance, divine mercy, and visions symbolizing the restoration and future glory of Jerusalem and the Jewish people.

shem.bio: Shem was one of the sons of Noah who was saved during the flood and became the ancestor of several nations, including the Hebrews.

friendship.bio: The Hebrew Bible includes several stories of friends showing respect and compassion toward one another, like those of David and Jonathan and Ruth and Naomi. It also uses the terms “friend” or “fellow” in the context of commandments such as loving one’s fellow as themselves. Later Jewish texts analyze different types of friendships and how to strive for the ideal friendship, discuss the obligations of friends toward one another, and extol the spiritual benefits of proper friendships.

parashat-yitro.bio: Yitro (“Jethro”) begins as Moses reunites with his father-in-law Yitro and accepts his advice to appoint judges who will help govern the Israelites. The Israelites prepare to encounter God at Mount Sinai. God descends amidst fire, smoke, thunder, and the blast of a shofar and gives the Ten Commandments.

clothing.bio: Clothing features from the opening chapters of the Torah: Adam and Eve are created without clothing and don't feel embarrassed, but after they eat from the tree of knowledge, they make clothing for themselves out of [fig leaves](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.3.7?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). Clothing features in other biblical narratives and laws: Jacob makes a special coat for his favored son, Joseph; the book of Exodus describes the unique clothing to be worn by priests and the high priest in the Tabernacle; and various biblical figures tear their clothes in mourning, among other instances. Later Jewish sources discuss the significance of clothing and analyze how clothing intersects with themes of holiness, protection, purity, and identity.

work.bio: In the Tanakh (Hebrew Bible), work can be seen as part of the human experience from the outset: When God creates man, God puts him in the garden of Eden to ["work it and preserve it."](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.2.15?lang=bi&with=Translations&lang2=en) When Adam and Eve later sin by eating from the tree of knowledge, Adam is cursed with a future of arduous labor: ["By the sweat of your brow you shall eat bread"](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.3.19?lang=bi&with=Translations&lang2=en). In describing the commandment of Shabbat, the Torah contrasts Shabbat with weekdays, which are described as [days of work](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.35.2?ven=hebrew|Tanach_with_Ta%27amei_Hamikra&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en_). Rabbinic and later texts delve into the question

parashat-vayechi.bio: Vayechi (“He Lived”) is the final Torah portion in the Book of Genesis. It opens as Jacob prepares for his death, making his son Joseph swear to bury him in the Cave of Machpelah. Jacob blesses Joseph’s two sons and his own 12 sons and then dies. Jacob’s sons bury him. The portion ends with Joseph’s death.

yetzira1.bio: The world of *Yetzira* is the third of the four worlds (after the supernal world of *Adam Kadmon*) that emanate and descend from one another in the process of creation. Some maintain that its name derives from the Hebrew words for form, *tzura*, or image, *tziyur*, since it is a kind of image or pattern for the final and fourth world, [*Asiya*](/topics/asiya2). Each of the worlds corresponds to one of the five primary [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot). The world of *Yetzira* corresponds to the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1) or the third [level](/topics/level) of creation. The created beings that dwell in this [level](/topics/level) are the angels called *ḥayot*.

rabbi-yehudah-b-betera-(i).bio: R. Yehudah b. Betera was a scholar and the head of an academy in Netzivin in northern Babylonia.

taharah.bio: Taharah is ritual purity and contrasts to tumah, ritual impurity. While the Temples stood, one needed to be in a state of ritual purity to enter the Temple and offer a sacrifice. To attain such a status, depending on the nature of the impurity contracted, the purification process may have entailed a waiting period, sprinkling with the ashes of a [red heifer](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/parah-adumah?sort=Relevance&tab=sources), and immersion in a [mikveh](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/mikveh?sort=Relevance&tab=sources). Purity and impurity are the focus of [Seder Toharot](https://www.sefaria.org/texts/Mishnah). While most of these laws are no longer observed in the absence of the Temple, some still are.

curses.bio: Curses are discussed in a variety of contexts throughout the Jewish textual tradition: biblical passages describe [attempts](https://www.sefaria.org/Numbers.23.7?lang=en&with=all&lang2=en) of [individuals](https://www.sefaria.org/Leviticus.24.11?ven=The_Koren_Jerusalem_Bible&lang=en&with=all&lang2=en) to curse others, [God's curses](https://www.sefaria.org/Leviticus.26.14?lang=en&with=all&lang2=en) for those who don't follow the commandments, and [several](https://www.sefaria.org/Leviticus.19.14?ven=The_Contemporary_Torah,_Jewish_Publication_Society,_2006&lang=en&with=all&lang2=en) [injunctions](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.22.27?ven=The_Contemporary_Torah,_Jewish_Publication_Society,_2006&lang=en&with=all&lang2=en) against cursing spec

netzah.bio: The [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of *Netzaḥ* is the seventh of the ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot). When the [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) are divided into the five primary [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) – *Keter*, *Ḥokhma*, *Bina*, *Tiferet*, and *Malkhut* – *Netzaḥ* is encompassed in the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1) as the [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1) [level](/topics/level) for the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1) (see [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1)).  *Netzaḥ* is the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) that completes a structure called the right-hand column in every [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1) or world. It is the fourth (thus, the [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1) [level](/topics/level)) [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) [level](/topics/level) wi

fire.bio: Rabbinic texts describe how the first humans discovered fire, and fire plays a role in many biblical stories, like that of the [burning bush](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/burning-bush?tab=sources). Fire features both physically and metaphorically in Jewish sources; fire serves, for example, as a metaphor for Torah in some rabbinic texts.

gevura.bio: The [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of *Gevura* is the fifth of the ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot). When the [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) are divided into the five primary [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) – [*Keter*](/topics/keter1), [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1), [*Bina*](/topics/bina3), [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1), and [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) – *Gevura* is encompassed in the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1) as the [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1) [level](/topics/level) of [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1) (see [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1))  The [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of *Gevura* is called by this name since it characterizes restraint and constriction. It represents the left column in a process called the rectification of lines, and it is this left column on which

parashat-vayetzei.bio: Vayeitzei (“He Went Out”) opens as Jacob dreams about angels going up and down a ladder, and then continues on his journey toward the home of his uncle Laban. During years of indentured servitude, Jacob marries Laban’s daughters Leah and Rachel, bearing children with them and with their maidservants, Bilhah and Zilpah.

parashat-pinchas.bio: Pinchas opens with God’s promise of a “covenant of peace” for the zealot Pinchas, followed by a census. The daughters of Tzelofchad request and receive new laws regarding inheritance. God instructs Moses to prepare Joshua for leadership, and God describes sacrifices brought daily and on special occasions.

birds.bio: Birds feature in the Jewish textual tradition from the opening chapter of the Torah, which [recounts](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.1.21?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) how birds were created on the fifth day of creation. [Noah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/noah?tab=admin) uses birds to help determine whether it is safe to leave the ark, and other parts of the Torah discuss laws surrounding birds, like which ones are prohibited for consumption and the obligation to send away a mother bird before taking her young. Later Jewish texts expand on these concepts and other aspects of the nature and qualities of birds.

parashat-bamidbar.bio: Bamidbar (“In The Desert”) is the first Torah portion in the book of Numbers. It describes God's command to take a census and details the camping formation of the people of Israel in the desert. It also begins to enumerate the responsibilities of the Levites when transporting the Tabernacle.

parashat-mishpatim.bio: Mishpatim (“Laws”) recounts a series of God’s laws that Moses gives to the Israelites. These include laws about treatment of slaves, damages, loans, returning lost property, the Sabbath, the sabbatical year, holidays, and destroying idolatry. The portion ends as Moses ascends Mount Sinai for 40 days.

hod1.bio: The [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of *Hod* is the eighth of the ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot). When the [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) are divided into the five primary [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) – [*Keter*](/topics/keter1), [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1), [*Bina*](/topics/bina3), [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1), and [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) – *Hod* is encompassed in the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1) as the [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) [level](/topics/level) for the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1).  *Hod* is the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) which completes the left line or column in every [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1) or world. Its essence is that of the fourth [level](/topics/level) ([*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1)). Because of th

parashat-bo.bio: Bo (“Come”) recounts the last three plagues that God inflicts on the Egyptians: locusts, darkness, and death of firstborns. God commands the Israelites to offer a Passover lamb sacrifice. After the last plague, Pharaoh and the Egyptians demand that the Israelites leave.

parashat-reeh.bio: Re’eh (“See”) details a series of laws in advance of the Israelites’ entrance into the Land of Israel. These include the obligation to destroy idolatry, laws of tithing and charity, dietary laws, holiday laws, and the prohibition of offering sacrifices outside of the place designated by God.

binding-of-isaac.bio: The Binding of Isaac is a story found in Genesis 22, in which God commands Abraham to sacrifice his son, Isaac. Abraham journeys with Isaac to the designated mountain and prepares. As Abraham grabs the knife, an angel of God calls out to him and instructs him not to sacrifice his son. Instead, Abraham sees a ram and sacrifices that instead. The story has inspired generations of analysis and commentary and is traditionally read on Rosh Hashanah, the Jewish new year.

parashat-vaetchanan.bio: Vaetchanan (“I Pleaded”) opens as Moses describes his pleading with God to be allowed into the Land of Israel. Moses warns the Israelites not to pursue idolatry and recounts the giving of the Ten Commandments. The portion also contains the Shema, a declaration of faith and a central text in Jewish prayer.

parashat-korach.bio: Parashat Korach recounts the rebellion of Korach (a cousin of Moses and Aaron), Dathan, Abiram, and 250 of their followers. Some rebels are swallowed by the ground, while others are consumed by a fire from God and others die in a plague. The portion ends by describing gifts given to priests and Levites.

high-priest.bio: The High Priest was the head priest of the Israelites, beginning with the biblical [Aaron](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/aaron?sort=Relevance&tab=sources) and continuing through the Second Temple period. The High Priest had a special role in the [Mishkan](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/mishkan?tab=sources) and Temple service, and wore unique garments detailed in the [book of Exodus](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.28.3?ven=The_Koren_Jerusalem_Bible&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en).

laban.bio: Laban is a biblical character who features in the Book of Genesis. He is the brother of Rebecca, father of Rachel and Leah, and father-in-law of Jacob. His actions are marked by trickery, as he deceptively marries Leah off to Jacob rather than Rachel, and later mistreats Jacob as an employee.

rabbi-chanina-b-dosa.bio: R. Chanina b. Dosa lived before and after the Temple's destruction. He was known for his piety, his faith and his ability to petition God. In this regard, the greatest scholars would turn to him to pray for them. He lived on next to nothing and experienced many miracles on behalf of himself and his family.

parashat-vayakhel.bio: Vayakhel (“He Assembled”) opens as God commands the Israelites to observe the Sabbath. Moses asks for material donations for the building of the Mishkan (Tabernacle), and the people donate. A group of artisans designated by God begin building the Mishkan and its vessels.

parashat-eikev.bio: In Eikev (“As a Result”), Moses recounts events that happened in the desert, including the manna, the golden calf, and Aaron's death. Moses describes the blessings God will bestow upon the Israelites if they follow God’s law and the punishments they will encounter if they disobey.

parashat-vayishlach.bio: Vayishlach (“He Sent”) follows Jacob and his family as Jacob wrestles with a man (commonly understood as an angel), is renamed Israel, and reconciles with his brother, Esau. Jacob’s daughter, Dina, is raped by a Hivite prince, and her brothers sack a city in response. Rachel dies as she gives birth to Jacob's youngest child, Benjamin.

parashat-noach.bio: Noach (“Noah”) begins as God decides to destroy mankind with a flood. At God’s command, the righteous Noah builds an ark, where Noah, his family, and select animals survive the flood. Noah’s children bear children, and several generations develop. God confounds the speech of people building the Tower of Babel.

silence.bio: While Judaism is often thought of as a sound-filled religion — God uses speech to utter the world into existence, and synagogues and study halls are often places of audible prayer and learning— silence has its place in the Jewish textual tradition. The high priest Aaron [reacts](https://www.sefaria.org/Leviticus.10.3?ven=hebrew|Tanach_with_Ta%27amei_Hamikra&lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) to the death of his two sons with silence, and Pirkei Avot praises silence as a "[fence to wisdom](https://www.sefaria.org/Pirkei_Avot.3.13?lang=en&with=all&lang2=en)." Sources throughout the centuries discuss the place and value of silence.

parashat-ki-teitzei.bio: Ki Teitzei (“When You Go Out”) contains numerous laws, more than appear in any other Torah portion. These include laws about the treatment of a captive woman, returning lost objects, forbidden mixtures, the erection of a rooftop fence, rape, collateral, and workers’ wages.

parah-adumah.bio: The red heifer — *parah adumah* in Hebrew — is an unblemished and completely red cow that has never been put to work. The animal’s ashes are used to purify someone who has come into contact with a corpse and thereby contracted [ritual impurity](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/tumah?tab=admin).

doeg.bio: Doeg was an Edomite who served King Saul. He carried out the executions of a number of priests who helped David when he was fleeing Saul.

knowing-god.bio: The idea that one can know God is a delicate proposition. Jewish tradition regards God as beyond comprehension, yet biblical [texts](https://www.sefaria.org/Isaiah.55.6?ven=hebrew|Tanach_with_Ta%27amei_Hamikra&lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) encourage seeking God. Various Jewish texts describe ways to gain a better understanding of God and enhance knowledge of God, such as through meditation, asking philosophical questions, Torah study, or observation of the natural world.

parashat-toldot.bio: Toldot (“Generations”) opens with the births of Isaac and Rebecca’s twins, Jacob and Esau. Esau sells his birthright to Jacob in exchange for soup.
