He was the artisan charged with building the [mishkan](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/mishkan?tab=sources) (Tabernacle), its vessels, and the [priestly garments](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/priestly-garments?tab=sources), assisted by Oholiav and other workers. The Torah describes how God filled Betzalel with ["with the spirit of God, in wisdom, and in understanding, and in knowledge, and in all manner of workmanship."](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.31.3?ven=The_Koren_Jerusalem_Bible&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en)

rabbi-yohanan-hasandlar.bio: R. Yohanan Hasandlar was born in Egypt, but moved to the Land of Israel where he became a devoted student of R. Akiva. Even when the latter was in a Roman jail, R. Yochanan found a way to come to him for instruction.

aliases: You Shall Love Your Neighbor as Yourself
Love Your Neighbor as Yourself [V'Ahavta Lereiacha Kamocha]
love-your-neighbor-as-yourself.bio: “Love your neighbor as yourself” (Leviticus 19:18) is the basis for a central principle known as the Golden Rule, or the concept of treating others as one wants to be treated themselves. Much commentary has been penned attempting to understand the verse’s moral message, scope, practical implications, and role in Judaism.

alcohol.bio: Consumption of alcoholic beverages and drunkenness are discussed from the first book of the Torah, as Noah plants a vineyard upon leaving his ark and deals with the consequences of his subsequent drunkenness. Other biblical texts convey mixed messages about alcohol: while the book of Psalms extols wine as "[gladdening the human heart](https://www.sefaria.org/Psalms.104.15?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en)," the book of Proverbs warns that "[in the end, [wine] bites like a snake.](https://www.sefaria.org/Proverbs.23.32?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en)" Later stories, legal discussions, and philosophical discourses throughout the Jewish textual tradition take up the topic of alcohol consumption, its role and contributions to Jewish ritual, and its limits and hazards.

milk.bio: Milk features throughout Jewish texts as a [marker of blessing](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.26.9?lang=bi&with=Ramban&lang2=en), a legal category, and a metaphor for spiritual sustenance. The biblical prohibition against boiling a goat in its mother's milk becomes the basis for the extensive system of separating meat and dairy in halakhah. Milk also appears in symbolic and poetic contexts, such as in [Song of Songs 4:11](https://www.sefaria.org/Song_of_Songs.4.11?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en), where it represents sweetness and intimacy, and in rabbinic literature [comparing](https://www.sefaria.org/Shir_HaShirim_Rabbah.4.5.1?lang=bi&with=Navigation&lang2=en) Torah to nourishing milk. Many Jews have a [custom](https://www.sefaria.org/Shulchan_Arukh%2C_Orach_Chayi

the-four-children.bio: “The Four Children” refers to a rabbinic text incorporated into the *maggid* section of many [haggadahs](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/haggadah?tab=sources). The text presents four types of children who ask different kinds of questions, from different perspectives, at the Passover [seder](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/seder?tab=sources). Each child is given a unique answer, built by weaving together different biblical verses. Variations of the text abound.

surrounding-light.bio: Although the supernal light wishes to permeate every [level](/topics/level) of reality, reality cannot fully contain it on account of its limited vessels, or receptacles. Therefore the supernal light that cannot be contained, called surrounding light, is always situated in the background and so has an indirect influence on reality. This contrasts with the [inner light](/topics/inner-light1), which is the supernal light that is currently [enclothed](/topics/enclothe) in reality and permeates it in accordance with the reality’s capacity to contain it. When reality is fully rectified in the future, the surrounding light too will be able to permeate it.

candles.bio: Candles (nerot, in Hebrew) — or any type of clean-burning oil lamps — are an important feature in the celebration of Shabbat and Jewish holidays. The main function of candles is to provide light in the home at night in order to enhance the festive atmosphere. Candles are also key to Temple and Chanukkah rituals.

showbread.bio: The Showbread (Lechem Hapanim) are the loaves that are placed on a special table in the Temple each week. Every Shabbat, the priests (kohanim) eat the Showbread, and fresh loaves are then placed on the table.

rabbi-mattia-b-heresh.bio: R. Matya fled the Land of Israel due to the persecutions there after the Bar Kokhba revolt. He came to Rome where he established an academy. There he became wealthy and famous, but was nevertheless known for his great piety.

mother.bio: Mothers feature throughout Jewish contexts in a variety of contexts. The Bible, for example, tells of the matriarchs in the book of Genesis and other maternal figures. Rabbinic sources discuss potential roles mothers can fulfill and the obligations of children toward their mothers.

havaya2.bio: The *yod* of *Havaya* is the first expansion of the [light](/topics/light2), which is called the first level (see v. 1, Answers, answer 69). The first *heh* of *Havaya* is the first prevailment in the [light](/topics/light2), called the second level. The *vav* of *Havaya* is the second expansion of the [light](/topics/light2), which is called the third level. Finally, the last *heh* of *Havaya* is the second prevailment in the [light](/topics/light2), called the fourth level (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 1, Q & A 70).

aliases: Onkelos b. Kalonikos b. Titus's sister
onkelos.bio: Onkelos, according to tradition, was a Roman convert to Judaism who lived in the first or second century. He translated Tanach into Aramaic, the spoken language of the day. Some identify Onkelos with a convert named Aquilas who translated the Tanach into Greek. According to tradition, he was counseled by his uncle, the emperor, to succeed by buying low and selling high. He later explained his conversion to his incensed uncle by repeating to him his own advice from years past. Onkelos' translation became the standard Aramaic translation, used daily by Jews world-wide long after Aramaic ceased being a living language for most Jews.

kelippot.bio: *Kelippot* are [levels](/topics/level) of reality that do not contain any will to give, only the will to receive. Since their essence is one of receiving or “taking,” they are constantly trying to pull all of reality into themselves – in essence, to swallow everything in existence. Since the essence of life is the divine light, which is composed entirely of the energy of giving, they and all that is connected to them are considered to be “dead.” The *kelippot* are therefore compared to the husks or shells of a fruit, which are detached from the life that is inside the fruit. Since these entities are wholly receiving, unable to give, they have no true connection with the life force of creation, like the dead.

compassion.bio: In the Torah, God commands the Jewish people to act compassionately toward the stranger. Jewish texts of many genres elaborate on what this means, how to emulate God’s compassion, and how to navigate situations where compassion conflicts with other values.

olives.bio: In the Bible, there are many uses for olives (zeitim) and their oil, from eating to anointing. The importance of olive oil’s role in the Temple in Jerusalem gave olives even more distinction, making the olive tree one of the most vital plants in ancient Israel.

tekhelet.bio: *Tekhelet* is a special blue or blue-ish dye that the Torah designates for use in ritual fringes, tapestries in the Tabernacle, and clothing of the high priest. Rabbinic literature explains that *tekhelet* is derived from a sea creature called the *chilazon* and delves into the spiritual significance of the dye. At some point in history, *tekhelet* disappeared from ritual practice, likely because the traditions of how to identify the *chilazon* or how to produce *tekhelet* were lost — yet acknowledgement of its spiritual relevance endured, such as the recitation of the commandment to wear *tekhelet* as part of the daily Shema prayer. Since the 19th century, there has been [renewed](https://www.sefaria.org/Treasures_Hidden_in_the_Sand?tab=contents) [discussion](https://www.sefaria.org/Ptil_

mamzer.bio: A mamzer is a child born from incest or a woman's adultery and is forbidden by Jewish law from marrying anyone except another mamzer, a convert, or a slave.

mincha-(קרבן).bio: The Mincha Offering (Korban Mincha) was the daily afternoon sacrifice of fine flour with olive oil and frankincense that was offered upon the Copper Altar in the Mishkan and in the Temple.

the-moon.bio: In the opening chapter of the Torah, God creates the moon, alongside other celestial bodies, on the fourth day of creation. In Jewish tradition and practice, the moon plays a central role in determining the calendar: New Hebrew months start with the appearance of new moons, which also lead to the determination of holiday dates. Texts throughout the ages discuss the moon's symbolism and significance; halakhic practices related to the moon, like the concept of blessing the new moon; and ways in which the lunar calendar interacts with the solar calendar.

rav-yosef-b-chiyya.bio: A contemporary of Rabbah, Rav Yosef became the head of the academy in Pumpedita after Rabbah's death. He was known for his humility and for his mastery of the corpus of Jewish law. While wealthy, Rav Yosef had health issues which brought him blindness and the loss of memory.

hasmoneans.bio: The Hasmonean dynasty was a ruling dynasty in ancient Judea established by the Maccabees, fighters from a priestly Jewish family who successfully waged war against the Seleucid Greeks and rededicated the Second Temple in Jerusalem in 164 BCE. Hasmonean leaders occupied the position of high priest and eventually the kingship as well. The dynasty ruled from 167 BCE to 37 BCE.

zion.bio: Zion — in Hebrew, *Tzion* — is another name for Jerusalem in the Bible. It can also refer to the land of Israel. The term often highlights a spiritual connection between heaven and earth or a yearning for messianic redemption.

yehudah-b-tabbai.bio: The third of the pairs, Yehudah b. Tabbai and Shimon b. Shetach served during the tumultuous reign of Alexander Yannai. Though Yannai's brother-in-law, Shimon had to hide at the height of Yannai's persecution of the Pharisees; while Yehudah fled to Egypt. Nevertheless, Shimon wrested control of the Sanhedrin away from the Sadducees.

naaman.bio: Na'aman was the commander of the army of Aram.

rabbi-yitzhak-b-elazar.bio: R. Yitzchak b. Elazar was a much beloved teacher in Caesarea who taught in its old synagogue.

adar.bio: Adar is the sixth month of the Hebrew calendar. In seven out of every 19 years, a second month of Adar is added in order to keep the agricultural cycle of the Jewish lunar calendar synchronized with the solar year. [The rabbis taught](https://www.sefaria.org/Taanit.29a.18?vhe=William_Davidson_Edition_-_Vocalized_Aramaic&lang=bi) that one rejoices when the month of Adar begins.

ammon.bio: Descended from Lot, Ammon was a nation that dwelled east of Israel in present-day Jordan. As with Moab — and although they were frequently in conflict — the Israelites were specifically instructed by the Torah to neither attack nor absorb the Ammonites.

menorah.bio: The candelabra used to fulfill the mitzvah of lighting Chanukkah candles is called a *chanukkiah.* Sometimes also called a menorah, it is lit in homes and synagogues during Chanukkah to commemorate the miracle of the holiday, in which one day's worth of oil for the Temple [menorah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/menorah1?tab=admin) burned for eight days. A chanukkiah has a spot for a candle for each of the eight nights and an additional one for the *shamash,* the candle used to light the others.

lag-baomer.bio: Lag BaOmer ("the 33rd day of the Omer") is a day of celebration during the otherwise solemn period of the 49 days between the holidays of Passover and Shavuot. Many mark this day with outdoor parties and bonfires.

leviathan.bio: The Leviathan is a giant sea creature that features in several places throughout the Jewish textual tradition. In the Bible, it is mentioned a total of six times: in the books of Isaiah, Psalms, and Job. Rabbinic literature describes how God created the Leviathan [during the creation of the world](https://www.sefaria.org/Bava_Batra.74b.5?lang=en&with=all&lang2=en) and how, in a future world to come, the [righteous will feast from the flesh of the Leviathan](https://www.sefaria.org/Bava_Batra.75a.4?lang=en&with=all&lang2=en).

fertility.bio: In Jewish texts and thought, it is God who grants fertility — whether agricultural bounty or children — through blessings and [rain](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/rain?tab=sources). Jewish texts and traditions include narratives of families struggling to conceive children, God opening wombs, prayers for rain, and more.

yannai.bio: King Yannai, or Alexander Jannaeus, was a Hasmonean king who expanded the kingdom of Judea but left a legacy of war and terrible treatment of Jewish scholars. The exception to this vicious treatment was his brother-in-law Rabbi Shimon ben Shetach, from whom he received counsel.

matot-masei.bio: [Matot](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/parashat-matot?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources)-[Masei](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/parashat-masei?tab=admin) (“Tribes”-”Travels”), a double Torah portion and the final one in the book of Numbers, describes the laws of vows, a war against the Midianites, and the request of some tribes to dwell outside the Land of Israel. It also reviews the Israelites’ travels in the desert and outlines Israel’s boundaries.

tamar.bio: Tamar was Judah's daughter-in-law. After she married two of his sons who then died, he refused to do his duty by giving her his third son in marriage. Instead, she seduced him by pretending to be a prostitute. She kept his personal effects as a pledge and used them to prove that he owed her his household and protection. The son they had became an ancestor of King David.

philosophy.bio: Philosophy refers to the study of questions such as faith, reason, knowledge, and existence. Jewish philosophy encompasses generations of books devoted to such topics, as well as parts of biblical and rabbinic literature that incorporate discussions of these topics among other focuses.

rabbi-bannaah.bio: Rabbi Banna'ah was a teacher highly appreciative of the Mishnah. Among his students was R. Yochanan.

parashat-terumah.bio: Terumah (“Donation”) opens as God tells Moses to collect donated materials in order to build a dwelling place for God called the Mishkan (Tabernacle). God describes how to build the vessels that will fill the Mishkan – including the ark, table, menorah, and sacrificial altar – as well as the Mishkan’s walls and curtains.

beauty.bio: While the book of Proverbs [declares](https://www.sefaria.org/Proverbs.31.30?lang=en) that "beauty is vain" and only "fear of God" is praiseworthy, the Torah notes the beauty of many of its central characters – like Sarah, Rachel, and Joseph, to name a few. Biblical commentators, rabbinic literature, and works of musar and Jewish thought all reckon with the role beauty – physical beauty, spiritual beauty, the beauty of human and animal forms, beauty of the Temple, and more – plays in Judaism, analyzing when it is to be seen as a value to strive for and when it is to be dismissed as a frivolous distraction from more important matters.

sin-of-the-spies.bio: The twelve spies feature in the books of Numbers and Deuteronomy. They were a group of leaders - one from each tribe - sent by Moses to scout the land of Canaan ahead of the Israelites arrival there. They came back reporting that the land was good, but that it was filled with giants and fearsome enemies. This prompted the Israelites to cry and complain that they did not want to enter the land. Two of the twelve spies, Joshua and Caleb, attempted to assuage their fears and remind the people of God’s commitment that the Israelites’ would enter the land.

the-priestly-service.bio: The priestly service consisted of ritual worship carried out by priests in the [Tabernacle (Mishkan)](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/mishkan?tab=sources) and later in the Temple. The service included offering daily sacrifices, cleaning and preparing the altar for sacrifice, arranging the [menorah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/menorah1?tab=sources) for lighting, offering the [ketoret](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/incense?tab=sources), and more. Particulars of the service are discussed in detail in the [book of Leviticus](https://www.sefaria.org/Leviticus?tab=contents) and in the fifth order of the Mishnah and Talmud, [Kodashim](https://www.sefaria.org/texts/Mishnah/Seder%20Kodashim).

the-menorah.bio: The Menorah is a large candelabra with seven branches that is located within the Temple shrine. The Menorah is lit and cleaned by the priests every day. The Menorah used on Hanukkah is called a Hanukkiah, and it has nine branches.

wild-animals.bio: The plague of wild animals is the fourth of ten plagues that God wrought upon the Egyptians when Pharoah refused to let the enslaved Israelites go. It is described in [Exodus 8:16-28](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.8.16?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he)

renewal.bio: Renewal is a concept that can involve revitalizing, reinvigorating, or returning to an original state. In Judaism, the term can be used to refer to a specific 20th-century Jewish movement, but it is also a concept found throughout biblical and rabbinic literature. The idea is particularly prevalent throughout Chasidic texts, which emphasize performing routine commandments as if it were one’s first time performing them.

comfort.bio: Comfort appears in the Jewish tradition as an ease to be wished and prayed for, as a luxury to be wary of, and as an assistance to be offered to others. The act of comforting those in grief or distress in particular is a personal and communal obligation, and also a divine act. Halakhah (Jewish law) regulating mourning practices provides guidelines for mourners and their supporters receiving and giving comfort after the death of a close family member. In the Tanakh (Hebrew Bible), prophets such as Isaiah and Jeremiah preach prophecies of comfort in response to national catastrophes (as well as prophecies of rebuke and warning in advance of them). Certain such prophecies of consolation from the book of Isaiah are traditionally read publicly in the seven weeks following the communal mourning

diverse-abilities.bio: Biblical [texts](https://www.sefaria.org/Jeremiah.31.8?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) describe people with disabilities as an organic part of the community. Halakhic texts analyze how certain disabilities can affect the fulfillment of particular commandments, with some rulings being reevaluated in light of modern medical technology and contemporary understandings. Rabbinic literature, with its emphasis on learning, discusses tailoring education to the needs of the pupil, including those with disabilities. Works throughout the Jewish textual tradition explore the concept of disability from a range of perspectives.

planting.bio: Planting (shtila) was — and remains — a central feature of the yearly cycle upon which Judaism and Israel are built. In ancient Israel, crops had to be planted at a specific time in order to be tithed at the Temple at the right time in the calendar.

simchat-torah.bio: Simchat Torah is a joyous festival celebrating the completion of the year's cycle of reading the Torah. It is common to find people singing and dancing in synagogues — and even in the streets — with Torah scrolls.

superstitions1.bio: Gentile practices related to idolatry or magic.

happiness.bio:
Many biblical characters express thankfulness and laughter; the first character described explicitly as feeling happy is [Aaron](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.4.14?ven=hebrew|Tanach_with_Ta%27amei_Hamikra&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). Later parts of the Torah tell of the [happiness](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.14.26?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) that will accompany worshiping God in God’s chosen place in the land of Israel, and the Torah commands [rejoicing](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.16.14?lang=bi&aliyot=0) on the festivals.
Some Jewish thinkers see happiness as naturally accompanying, or as something that should accompany, [performing the commandments](https://www.sefaria.org.il/Mishneh_Torah%2C_Shofar%2C_Sukkah_and_Lulav.8.15?lang=bi&with=all&amp

menasseh-(king-of-judah).bio: Menasseh was the fourteenth king of Judah and considered to be a wicked king. He reintroduced polytheistic practices into Judah and was a vassal king of Assyria.

shivah-asar-betammuz.bio: The 17th day of the Hebrew month of Tammuz is a minor Jewish fast day. It commemorates the breach of Jerusalem's walls in 70 CE that led to the destruction of the Second Temple three weeks later. The day marks the onset of a period of three weeks of mourning practices, culminating with the fast day of [Tisha B'Av](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/tisha-bav?tab=sources), the day on which the Temple was destroyed.

tikkun-olam.bio: In modern times, the concept of "*tikkun olam*" — translated as “repair,” “betterment,” or “improvement” of the world — is commonly used to refer to the pursuit of social justice. When the term first appears throughout the [fourth chapter of tractate Gittin](https://www.sefaria.org/Mishnah_Gittin.4.1?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) in the Mishnah, it refers to rabbinic edicts likely meant to foster social order, like [Hillel](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/hillel?tab=admin)’s [prozbul](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/prozbul?tab=admin) and the decree that two witnesses [must sign](https://www.sefaria.org/Mishnah_Gittin.4.3?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) a divorce document. In the ancient [*Aleinu*](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/aleinu?tab=admin) prayer, the term is used in the conte

bar-mitzvah.bio: A bar mitzvah is a coming of age ceremony, traditionally marked for Jewish boys at the age of 13. At this age, rabbinic sources indicate that boys become obligated in the commandments and accountable for their own actions. The practice of marking this transition with a religious ceremony can be traced back to the medieval period.

rabbi-elazar-b-pedat.bio: R. Elazar studied under Rav before moving from Babylonia. In Palestine, he studied under R. Chiyya and became one of the community's outstanding scholars. While having differences with R. Yochanan, the two often collaborated. When the latter died, R. Elazar became the head of the academy in Tiberias.

yehudah-b-tema.bio: Not much is known about R. Yehudah b. Tema, but he is described as a master of the Mishnah. He was a student of R. Yehuda haNasi.

etrog.bio: An etrog is a citron fruit and one of [four species](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/the-four-species?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) held together and shaken during the holiday of Sukkot. The commandment to gather the four species originates in [Leviticus 23:40](https://www.sefaria.org/Leviticus.23.40?lang=he&with=Translations&lang2=he); rabbinic sources discuss the exact meaning of each of the phrases in the verse and ultimately identify "the product of *hadar*  trees" as the etrog.

shifrah-and-puah1.bio: Shifrah and Puah are biblical characters mentioned in [Exodus 1](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.1.15?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). They are described as midwives who defy Pharaoh’s orders to kill all Israelite baby boys. Instead, Shifrah and Puah keep the babies alive, telling Pharaoh that the Israelite women give birth before the midwives are able to get there. God rewards the midwives for their actions. The midrash identifies Shifrah and Puah as [Yokheved](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/yokheved) and her daughter [Miriam](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/miriam), respectively.

four-cups.bio: On Passover night during the seder, Jews are commanded to drink four cups (Arba'ah Kosot) of wine (or grape juice).

potiphar.bio: Potiphar is a biblical character who features primarily in Genesis 39. An officer of Pharaoh, Potiphar purchases Joseph as a slave and subsequently appoints him to oversee the household. When Potiphar’s wife attempts to seduce Joseph and Joseph refuses, she accuses Joseph of trying to sleep with her, and Potiphar imprisons Joseph.

selichot.bio: Selichot are penitential prayers and hymns recited before and during the Ten Days of Repentance that extend from Rosh Hashanah to Yom Kippur. Its central components are the confession of sins (vidui) and the recitation of God's thirteen attributes (Exodus 34:6–7).

efron.bio: Efron was the Hittite property owner, from whom Abraham about the Cave of Machpelah in Hebron, which become the Tomb of the Patriarchs, where Abraham buried his wife Sarah, and where Isaac, Rebecca, Jacob and Leah were later buried.

generosity.bio: Jewish textual tradition values giving and generosity. The Torah instructs providing for those in need and requires the distribution of agricultural gifts to the poor, the priests, and the Levites. Later Jewish texts elaborate on these commandments, discussing who should give, how much, and in what ways, as well as the spiritual impacts of generosity.

parashat-vzot-haberachah.bio: VeZot HaBerakhah is the final portion in the annual cycle of Torah reading. In it, Moses blesses each of the tribes, excluding the tribe of Simeon. He then ascends Mount Nebo, sees the land of Israel, and dies at the age of 120. The Israelites mourn, and the portion ends highlighting Moses’ unparalleled greatness.

menorah1.bio: The menorah was a large candelabra with seven branches located within the Temple shrine in Jerusalem. The menorah was lit and cleaned by the priests every day. The menorah used on the holiday of [Chanukkah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/chanukkah?tab=sources) is called a [chanukkiah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/menorah?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources), and it has nine branches.

grief.bio: As a powerful emotion and a universal part of the human experience, grief appears throughout the Jewish textual tradition. Biblical and talmudic sources tell stories of people who grieve, halakhic texts discuss practices and customs of mourning, and works of Jewish thought discuss how to process and cope with grief.

returning-light1.bio:
This is the [light](/topics/light2) that is not received in the fourth level. That is, its special function is to fill the fourth level, which, however, does not receive the [light](/topics/light2), on account of the [partition](/topics/partition2) that blocks and rebuffs it. This action is called [fusion through collision](/topics/fusion-through-collision) (see *Histaklut Penimit*, v. 2, section 22, s.v., “*ve-hineh*”). Likewise, every receiving [vessel](/topics/vessel1) in the [*partzufim*](/topics/partzuf), from the [constriction](/topics/constriction) ([*tzimtzum*](/topics/tzimtzum1)) and onwards, is drawn from this returning light, which serves for them in place of the fourth level in [*Ein Sof*](/topics/ein-sof1). 
V. 2, *Histaklut Penimit*, 79 (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 2, Q & A

alexander.bio: Alexander the Great, or Alexander III of Macedon, is discussed in a legendary fashion in the Rabbinic sources, which describe him as a powerful yet fair-minded conqueror. Some Jewish sources have Alexander seeking advice from the Jewish sages to help him in his campaigns.

mouth.bio: The mouth, as the lowest organ in the human head, represents the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1), the last and lowest of the [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot). With respect to the structure of the [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1), which corresponds to the entire structure of man, the mouth is the [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) of the ten *sefirot* of the [head](/topics/head2).

shamaya-and-avtalyon.bio: Descended from converts, Shamaya and Avtalyon safeguarded the study of Torah and the preservation of Jewish law during the turbulent succession of Queen Shlomtzion and into the reign of Herod. Though generally uninvolved in politics, Shamaya urged his colleagues to be unafraid of Herod and to treat him impartially in court.

mouth1.bio: The [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) of the [head](/topics/head2) is called mouth (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 3, Q & A 80).

beit-midrash.bio: Beit midrash--"house of study"--is a dedicated space for Torah learning. Unlike a synagogue, which primarily serves as a venue for prayer services, a beit midrash is a place to study individually, with a *chavruta* (study partner), or in groups.

plague-of-the-firstborn.bio: The plague of the firstborn, in which firstborn Egyptian sons died, was the last of ten plagues that God wrought upon the Egyptians when Pharoah refused to let the enslaved Israelites go. It is described throughout [Exodus 11:1—12:36](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.11.1?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en).

the-tree-of-knowledge.bio: The tree of knowledge is a tree that features in the opening chapters of the [book of Genesis](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis?tab=contents). God causes the tree to grow in the [Garden of Eden](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/garden-of-eden?tab=sources), and commands [Adam](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/adam?tab=sources) not to eat from it, lest he die. In the following chapter, [Eve](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/eve?tab=sources), following the enticing of a snake, notices the desirable qualities of a tree in the Garden. She eats from the tree's fruits and gives to Adam as well. God then calls out to Adam, and describes consequences that the snake, Eve, and Adam will each face as a result of their actions.

physicality.bio: Many biblical and rabbinic sources describe God using the language of physicality, but influential rabbinic texts emphasize that denial of God's physicality is a [key principle of Jewish faith](https://www.sefaria.org/Rambam_on_Mishnah_Sanhedrin.10.1.18?lang=en). Philosophers, scholars, kabbalists, and commentators have all attempted to explain how a transcendent God can be described in human terms. A few have maintained that God does or could indeed possess a physical form, some have interpreted physical language in traditional sources metaphorically, and some have reinterpreted it in light of kabbalistic concepts.

ban.bio: *Ban*, or *bet-nun*, is the numerical value of 52 in Hebrew letters (*nun*=50; *bet*=2). This numerical value is derived from the letters of the Tetragrammaton, the four-letter name of God referred to as the name of [Havaya](/topics/havaya2), when they are spelled out in full using the letter *heh*. In other words, each of its letters of the name of [Havaya](/topics/havaya2) is written out as a separate word, and the letter *heh* is used in order to fill out the letters, as follows: The *yod* is spelled out as *yod-vav-dalet* (יוד), the *heh* is spelled *heh-heh* (הה), the *vav* is spelled *vav-vav* (וו), and the final *heh* is spelled *heh-heh* (הה). *Ban* represents the [level](/topics/level) of [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) or the light of [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1).

navot.bio: Navot (Naboth in English) owned a vineyard adjacent to the property of King Ahab of the Northern Kingdom. Ahab covets Navot's property and schemes to take ownership of it, which he ultimately did and for which he is properly chastised by the prophet Elijah.

haya1.bio: The light of [Ḥokhma](/topics/hokhma1), in the mystical meaning of the verse (Ecclesiastes 7:12): “Wisdom [*ḥokhma*] preserves the life of he who has it” (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 2, Q & A 27).

parents.bio: Parents feature in Jewish texts in a variety of contexts. The Torah, for example, tells of the patriarchs and matriarchs in the book of Genesis. Rabbinic sources discuss parental responsibilities and obligations of children to their parents.

niggun.bio: Niggun is a melody, generally unaccompanied by words. In the Bible, it is often associated with uplifting or calming properties. Its spiritual potential was reclaimed in Chasidut, such that one of the common duties of a Chasidic rebbe is to sing, and sometimes even compose, melodies.

yochanan-ben-bag-bag.bio: Ben Bag Bag was an early scholar who was considered to be very learned.

rosh-hashanah-and-yom-kippur-prayers.canonical: Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur Prayers
aliases: Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur Prayers
rosh-hashanah-and-yom-kippur-prayers.bio: The Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur Prayers are based upon the structure of the daily prayers. Like on most special days, there is an additional service in the morning, and Yom Kippur has a fifth service as well. Alongside, many psalms, prayers and hymns are also added.

yochanan-the-high-priest.bio: Yochanan (John Hyrkanus) the High Priest was the son of Shimon the Hasmonean and succeeded him as high priest and political leader. He fought several wars against the Syrian Greeks and local enemies. Among them were the Idumeans whom he forced to convert. Though loyal to the Pharisees most of his life, he was finally seduced into joining with the Sadducees.

reincarnation.bio: The concept of souls being reborn in other bodies or things after death is not explicitly mentioned in the Bible or in classical rabbinic texts like the Mishnah and Talmud but is discussed in later kabbalistic texts and works of Jewish thought. Some of these texts debate the existence of reincarnation, while others explain the purpose of reincarnation, how some biblical figures were reincarnations of other biblical figures, and how certain commandments in the Torah help facilitate the process of reincarnation.

graves.bio: There are many laws about graves (kever) in Judaism not only because of their ritual impurity, but also because of their status as property. Abraham’s first permanent stake in the land of Israel was the purchase of a burial plot for his wife and family near Hebron.

body3.bio: The [enclothing](/topics/enclothe) of the lights in the vessels must initially be in “potential” form, and only subsequently “actualized.” The [enclothing](/topics/enclothe) of the ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) in “potential” form is called [head](/topics/head2), while the [enclothing](/topics/enclothe) of the ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) in “actualized” form is called body (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 4, Q & A 13).

bal-tashchit.bio: The Hebrew term *bal tashchit* literally means "do not destroy" and refers to the biblical prohibition against destroying or wasting that which is yet useful. The initial biblical context of this commandment refers to the needless destruction of fruit-bearing trees. The rabbis of subsequent generations extended this principle to include other kinds of senseless waste.

asiya2.bio: The ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) of the level of [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1), which receives from [*Ze’er Anpin*](/topics/zeer-anpin1), are called the world of *Asiya*.

ruah.bio: This is the name for the second of the five [levels](/topics/level) (and corresponding light) of the soul: [*nefesh*](/topics/nefesh), [*ruaḥ*](/topics/ruah1), [*neshama*](/topics/neshama2), [*ḥaya*](/topics/haya1), and [*yeḥida*](/topics/yehida1). These five [levels](/topics/level) of the soul correspond to the five primary *sefirot*, [*Keter*](/topics/keter1), [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1), [*Bina*](/topics/bina3), [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1), and [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1), as well as the five worlds, [*Adam Kadmon*](/topics/adam-kadmon2), [*Atzilut*](/topics/atzilut), [*Beria*](/topics/beria1), [*Yetzira*](/topics/yetzira1), and [*Asiya*](/topics/asiya2). The *ruaḥ* [level](/topics/level) of the soul corresponds to and is the light of the [level](/topics/level) of [*Tiferet*](/topic

rav-idi-b-avin-(i).bio: Rav Idi b. Avin was a student of Rav Chisda and passed on many teachings from the Land of Israel. He was a colleague of Abaye and a leading teacher in his day. He had both illustrious students, like Rav Pappa, and children, like Rav Sheshet.

kaddish.bio: Kaddish is an Aramaic prayer that declares praise of God. Its four main variants punctuate Jewish prayer services, marking the end of each section and its conclusion. It describes God's holiness and greatness and calls for the recognition of God's eternal rule over all of mankind. It is only recited in the presence of a prayer quorum and is further adapted for different contexts, including burials and to mark ritually the conclusion of a significant body of learning, a siyyum.

asarah-betevet.bio: Asarah BeTevet, literally, "the tenth of Tevet" is minor fast day on the tenth day of the Hebrew month of [Tevet](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/tevet) that lasts from dawn until nightfall. It commemorates the Babylonian siege of Jerusalem in the sixth century BCE that ultimately culminated with the destruction of the First Temple.

tallit.bio: A tallit is a large shawl — or some other four cornered garment — that requires tzitzit (fringes) and is worn over the head and shoulders during prayer. The tallit serves as a reminder of God's presence and God's commandments.

cave-of-the-patriarchs.bio: The Cave of the Patriarchs and Matriarchs — or ma'arat hamakhpelah in Hebrew — is the area in Hebron that Abraham purchased as a burial place for his wife Sarah in [Genesis 23](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.23.1?lang=bi&with=Navigation&lang2=en). As the family tomb of the patriarchs and many of matriarchs of the Jewish people, it is considered a holy site and is a pilgrimage destination and site of prayer.

nisan.bio: Nisan is the seventh month of the Jewish calendar and the first month in the biblical calendar. It is the month during which God redeemed the children of Israel from slavery in Egypt and so also the month during which [Passover](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/passover?tab=sources) is celebrated.

tower-of-babel.bio: The Tower of Babel refers to a narrative presented in [Genesis 11:1-9](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.11.1?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). The story describes how people attempted to build a city and tower, the top of which would reach the sky. God confounds their speech and scatters them, ending the project. The story has been the subject of much analysis and discussion throughout thousands of years of the Jewish textual tradition.

the-tree-of-life.bio: The first appearance of the phrase "tree of life" (*etz chaim* in Hebrew) in the Torah is in the Genesis story: a tree of life grows in the garden of Eden alongside the tree of knowledge. A [verse](https://www.sefaria.org/Proverbs.3.18?lang=bi) from Proverbs comparing the Torah to a tree of life is commonly sung in Ashkenazi synagogues when returning the Torah to the ark, and the term is used [elsewhere](https://www.sefaria.org/Proverbs.11.30?lang=bi&with=Navigation&lang2=en) [in](https://www.sefaria.org/Proverbs.15.4?lang=bi&with=Navigation&lang2=en) the book of Proverbs as well. In kabbalistic sources, the tree of life often represents a diagram of ten divine emanations, or *sefirot*.* It is also the title of a kabbalistic [book](https://www.sefaria.org/Sefer_Etz_Chaim?ta

neshama1.bio: The term *neshama* commonly refers to the soul, the divine aspect that exists within every being and sustains it. But according to the teachings of the Kabbala, the soul actually contains five [levels](/topics/level) or lights – [*nefesh*](/topics/nefesh), [*ruaḥ*](/topics/ruah1), [*neshama*](/topics/neshama2), [*ḥaya*](/topics/haya1), and [*yeḥida*](/topics/yehida1) – the third of which is called “*neshama*.” These five [levels](/topics/level) (synonymous with the five lights that correspond and fill the five [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) vessels) of the soul correspond to the five [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) of [*Keter*](/topics/keter1), [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1), [*Bina*](/topics/bina3), [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1), and [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1), as well as the five worlds of [*

winds.bio: Wind plays a role from the very beginning of the Torah, as a [wind from God](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.1.2?lang=bi&vside=The_Contemporary_Torah,_Jewish_Publication_Society,_2006|en&with=Translation%20Open&lang2=en) sweeps over the waters in the opening verses of Genesis. Winds are critical in other prominent biblical stories as well, such as when the wind helps split the Red Sea, brings [quail](https://www.sefaria.org/Numbers.11.31?lang=bi&aliyot=0) to the desert, or helps revive the dead in Ezekiel's [Valley of Dry Bones prophecy](https://www.sefaria.org/Ezekiel.37.1?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). In rabbinic texts, the rabbis discuss how wind affects areas of halakhah, such as under what conditions someone is [liable for damages](https://www.sefaria.org.il/Bava

yom-haatzmaut.bio: Yom HaAtzmaut is Israel's Independence Day. It is usually celebrated on the fifth day of the Hebrew month of Iyyar, the date on which the Israeli provisional government signed its Declaration of Independence in 1948. The day is marked by a variety of ceremonies, celebrations, and observances, including a unique liturgy in many synagogues.

clouds.bio: Clouds feature in the Torah in various contexts: God [tells](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.9.14-16?lang=bi&aliyot=0) Noah and his family that a rainbow in a cloud will serve as a sign of God's covenant with humanity and promise to never again to destroy the world with a flood. A [heavy cloud](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.19.16?ven=hebrew|Tanach_with_Ta%27amei_Hamikra&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) descends on Mount Sinai as the people of Israel receive the Torah, and a pillar of cloud from God leads the people of Israel as they wander through the desert. Later Jewish texts analyze the meaning of clouds in biblical stories, the mystical significance of clouds, and halakhic (legal) considerations relating to clouds — like how movements of clouds may affect when one says a ble

head-coverings.bio: Through the ages, Jews have covered their heads in different ways, depending somewhat on social context, even as they debate whether this practice is a law or a custom. While today, covering one’s head is associated primarily with humility, modesty, and piety, discussion of the issue has included mystical dimensions and protection against evil spirits.

redemption-of-captives.bio: The redemption of captives is a topic discussed throughout rabbinic literature. Instances of redeeming captives are found in the Bible, for example when Abraham [goes to war](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.14.14?ven=The_Koren_Jerusalem_Bible&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) with four kings in order to save his captured nephew, [Lot](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/lot?tab=sources). While sources stress the importance of redeeming captives, calling it a "[great mitzvah](https://www.sefaria.org/Bava_Batra.8b.2?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en)," other texts warn not to redeem captives [for more than their value](https://www.sefaria.org/Mishnah_Gittin.4.6?lang=bi).

yetzer-hatov.bio: Though the Hebrew word *yetzer* only appears in the Bible in reference to people's [evil inclinations](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/yetzer-hara?tab=admin), rabbinic sources discuss how alongside a person's evil inclination is a parallel good inclination (*yetzer hatov*) that has the potential to conquer or control the evil inclination. Some texts describe this good inclination developing at age 13, or [bar mitzvah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/bar-mitzvah?tab=admin) age.

bava-b-buta.bio: Bava b. Buta studied under Shammai. He was so pious that he brought a sacrifice every day just in case he sinned. Though he was blinded by Herod, he was the only sage in his entourage whose life was spared,

shiluach-haken.bio: The commandment of sending away the mother bird from the nest.

ben-sorer-umoreh.bio: The *Ben Sorer Umoreh* — known in English as the“wayward and rebellious son” — is a case discussed in Deuteronomy 21:18–21 This “rebellsious son” acts as a glutton and drunkard, does not listen to his parents, and is ultimately put to death. Rabbinic texts delve into the conditions under which one becomes a ben sorer umoreh and the scenario's moral complexities. Most opinions in rabbinic literature maintain that such a case never happened and never could happen.

itamar.bio: Ithamar was the youngest of four sons of Aaron the High Priest. He was involved in the ritual work that took place in the Tabernacle (Mishkan), and oversaw the work of the Levite families of Gershon and Merari as they carried the Tabernacle’s curtains and beams during the Israelites’ travels in the desert.

for-its-own-sake.bio: In Judaism, to do something “for its own sake" (l’shma) is understood as having the purest, most selfless of intentions. For example, someone who studies Torah for its own sake seeks no personal gain by learning but rather wants only to show love and respect for God.

zachor.bio: Paradoxically, the Torah requires Jews to remember [Amalek's](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/amalek?tab=sources) attack [on them as they left slavery in Egypt](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.17.8?ven=The_Contemporary_Torah,_Jewish_Publication_Society,_2006&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) even as they are required simultaneously to "blot out the memory of Amalek." This mitzvah is typically fulfilled by ensuring that one hears the special liturgical reading of the verses in Deuteronomy that contain this commandment on the Shabbat before the holiday of [Purim](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/purim?tab=sources), which celebrates the Jewish triumph over the wicked Amalekite [Haman](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/jerusalem?sort=Relevance&tab=sources).

aliases: Jael, wife of Heber the Kenite
Yael, the wife of Heber the Kenite
yael.bio: Yael was a figure in the book of Judges who lured Sisera, an enemy Canaanite general, into her tent. She put him to sleep by giving him warm milk and killed him by hammering a tent peg into his skull.

teaching.bio: Teaching is a central value in Judaism that features in a variety of context. The biblical patriarch Abraham is instructed to teach his descendants the ways of justice and righteousness, for examples, and rabbinic literature analyzes the responsibilities of parents to ensure that their children receive an education.

shabbat-candles.bio: Shabbat candles — nerot shabbat, in Hebrew — are used in the sanctification and celebration of Shabbat. The two candles symbolize the commandments to observe and remember the Sabbath, and they provide light in the home at night to enhance the atmosphere of Shabbat.

wholeness.bio: The Torah uses a version of the word "wholeness" in the context of a commandment to have "[completely honest](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.25.15?lang=bi&with=Translations&lang2=en)" weights and measures and in the context of the commandment to build the altar with [whole stones](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.27.6?lang=bi&with=Translations&lang2=en). Verses in the Prophets section of the Hebrew Bible speak of [wholeness](https://www.sefaria.org/I_Kings.8.61?lang=bi&with=Translations&lang2=en) of the [heart](https://www.sefaria.org/I_Kings.15.3?lang=bi&with=Translations&lang2=en), meaning honesty and thorough commitment. Sources of Jewish thought use the Hebrew word for "wholeness" – *sheleimut* – to talk about the goal of achieving [spiritual per

aliases: The Ten Days of Repentance - test
the-ten-days-of-repentance.bio: The first ten days of the Hebrew month of [Tishrei](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/tishrei?tab=admin)—beginning with [Rosh Hashanah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/rosh-hashanah?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) and ending with the conclusion of [Yom Kippur](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/yom-kippur?tab=admin)—are referred to in Judaism as the ten days of repentance. These days are a time for renewal, introspection, prayer, and good deeds. They are marked by unique additions to the daily liturgy and customs that vary by community, such as only eating certain types of food.

altars.bio: Prior to the construction of the Temple in Jerusalem, many homes and other locations had altars, or bamot, that allowed for sacrifices and other offerings. After the Temple was built, however, these altars — both private and public — were prohibited as ritual was centralized.

shabbat-hagadol.bio: Shabbat HaGadol (“The Great Shabbat”) is the Shabbat that immediately precedes the holiday of Passover. Jewish texts offer many explanations as to why this Shabbat is called “great,” like those that  point to a great miracle performed on this Shabbat during the exodus, or those that point to the word “great” at the end of the haftarah portion traditionally read on this Shabbat. In many communities, the rabbi delivers a lengthy sermon on a Passover-related topic.

bezalel-and-oholiav.bio: Bezalel is a biblical character who features in the Book of Exodus. He was the artisan charged with building the [mishkan](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/mishkan?tab=sources) (tabernacle), its vessels, and the [priestly garments](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/priestly-garments?tab=sources), assisted by Oholiav and other workers. The Torah describes how God filled Bezalel with ["with the spirit of God, in wisdom, and in understanding, and in knowledge, and in all manner of workmanship."](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.31.3?ven=The_Koren_Jerusalem_Bible&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en)

proverbs.bio: Proverbs (“Mishlei”), the second book of the section in the Hebrew Bible called Writings, contains guidance for living a wise, moral, and righteous life, in the form of poems and short statements. It consists of both general observations about human nature as well as lessons directly instructing the listener to take a course of action, covering themes like avoiding bad influences, the value of hard work, properly using the power of speech, and the importance of fearing God.

beruriah.bio: The wife of R. Meir and daughter of R. Chananyah b. Teradyon, Beruriah was an outstanding scholar in her own right. Her wisdom is showcased in the Talmud, showing her great insight which particularly benefited her husband.

aarons-death.bio: The death of the High Priest, Aaron, is discussed in [Numbers 20:23-29](https://www.sefaria.org/Numbers.20.24-29?lang=bi&aliyot=0) and [Deuteronomy 10:6](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.10.6?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en).

rest.bio: Rest is the abstention from creative work. After the six days of creation, God rested, and the fourth of the Ten Commandments — Shabbat — is to cease from creative work and rest just as God did. The Torah also commands that the land of Israel be left to lie fallow every seventh year in observance of [shemittah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/shemitah?tab=sources). Rest is also required on holidays. While rest is the means of renewing our bodies and souls, tradition also recognizes it as a taste of the world to come.

encampment-of-israel.bio: The Encampment of Israel (Machane Yisrael) refers to the layout of the Israelite camp on their way from Egypt to Israel. This encampment is often contrasted to the dwellings of the Levites, which the Israelite encampment surrounded, or to the area outside of it to where the impure were sent.

tiberias.bio: Located along the Sea of Galilee, Tiberias (Tiveria) has long been one of northern Israel’s most important cities. Founded by the Romans and known for its hot springs, Tiberias became a spiritual center with a large Jewish community and a thriving fishing industry.

minyan.bio: A *minyan* is a quorum of ten Jewish adults – in traditional Orthodox communities, ten males, and in more progressive communities, ten people – required to recite specific public prayers and for other religious purposes. The Talmud [derives](https://www.sefaria.org/Berakhot.21b.5?lang=bi&namedEntity=jewish-people&namedEntityText=children%20of%20Israel&with=Lexicon&lang2=en) this concept from the story of the [spies](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/the-spies?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) and [elsewhere](https://www.sefaria.org/Ketubot.7a.13?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) mentions the books of Ruth and Psalms as sources of the requirement for a *minyan* in particular circumstances.

ein-sof1.bio: In kabbalistic literature, *Ein Sof*, or the Infinite One, has two meanings. One is the initial stage of creation, what is referred to as the Creator’s intent for creation, before the first [constriction](/topics/constriction) ([*tzimtzum*](/topics/tzimtzum1)), when the light and vessel are infinite. That is, there is an infinite will to receive that draws into it infinite light. The other meaning of *Ein Sof* is a “relative” *Ein Sof*, which is the [*Keter*](/topics/keter1) of each [level](/topics/level) of reality, because in relation to the [levels](/topics/level) beneath it, [*Keter*](/topics/keter1) is regarded as absolute light, without vessel or limitation. Thus, for example, the world of [*Adam Kadmon*](/topics/adam-kadmon2) is defined as *Ein Sof* in relation to the worlds of [*At

havdalah.bio: Havdalah — literally, separation — is the ritual that marks the end of Shabbat, separating it from the rest of the week. It consists of blessings over wine, fire, and sweet fragrances, and the separation between holy time and non-holy time, light and darkness, and Israel and the nations.

maror.bio: Maror refers to the bitter herbs — usually horseradish or romaine lettuce — eaten by Jews on Passover as commanded in the Bible: “with bitter herbs they shall eat it” (Exodus 12:8). Maror symbolizes and serves as a reminder of the bitterness of slavery and oppression in Egypt.

rebellions.bio: The Bible tells the stories of several rebellions, like that of [Korach](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/korach?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) and his followers, those of [Adoniah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/adoniah?tab=admin) and [Absalom](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/absalom?tab=admin) against their father, King David, and the anointing of [Jehu](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/yehu?tab=admin) as king as a rebellion against [Ahab](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/ahab?tab=admin). The Torah also discusses how parents deal with [a wayward and rebellious son](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/ben-sorer-umoreh?tab=admin). Post-biblical Jewish history is filled with notable revolts and rebellions, like the [Maccabean](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/maccabees?tab=admin) revolt and the

tosafot.bio: The tosafists — *ba'alei hatosafot* — were members of a school of Torah and talmudic interpretation that flourished in 12th- and 13th-century France and Germany. Their roots are in the work of [Rashi](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/rashi?tab=popular-writing-of). Rashi's own sons-in-law and grandsons are counted among the founders and most influential tosafists. Their comments on almost every tractate of the Talmud were collected, edited, augmented, and passed on for generations. Their approach is analytical, comparative, and incisive. They also often take a critical stance regarding Rashi's commentary. They were amongst the first French and German rabbis to quote the Jerusalem Talmud extensively and the commentary of [Rabbenu Chananel](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/rabeinu-chananel) on th

plague-of-hail.bio: Hail is the seventh of ten plagues that God wrought upon the Egyptians when Pharoah refused to let the enslaved Israelites go. It is described in [Exodus 9:13-35](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.9.13?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he)

huldah.bio: Huldah was a prophetess in the Southern Kingdom of Judah in the late seventh century BCE. King Josiah consults her when he finds a scroll of the law in the Temple, which she then authenticated.

vegetarianism.bio: According to the Talmud, from Adam to Noah humans practiced vegetarianism (tzimchonut). In the Jewish sources, God's eventual permission to eat meat is often seen as nothing more than an ethical compromise and Jewish law restricts the type and way that meat is eaten.

red-sea.bio: The Red Sea (Yam Suf in Hebrew) — often mistranslated as the Reed Sea — is located along Egypt's eastern border. In the book of Exodus, when the Israelites flee slavery in Egypt, the Red Sea stands between them and their freedom. God miraculously splits the Red Sea, allowing the Israelites to pass through. The Egyptians though pursued the Israelites into the Red Sea, wherein they drowned.

agriculture.bio: The opening chapter of the Torah recounts how humanity, from the outset of its creation, is charged with [mastering the natural world](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.1.28?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he). When Adam and Eve sin in the Garden of Eden, [God tells Adam](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.3.19?lang=he&with=Translations&lang2=he) that he will now have toil by the sweat of his brow to cultivate the land and produce bread. Later in biblical literature, agriculture becomes the basis of national and religious life as the Israelites settle in the land of Israel. Many commandments and rituals are centered upon agriculture and the agricultural calendar.

tzimtzum1.bio: The constriction refers to a diminishing of the divine light. This constriction does not constitute a change in the divine light itself, as the Creator bestows His light constantly and fully. Rather, any constriction of the light is the result of a change in the created vessels formed of the will to receive. In order for constriction to take place, the vessel is modified through the placement of a [partition](/topics/partition2) upon it so that it is prevented from receiving the light. There are two types of constriction in the process of creation, the first constriction (in which a [partition](/topics/partition2) is placed upon the vessel), and the [second constriction](/topics/second-constriction).

libations.bio: Libations (nesekim) are liquid offerings placed upon the Copper Altar that generally consist of wine and olive oil. On Sukkot, water is also used.

ohel-moed.bio: The Ohel Moed (Tent of Meeting) was a temporary structure that Moses used to communicate with God from outside the Israelite camp after the incident of the [golden calf](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/golden-calf?sort=Relevance&tab=sources). Most Jewish sources believe this tent was decommissioned once the [Tabernacle (Mishkan)](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/mishkan?tab=sources) was set up in [Exodus 40:17](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.40.17?lang=bi&aliyot=0) or became synonymous with the Tabernacle.

bathhouses.bio: Public bathhouses (merchatz) were a common feature in Talmudic and Medieval times. The sources discuss the use of bathhouses on Shabbat, whether they are permitted when connected to idols, and so on.

colors.bio: Colors appear throughout the Tanakh as elements of ritual, status, and symbolism. The commandment of [ritual fringes](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/tzitzit?sort=Relevance&tab=admin), for example, includes the incorporation of *[tekhelet](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/tekhelet?sort=Relevance&tab=admin)*, a blueish dye. Descriptions of the Mishkan repeatedly emphasize the use of colored materials — blue, purple, and crimson wool — in its curtains and priestly garments. The Torah requires taking a [red heifer](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/parah-adumah?sort=Relevance&tab=admin) as part of the purification process for those who have come into contact with death, and a priest determines whether someone has *tzara'at* – a discoloration condition on skin, a house, or clothing – in

samael.bio: Samael is an angel mentioned in second-Temple works of Apocrypha, rabbinic literature, and kabbalistic sources. He is generally presented as an agent of evil—occasionally identified as Satan—serving as an adversary, seducer, or destroyer.

aliases: Shimon ben Yeshua ben Eliezer ben Sira
ben-sira.bio: Ben Sira, also known as Simeon ben Jeshua ben Eleazar ben Sirach, was a second-century BCE Hellenistic Jewish scribe and sage from Seleucid-controlled Jerusalem. He wrote the book of Ben Sira, also known as Ecclesiasticus, an apocryphal poetic book of guidance for living a wise, ethical, and God-fearing life.

pregnancy.bio: Toward the beginning of the Torah, pregnancy and childbirth are mentioned as part of Eve's punishment for eating from the tree of knowledge: God tells her, "[In hardship shall you bear children.](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.3.16?ven=hebrew|Tanach_with_Ta%27amei_Hamikra&lang=bi&with=Navigation&lang2=en)" Later biblical stories tell of [struggles to conceive](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/infertility?tab=admin) and [challenges](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.25.22?ven=hebrew|Tanach_with_Ta%27amei_Hamikra&lang=en&with=all&lang2=en) of pregnancy. Rabbinic texts [express wonder](https://www.sefaria.org/Vayikra_Rabbah.14.2?lang=bi) at the miraculous nature of pregnancy, analyze halakhic aspects of pregnancy, – like whether pregnant women should fast on Yom Kippur –

moses-prayer-for-himself.bio: Moses' Prayer for Himself was the prayer at the end of his life that God cancel His decision not to allow him to cross the Jordan into Israel together with his people. Its conclusion - described in Deuteronomy - comes when God tells Moses that He will still not allow this.

yehida.bio: The name of the fifth and highest of the five [levels](/topics/level) of the soul,[*nefesh*](/topics/nefesh), [*ruaḥ*](/topics/ruah1), [*neshama*](/topics/neshama2), [*ḥaya*](/topics/haya1), and [*yeḥida*](/topics/yehida1). These five [levels](/topics/level) of the soul correspond to the five primary *sefirot*, [*Keter*](/topics/keter1), [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1), [*Bina*](/topics/bina3), [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1), and [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1), as well as the five worlds, [*Adam Kadmon*](/topics/adam-kadmon2), [*Atzilut*](/topics/atzilut), [*Beria*](/topics/beria1), [*Yetzira*](/topics/yetzira1), and [*Asiya*](/topics/asiya2). The concept of the five [levels](/topics/level) of the soul relates to five qualities of supernal light which illuminate people as well as the [levels](/t

rabbi-dostai-b-r-yannai.bio: R. Dostai b. Yannai appears throughout the Talmud and Midrash, primarily as a teacher of homiletical ideas. It is likely that he was a student of R. Meir.

maccabees.bio: The Maccabees were fighters from a priestly Jewish family who successfully waged war against the Seleucids and rededicated the Second Temple in Jerusalem — which is now commemorated on [Chanukkah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/chanukkah?tab=sources). The Maccabees established the [Hasmonean dynasty](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/hasmoneans?tab=sources), which ruled from 167 BCE to 37 BCE.

courage.bio: Many biblical characters display courage, like the midwives who defy Pharaoh's decree to kill Jewish babies or [Queen Esther](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/esther), who exposes the plan of the evil [Haman](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/haman) to King [Ahasuerus](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/ahasuerus). The charge to "[be strong and courageous](https://www.sefaria.org/Joshua.1.6?lang=bi&with=Translations&lang2=en)" appears several times throughout the Bible, especially to the character of Joshua as he prepares to lead the people into the land of Israel. Rambam, the 12th-century scholar, [counts](https://www.sefaria.org/Sefer_HaMitzvot%2C_Negative_Commandments.58.1?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) the prohibition of fearing the enemy and deserting battle as one of the commandme

ovadiah-seforno.bio: Ovadiah ben Ya'akov Seforno was an Italian rabbi, biblical commentator, philosopher, halakhic authority, and physician, who wrote commentaries on a good portion of the Tanakh. After an early period of wandering, he settled in Bologna, where he founded a yeshiva. Seforno was held in high regard by his contemporaries, Maharam Padua and Maharik Kolon, and is also quoted in responsa of contemporary authorities who consulted him on issues of halakhah. Seforno was admired for the breadth of his knowledge by King Henry II of France to whom he sent a Latin translation he prepared of his philosophical work, Ohr Amim.

gabriel-the-angel.bio: The first mention of the angel Gabriel is in the book of Daniel, [where he helps](https://www.sefaria.org/Daniel.8.16?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) to explain Daniel’s vision. He reappears throughout rabbinic literature and in the texts that accompany the bedtime recitation of the Shema. His name, which means “might of God,” explains why he often serves as an emissary of God’s strength.

creativity.bio: The Torah, Judaism's foundational text, begins with creative acts as God makes the world over six days. The same chapter describes how the first humans are created in [the image of God](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.1.27?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) and tasked with the project of creating new life and filling the earth. Texts throughout the Jewish textual tradition speak of the importance of imbuing religious practice — from [prayer](https://www.sefaria.org/Psalms.96.1?ven=hebrew|Tanach_with_Ta%27amei_Hamikra&lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) to [Torah study](https://www.sefaria.org/Chagigah.3a.15?ven=hebrew|William_Davidson_Edition_-_Vocalized_Aramaic&lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) — with creative spirit, the nature of creative work and its prohibition on Shabbat, and

lilith.bio: Lilith is a figure in Midrashic stories. She usually appears as a demon that preys on women in childbirth and young children. In Medieval Midrash, she was popularized as Adam's first companion who refused to submit to him and so was replaced by Eve.

tu-bav.bio: Tu B'Av — the fifteenth of Av — is a minor holiday. Falling just six days after the fast day of Tisha B'Av, which commemorates the destruction of the Temples in Jerusalem, Tu B'Av is described in rabbinic literature as one of the happiest days for the Jewish people. In antiquity, women would go out in festive clothing to dance in vineyards on this day. Today, the holiday is often viewed as a celebration of love.

aliases: Rabban Gamaliel the son of Rabbi Judah Hanasi
rabban-gamliel-b-rabbi-(iii).bio: R. Yehudah haNasi's older son, Rabban Gamliel b. Rabbi took over the post of nasi after his illustrious father. Though he died a few years later, he set a trend weakening the office when he appointed two others to the new and/or reinstated positions of Head of Court and Sage.

jewish-identity.bio: Many Jewish texts across genres illuminate the effort to define Jewish identity. Opinions vary whether character traits, national qualities, birth status, individual choice, covenantal obligation, spiritual quest, or something else define it.

jericho.bio: Jericho (Yericho) was a heavily fortified city located just west of the Jordan River and was the first settlement encountered by Joshua and the Israelites upon entering Israel. The city is mentioned again during the reign of King Ahab, when Hiel of Bethel attempts to rebuild it despite a curse placed on Jericho in the book of Joshua.

gad.bio: Gad was the seventh son of Jacob and his first with his concubine Zilpah. He was the founder of the Tribe of Gad.

yavneh.bio: After the destruction of Jerusalem and the Second Temple in 70 CE by the Romans, [Rabban Yochanan b. Zakkai](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/rabban-yochanan-b-zakkai?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) moved the [Sanhedrin](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/sanhedrin?tab=admin) to Yavneh, which became an important center of Jewish learning, rabbinic scholarship, and the development of rabbinic Judaism.

metatron.bio: Metatron is an angel mentioned several times throughout apocryphal, rabbinic, and kabbalistic literature. He is occasionally presented as a heavenly scribe. In two of the three instances in which he appears in the Talmud, his character prompts questions from heretics who question whether Metatron is a god. In some sources, Metatron is associated with the biblical character Enoch.

dew.bio: In biblical literature, dew appears as a marker of [divine sustenance](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.27.28?lang=bi&aliyot=0) and [blessing](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.33.13?lang=bi&aliyot=0). Many Jews recite a liturgical [poem](https://www.sefaria.org/Shabbat_Siddur_Sefard_Linear%2C_Prayers_for_Yom_Tov%2C_Musaf_for_Yom_Tov%2C_Prayer_for_Dew.1?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) called *tefillat tal* — "a prayer for dew" — on the holiday of Passover, and then continue to incorporate a [line](https://www.sefaria.org/Siddur_Edot_HaMizrach%2C_Weekday_Shacharit%2C_Amida.5?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) about dew in the daily Amidah (standing prayer) throughout the spring and summer months. Dew is also discussed in the context of resurrection, as a metaphor for Torah, and

vav.bio: Vav is the sixth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.

cup-for-the-blessing.bio: During the recitation of Birkat Hamazon (grace after the meal), when there are at least three people present, the Cup for the Blessing (kos shel bracha) is filled with wine and a special blessing is made. The person who is honored by leading birkat hamazon also drinks the cup of wine.

bilhah.bio: Bilhah is a biblical character first introduced in [Genesis 29:29](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.29.29?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) as Laban's maidservant, whom he gives to his daughter Rachel upon Rachel's marriage to Jacob. When Rachel cannot bear children, she gives Bilhah to Jacob so that Bilhah can bear children. Bilhah gives birth to two sons, Dan and Naftali.

the-prophecy-of-ezekiel.bio: Ezekiel was a prophet who spoke from Babylonian exile during the destruction of the First Temple. He had visions about [God's chariot](https://www.sefaria.org/Ezekiel.1.3?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=he&with=all&lang2=he), dry [bones](https://www.sefaria.org/Ezekiel.37.1?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) coming to life, a [future Temple](https://www.sefaria.org/Ezekiel.40.1?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=he&with=all&lang2=he), and more. According to Jewish tradition, accounts of his visions make up the [book of Ezekiel](https://www.sefaria.org/Ezekiel?tab=contents).

galil.bio: After the destruction of the Second Temple, the Galil region of northern Israel served as the center of Jewish life and seat of the Great Sanhedrin (Jewish council). In the Middle Ages, the Galilee once again became a center of Jewish life, culture, and mysticism.

plague-of-darkness.bio: Darkness is the ninth of ten plagues that God wrought upon the Egyptians when Pharaoh refused to let the enslaved Israelites go. It is described in [Exodus 10:21-29](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.10.21?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he).

ab.bio: *Ab*, or *ayin-bet*, is the numerical value of 72 in Hebrew letters (*ayin*=70; *bet*=2). This numerical value is derived from the letters of the Tetragrammaton, the four-letter name of God referred to as the name of [*Havaya*](/topics/havaya2), when they are spelled in full using the letter *yod*. In other words, each of the letters of the name of [*Havaya*](/topics/havaya2) is written out as a separate word, and the letter *yod* is used in order to fill out the letters: The *yod* is spelled out as *yod-vav-dalet* (יוד), the *heh* is spelled *heh-yod* (הי), the *vav* is spelled *vav-yod-vav* (ויו), and the final *heh* is spelled *heh-yod* (הי).. *Ab* represents the [level](/topics/level) of [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1) or the light of wisdom. In the world of [*Adam Kadmon*](/topics/adam-ka

halitzah.bio: Chalitzah is a rite, [described in the Torah](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.25.7?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) and practiced today, in which the widow of a childless man takes off the shoe of her husband’s brother and spits on the floor, discharging the obligation of [*yibbum*](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/levirate-marriage?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) (levirate marriage).

meat.bio: There is a lot of discussion in the Jewish sources about eating meat (basar), focusing largely on the laws for ensuring that meat is made kosher, or fit. In regards to mixing meat and milk, there is discussion about whether or not chicken and fish should be categorized as meat.

bedikat-chametz-biur-chametz.bio: Bedikat Chametz is the process of searching a home to ensure that it is free of chametz — that is, leavened products forbidden on Passover— performed before the holiday begins. Biur Chametz (literally, “burning of the chametz”) is the process of destroying any remaining chametz on the morning before Passover. Jewish sources abound with discussions of how exactly the chametz is to be eradicated, the timing of the process, and its spiritual significance.

jacob-ben-asher.bio: Jacob ben Asher was born in Germany and lived most of his life in Spain. He is most famous for having composed the great law code, the Arba'ah Turim or "Four Pillars," upon which Rabbi Joseph Karo based his Shulchan Arukh. He also composed a Torah commentary that remained in manuscript for centuries, a small extract of which, known as the Ba'al HaTurim al HaTorah, is largely characterized as gematria-based associations — that is interpretations derived from the numerical value equivalents of Hebrew words. These interpretations served as introductions to the main body of this work, eventually published in full, that draws on the works of other commentators, especially that of Ramban.

seven-species.bio: The seven species are seven special products of the land of Israel, listed in [Deuteronomy 8:8](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.8.8?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he). They are: wheat, barley, grape, fig, pomegranates, olive (oil), and date (date honey). The fruits have significance in different areas of Jewish law; for example, a unique blessing is recited after eating them.

yehoshua-b-perachya.bio: Yehoshua b. Perachya and Nittai haArbeli served as Nasi and head of the court under Yochanan the High Priest. When the latter turned against the Pharisees, Yehoshua - and presumably Nittai - temporarily fled to Egypt and became leader of the large Jewish community in Alexandria.

sefira2.bio: The ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) of [direct light](/topics/direct-light1), which are [enclothed](/topics/enclothe) in the ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) of [returning light](/topics/returning-light1), emerging from the first [fusion through collision](/topics/fusion-through-collision), are called one *sefira*. They are named after the highest *sefira* in that level, despite the fact that it incorporates ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) lengthwise and ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) in its thickness (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 3, Q & A 74).

firmament.bio: In the Bible, firmament (raqia) is the word used for a layer of sky or space. In Genesis, the Bible tells of how God created the firmament on the second day to separate the waters above (the sky) from the waters below.

three-weeks.bio: The "three weeks" refers to a period from the fast of the [17th of Tammuz](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/shivah-asar-betammuz?tab=admin) until the fast of [Tisha B'Av](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/tisha-bav?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources). The period is considered one of mourning over the destruction of the Temples in Jerusalem and other calamities in Jewish history. In many communities, it is marked by mourning customs, such as avoiding weddings, events with live music, and haircuts.

burning-bush.bio: The "burning bush" refers to a story in the book of Exodus in which Moses, while shepherding in the desert, encounters a bush that is burning but not consumed by fire and turns to examine it. God speaks to Moses from the bush, instructing him to return to Egypt to help take the people of Israel out of there.

rabbi-hananyah-b-hakhinai.bio: R. Chananyah b. Chakhinai was a prominent student of R. Tarfon and R. Akiva, notable for his complete devotion to his studies and for his knowledge of Jewish mysticism. He was martyred by the Romans.

daat1.bio: There are a number of ways to count and categorize the [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot). The most well-known count is that of ten, including [*Keter*](/topics/keter1), [*Hokhma*](/topics/hokhma1), [*Bina*](/topics/bina3), [*Hesed*](/topics/hesed), [*Gevura*](/topics/gevura), [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1), [*Netzah*](/topics/netzah), [*Hod*](/topics/hod1), [*Yesod*](/topics/yesod1) and [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1). This count can be thought of as the “core” [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot), the root ingredients of everything in existence. However, an alternative count of ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) removes [*Keter*](/topics/keter1) and adds the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of *Da’at* after [*Bina*](/topics/bina3). *Da’at* is not a true substitute for [*Keter*](/topics/keter1), as it is not one

plague-of-frogs.bio: The plague of frogs is the second of ten plagues that God wrought upon the Egyptians when Pharoah refused to let the enslaved Israelites go. It is described in [Exodus 8:1-11](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.8.1?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he)

the-gold-altar.bio: The Golden Alter (Mizbeach Hazahav), which is also known as the Altar of Incense, is located within the Temple shrine and is used for burning incense.

baseless-hatred1.bio: Baseless hatred is known in Judaism as the reason that the Second Temple was destroyed. Sources throughout the Jewish library discuss the corrosive nature of baseless hatred, its legal boundaries and implications, and ways to escape its grasp.

mixtures.bio: Mixtures (taaruvot) of permissible and forbidden substances are a subject of much discussion and debate in Jewish law. Different criteria are used to decide whether there is a sufficient amount of a forbidden material to render a whole mixture forbidden.

chosenness.bio: In [Genesis 12](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.12?lang=bi&aliyot=0), God singles out Abraham to make a covenant and later [quietly expresses what the role of his nation of descendants will be](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.18.20?lang=bi&aliyot=0). Later Jewish thinkers, across textual genres, emphasize the eternal, unconditional, and unbreakable nature of God’s election of and covenant with the Jewish people.

hoshea-(the-prophet).bio: Hosea was a prophet in the Northern Kingdom of Israel. He was directed by God to take an unfaithful wife and to have his life play out as a metaphor for the Israelites who would stray and worship false gods.

yehoshua-b-gamla.bio: Yehoshua b. Gamla was an influential member of the Sanhedrin during the time immediately before the Temple's destruction. He was most famous for his ordinance that all communities hire teachers to instruct children. Yet he was also accused of having bought the office of the high priest.

weather.bio: Throughout the Torah, rain serves as an indicator of God's relationship with the people of Israel: God brings rain and abundance when they observe the commandments, and God withholds when they disobey. Jewish liturgy incorporates special prayers for rain and blessings upon seeing lightning, a rainbow, or hearing thunder. Rabbinic and philosophical sources are replete with stories and discussions about the significance of rain and the laws and customs associated with asking for rain.

arvit.bio: Arvit is the evening prayer service. Its main components are Shema, blessings before and after it and the Shemoneh Esrei (standing) prayer. Though the classical sources call it optional, it has evolved in common practice and become a type of obligation for Jewish men.
