A colleague of Rabbi Akiva, he occasionally transmitted teachings in the name of Rabbi Yehoshua that Akiva disputed. Rabban Gamliel and Rabbi Shimon cited statements in his name. He is best known for his teaching in Avot: "Give to Him from His own, for you and yours are His." He practiced this with extreme generosity, giving all his money to charity. Once, after contributing his daughter's dowry to marry off orphans, he used his last remaining coin to buy grain; when a miracle filled his storehouse, he refused to benefit from it and told his daughter she would receive from the grain like one of the city's poor. His town of Birtota is unknown, possibly in the Galilee. Some say Rabbi Sh

rabbi-elazar-b-chisma.bio: Rabbi Elazar b. Chisma, also called Rabbi Elazar HaSma, was a third-generation Tanna and a prominent disciple of Rabbi Akiva. He also studied under Rabbi Yehoshua b. Hananiah and Rabban Gamliel of Yavneh.

rabbi-chutzpit-ha-meturgeman.bio: Rabbi Chutzpit ha-Meturgeman was a Tanna of the third generation in the early second century CE. He is numbered among the Asarah Harugei Malchut (the Ten Martyrs).

rabbi-b-petori.bio: Rabbi b. Petori was a Tanna of the third generation. He appears in rabbinic literature under variant spellings, including b. Petora and b. Petura.

rabbi-chelafta.bio: Rabbi Chelafta was a Tanna of the third generation who lived between the 1st and 2nd centuries CE. He resided in Tzippori, where he led the community, and was also known as Abba Chelafta. He was the father of Rabbi Yosi and the grandfather of Rabbi Yishmael b. Rabbi Yosi. The Tanna Rabbi Shimon b. Chelafta was either his son or grandson.

rabbi-chanina-b-gamliel.bio: Rabbi Chanina b. Gamliel was a Tanna of the late third and early fourth generations, the son of Rabban Gamliel of Yavneh. He is mentioned five times in the Mishnah and dozens of times in the Talmud.

rabbi-chanina-b-antigonus.bio: Rabbi Chanina b. Antigonus was a first- or second-century CE Tanna who lived around the time of the destruction of the Second Temple. A kohen, he frequently testified about various details of the Temple after its destruction. He was known for his strict observance of the laws of ritual purity and impurity, and he specialized in examining animals for blemishes. He had a son who was also a sage and an expert in the laws of purity and impurity.

aquila-the-proselyte.bio: Aquila the Proselyte was a righteous convert who converted before the Bar Kokhba revolt, during the period of the Tannaim. He authored the Greek translation of the Tanakh known as the Aquila translation, composed according to the views of Rabbi Yehoshua and Rabbi Eliezer, and was a student of Rabbi Akiva.

rabbi-hanina-ish-ono.bio: Rabbi Hanina ish Ono was a third-generation Tanna from Ono, near Lod. The Mishnah records one halakha he transmitted (Gittin 6:7) concerning a man who tells three people to give a get to his wife, which Rabbi Hanina brought from the house of imprisonment. The Tosefta also mentions him testifying before Rabban Gamliel in Yavneh that the year may be intercalated only in Judea, though if intercalated in the Galilee it is valid.

rabbi-hananya-b-teradyon.bio: Rabbi Hananya b. Teradyon was a prominent Tanna of the generation of the Bar Kokhba revolt and the subsequent period. He served as head of a rabbinic court. He was the father of Beruriah and the father-in-law of Rabbi Meir, and he is counted among the Ten Martyrs.

rabbi-yehuda-hanachtom.bio: Rabbi Yehuda HaNachtom was a Tanna of the third generation. He is not mentioned in the Mishnah; two statements of his, one concerning the boundaries of the district of those who came up from Babylonia and one concerning the laws of sacrifices, appear in the Tosefta. The Jerusalem Talmud and Midrashim indicate that he was killed during the decrees of persecution, and according to some Midrashim he was counted among the Ten Martyrs. Midrash Shir HaShirim states that he never walked four cubits without Torah.

rabbi-yehoshua-b-kapusai.bio: Rabbi Yehoshua b. Kapusai was a Tanna of the third generation and the son-in-law of Rabbi Akiva.

rabbi-hananiah-b-ahi-rabbi-yehoshua.canonical: Rabbi Hananiah b. Ahi Rabbi Yehoshua
aliases: R. Hananiah b. Ahi Rabbi Yehoshua
Rabbi Hanania b. Ahi Rabbi Yehoshua
Rabbi Hananiah ben Ahi Rabbi Yehoshua
Rabbi Hananiah b. Ahi R. Yehoshua
rabbi-hananiah-b-ahi-rabbi-yehoshua.bio: Rabbi Hananiah b. Ahi Rabbi Yehoshua was a third-generation Tanna of the early second century CE. Born in Eretz Yisrael, he later moved to Babylonia. Unlike many Tannaim, he is identified by his uncle, Rabbi Yehoshua b. Hananiah, rather than by his father's name. He is sometimes cited in baraitot simply as Hananiah, and he does not appear in the Mishnah.

aliases: Rabbi Yehuda b. Betera the Second
rabbi-yehuda-b-betera-ii.bio: Rabbi Yehuda b. Betera II was a Tanna of the third generation, living at the beginning of the second century CE. His main place of residence was Nisibis in Babylonia, and he was the grandson of Rabbi Yehuda b. Betera I.

rabbi-yochanan-b-torta.bio: Rabbi Yochanan b. Torta was a third-generation Tanna of the early 2nd century CE and a close friend of Rabbi Akiva. He is remembered for opposing Bar Kokhba, notably challenging Rabbi Akiva's messianic application of "A star shall go forth from Jacob" (Numbers 24:17) to him. Only two statements of his are preserved in rabbinic literature. Aggadic tradition associates him with the legend of a Shabbat-observant cow that inspired a gentile's conversion; some versions identify him as the convert who took the name ben Torta ("son of the cow").

yochai.bio: Yochai was a Jewish figure in the second century CE, during the Tannaic period. He belonged to the third generation of Tannaim, though he was not known as a sage himself. He appears to have been close to the Roman government under Emperor Hadrian (117–138 CE), though the precise nature of his relationship with the Romans remains unclear. Yochai is remembered historically chiefly as the father of Rabbi Shimon b. Yochai, a leading Tanna of the fourth generation.

rabbi-yeshavav-the-scribe.bio: Rabbi Yeshavav the Scribe was a Tanna of the third generation in the early second century CE. A disciple of Rabbi Yehoshua and colleague of Rabbi Akiva, he was renowned for his generosity, giving his possessions to the poor. He is numbered among the Ten Martyrs. Tradition relates that at age ninety, on the day of his execution, he exhorted his students to pursue peace and justice, and that his debate with the emperor concerning the World to Come provoked the death decree. Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi considered him among the last of the pious; he was denied burial and his body was cast to dogs, as lamented in the Tisha B'Av kinnot.

rabbi-yosei-b-dormaskit.bio: Rabbi Yosei b. Dormaskit was a Tanna of the third generation of Mishnaic sages. A student of Rabbi Eliezer b. Hyrcanus and a disputant of Rabbi Yehuda, he appears in the Talmud solely as an aggadist. His only halakhic statement, addressing whether the prohibition of orlah applies outside the Land of Israel, is transmitted in the name of his teacher Rabbi Eliezer the Great.

rabbi-yosei-b-kisma.bio: Rabbi Yosei b. Kisma was a Tanna of the third generation. The Gemara records a sharp dispute between Rabbi Elazar and Rabbi Yosei in a Tiberias synagogue that tore a Torah scroll; Rabbi Yosei b. Kisma predicted the synagogue would become a house of idolatry, which later occurred. His views on Roman decrees were more moderate than those of other sages. He disputed with Rabbi Hanina b. Teradion over public Torah study under Roman ban, and before dying asked to be buried deep to prevent grave desecration. Roman dignitaries attended his burial; upon returning they found Rabbi Hanina b. Teradion teaching Torah publicly and executed him by fire. The Gemara cites his teaching that the Son of David will come after a certain gate falls and is rebuilt repeatedly. He is traditionally buried on Mount

rabbi-matya-b-charash.bio: Rabbi Matya b. Charash was a Tanna of the third and fourth generations who lived in the 2nd century. Born in Eretz Yisrael, he later moved to Rome, where he founded a renowned yeshiva and court.

rabbi-shimon-b-nanas.bio: Rabbi Shimon b. Nanas, also known simply as ben Nanas, was a Tanna of the third generation.

rabbi-nehemiah-ish-shichin.bio: Rabbi Nehemiah ish Shichin was a third-generation Tanna who lived in the Lower Galilee town of Shichin. He appears in the Jerusalem Talmud and Midrashim, where he is depicted in connection with Rabbi Akiva, once testifying before him about the laws of sotah. He was known for his generosity; the Talmud relates that he tried to aid a refugee from Jerusalem after the Great Revolt but accidentally caused the man's death by overfeeding him. A homily of his on creation appears in Genesis Rabbah, linking heaven, earth, and sea to three-day periods and three offspring each. The site of Shichin, noted for its pottery industry and proximity to Tzippori, is identified by archaeologists at a ruin north of Tzippori, where remains of a synagogue dated to his era have been found.

rabbi-abba-gurion-of-sidon.bio: Rabbi Abba Gurion of Sidon was a Tanna of the fourth or fifth generation, active around the 2nd century CE. A few aggadic statements are preserved from him, which he transmitted in the name of Abba Shaul and Rabban Gamliel.

rabbi-aha.bio: Rabbi Aha was a Tanna who lived at the end of the 2nd century, in the fourth and fifth generations of Tannaim. He is not mentioned in the Mishnah, but appears in the Talmud dozens of times for his statements in baraitot. He transmitted laws in the name of Rabbi Yehuda and of Rabbi Achi b. Chanina (with a variant reading: Rabbi Yaakov b. Rabbi Chanina). In several places he disagrees with his contemporaries Rabbi Meir and Rabbi Shimon.

rabbi-chidka.bio: Rabbi Chidka was a Tanna of the fourth generation, likely among the students of Rabbi Akiva. Some manuscripts give his name as Chidkai, while the Jerusalem Talmud refers to him as Hundeks.

rabbi-hanina-b-akavya.bio: Rabbi Hanina b. Akavya was a Tanna of the fourth generation. He preserved ancient halachic traditions and transmitted them to his fellow Tannaim, including a version of a dispute between Rabban Gamliel and the Sages, and a received tradition concerning laws of impurity and purity. Rav Yehuda said in the name of Rav that one should never absent oneself from the study hall, noting that Rabbi Hanina b. Akavya revealed the reason for a mishnah studied there for many years; Heiman interpreted that Rav was present on that occasion. He also transmitted a tradition that the prohibition on moving purification waters on a boat applied only in the Jordan and only on a boat, corresponding to the incident in question.

rabbi-eliezer-b-rabbi-yosei-hagelili.canonical: Rabbi Eliezer b. Rabbi Yosei HaGelili
rabbi-eliezer-b-rabbi-yosei-hagelili.canonicalHe: רבי אליעזר בן רבי יוסי הגלילי
aliases: Rabbi Eliezer ben Rabbi Yosei HaGelili
R. Eliezer b. Rabbi Yosei HaGelili
Rabbi Eliezer b. R. Yosei HaGelili
Rabbi Eliezer ben Rabbi Yossi HaGelili
Rabbi Eliezer b. Rabbi Yosei ha-Galili
rabbi-eliezer-b-rabbi-yosei-hagelili.bio: Rabbi Eliezer b. Rabbi Yosei HaGelili was a fourth-generation Tanna of the second century CE, after the Bar Kokhba revolt. A sage of Usha and student of Rabbi Akiva, he appears once in the Mishnah but often in beraitot in both Talmuds and midrashim, mostly in aggadah rather than halakhah. He is credited with the beraita of the thirty-two rules of aggadic interpretation, parallel to Rabbi Yishmael's thirteen halakhic rules. He taught that a mitzvah begun by one and completed by another is credited to the one who completes it. The sages, citing Rabbi Yochanan in the name of Rabbi Elazar b. Rabbi Shimon, urged careful attention to his aggadic teachings. The Jerusalem Talmud links him to the family of Bnei Senaah of the tribe of Benjamin, though this may be a corrupted reading. He was buried n

rabbi-yehoshua-b-korcha.bio: Rabbi Yehoshua b. Korcha was a Tanna of the fourth generation. He was a colleague of Rabbi Meir, Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel, and the students of Rabbi Akiva. Some identify him as the son of Rabbi Akiva, who was bald.

rabbi-yehoshua-ha-garsi.bio: Rabbi Yehoshua ha-Garsi was a Tanna of the fourth generation. He appears in sources primarily as the attendant of Rabbi Akiva during Rabbi Akiva's imprisonment in a Roman prison for teaching Torah.

rabbi-yochanan-b-beroka.bio: Rabbi Yochanan b. Beroka was a third-generation Tanna and student of Rabbi Yehoshua b. Hananiah, and a colleague of Rabbi Elazar b. Hisma. Several laws in the Mishnah and many baraitot are cited in his name, often together with Rabbi Meir, Rabbi Yehuda, and Rabbi Shimon. His statement in Avot concerns desecration of God's name. He innovated a ruling that one may redirect an inheritance among heirs of the same gender category fit to inherit, and the halakha follows his opinion. He also ruled that a man must allot provisions for his daughters after his death. Distinctively, he held that the commandment of procreation applies to women as well as men, based on the word "them" in the biblical verse. His son, Rabbi Yishmael, was also a sage whose teachings appear in Avot and in baraitot.

rabbi-yosei-ben-ha-chotef-efrati.bio: Rabbi Yosei ben ha-Chotef Efrati was an Eretz Yisraeli tanna of the fourth generation, mentioned once in the Mishnah in tractate Kilayim. The Jerusalem Talmud records that he studied under Rabbi Yishmael at the end of Rabbi Yishmael's life. His father's epithet ha-Chotef ("the snatcher") is explained in a responsum of Rabbi Menachem Azariah of Fano as denoting one who eagerly seized mitzvot in the marketplace; others speculate it referred to a particular incident or to hasty speech. The Jerusalem Talmud also cites a "Yosef Efrati" who served Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi, apparently the same figure.

rabbi-yaakov-b-karshi.bio: Rabbi Yaakov b. Karshi was a Tanna of the fourth generation. He is usually cited as Rabbi Yaakov, and only occasionally as Rabbi Yaakov b. Karshi (or, in another version, b. Kadushai or b. Kodshi). He was one of the teachers of Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi, the compiler of the Mishnah.

rabbi-monbaz.bio: Rabbi Monbaz was a Tanna who apparently lived in the fourth generation of the Tannaim. He is mentioned as having discussed Torah before Rabbi Akiva, and is therefore considered his student. Some hold that he was a descendant of King Monbaz.

rabbi-pinchas-b-yair-2.bio: Rabbi Pinchas b. Yair was a Tanna of the fifth generation.

rabbi-shimon-shezuri.bio: Rabbi Shimon Shezuri was a Tanna of the fourth generation. He was called Shezuri after his craft of rope weaving or after the village of Shezur where he lived. His teachings appear throughout the Mishnah and Talmud. The Amoraim disagreed as to whether the halakha follows him universally, only in the Mishnah, or even in baraitot; others, such as Rabbi Yonatan, held it follows him only in two specific cases: the writing of a get for a dangerously ill person and terumat maaser of demai, a view adopted by the Rishonim. The Shakh, however, noted inconsistencies in the Rishonim's application of this rule.

rabbi-shimon-b-akshia.bio: Rabbi Shimon b. Akshia was a Tanna whose era is unknown; some speculate that he lived in the time of the Tanna b. Azzai. He appears only once in the Mishnah, where his teaching distinguishes between elders who engage in Torah and those who do not.

rabbi-hanina-b-rabbi-yosei-hagelili.canonical: Rabbi Hanina b. Rabbi Yosei HaGelili
rabbi-hanina-b-rabbi-yosei-hagelili.canonicalHe: רבי חנינא בן רבי יוסי הגלילי
aliases: R. Hanina b. Rabbi Yosei HaGelili
Rabbi Hanina ben Rabbi Yosei HaGelili
Rabbi Hanina b. R. Yosei HaGelili
rabbi-hanina-b-rabbi-yosei-hagelili.bio: Rabbi Hanina b. Rabbi Yosei HaGelili was a Tanna of the fourth generation who lived in the second century, in the generation following the Bar Kokhba revolt. He was among the sages of Usha and the son of Rabbi Yosei HaGelili. He appears in only two passages in the Gemara: once in a dispute concerning the sanctification of the new moon, and once in a dispute regarding his kids and chickens that he found slaughtered. According to tradition, he is buried in Dalton in the Galilee, near the graves of his father and his brothers Rabbi Eliezer b. Rabbi Yosei HaGelili and Shmuel b. Rabbi Yosei HaGelili.

rabbi-elazar-b-rabbi-shimon.bio: Rabbi Elazar b. Rabbi Shimon was a fifth-generation Tanna, the son of Rabbi Shimon bar Yohai and son-in-law of Rabbi Shimon ben Yosei. A contemporary and friend of Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi, he was pursued by Roman authorities in his youth but later served them as an official responsible for public order and security. Legends describe his extraordinary physical stature. In his final years he suffered severe afflictions, and various traditions surround the circumstances of his death.

rabbi-eliezer-hakappar.bio: Rabbi Eliezer HaKappar was a Tanna of the fifth and final generation, active in the latter half of the 2nd century CE. He was close to Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi, the redactor of the Mishnah. Scholars dispute his identity: some identify him as b. Kappara, while others identify him as the father of b. Kappara. He is one of the few sages of the rabbinic period for whom direct archaeological evidence has been found.

rabbi-achi-b-rabbi-yoshiah.bio: Rabbi Achi b. Rabbi Yoshiah was a Babylonian Tanna of the fifth generation who lived at the end of the second century CE. He is cited in baraitot appearing in both Talmuds, as well as in Avot de-Rabbi Natan and the halakhic midrashim.

rabbi-issi-b-yehuda.bio: Rabbi Issi b. Yehuda was a Tanna of the fifth generation (late second to early third century CE). Originally from Hutzal in Babylonia, he immigrated to Eretz Yisrael, where he studied under Rabbi Elazar b. Shammua. Because of his Babylonian origin he was also known as Yosef ha-Bavli, Issi ish Hutzal, and Issi bar Natan. He is known for his view that the commandment of rising before the elderly applies to every old person, not only to a Torah scholar, extending even to a wicked elder.

rabbi-abba-cohen-bardela.bio: Rabbi Abba Cohen Bardela (also called Rabbi Abba Cohen bar Delia) was a Tanna of the late Tannaic period, living in the 2nd and 3rd centuries.

rabbi-zutra.bio: Rabbi Zutra was a Babylonian Tanna of the fifth generation. He appears once in the Talmud, in a baraita in Berakhot 13b, where he disagrees with Rabbi Yoshiyah. Some scholars have suggested that he is not an independent Tanna, since Zutra is a Babylonian name, but rather a nickname for Rabbi Yehuda Nesiah, who was called "Zutra" (the lesser) in relation to his grandfather Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi. According to some versions in Berakhot 43b, he transmits a teaching in the name of Rabbi Shimon b. Yochai.

rabbi-huna-kama.bio: Rabbi Huna Kama, also called Rav Huna the First, served as Exilarch in Babylonia during the era of Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi in Eretz Yisrael, in the fifth generation of the Tannaim. A descendant of the royal house of King David, he is distinguished from the famous second-generation Babylonian Amora Rav Huna.

rabbi-yosei-b-zimra.bio: Rabbi Yosei b. Zimra was a Tanna and a priest who lived during the transitional period between the Tannaim and the Amoraim.

rabbi-yehuda-b-gerim.bio: Rabbi Yehuda b. Gerim was a tanna of the fifth generation and a disciple of Rabbi Shimon b. Yochai. The Babylonian Talmud relates that he publicized his teacher's critical remarks about the Romans, inadvertently bringing about Rabbi Shimon b. Yochai's persecution by the authorities.

rabbi-yedaiah-ha-bavli.bio: Rabbi Yedaiah ha-Bavli was a fifth-generation Tanna and student of Rabbi Meir. He appears once in the Mishnah, transmitting a ruling in Rabbi Meir's name concerning a guardian's liability: an attack by two dogs from a single direction does not constitute ones, but from two directions it does. His name indicates Babylonian origins; scholars generally assume he was born there and immigrated to Eretz Yisrael to study under Rabbi Meir, though some suggest he may have studied with Rabbi Meir when the latter fled to Babylonia at the end of his life.

rabbi-yosei-b-rabbi-yehuda.bio: Rabbi Yosei b. Rabbi Yehuda was a Tanna of the final generation of Tannaim. The son of Rabbi Yehuda—one of the most frequently cited Tannaim in the Mishnah—and the grandson of Rabbi Elai, he lived in the generation of Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi. He learned most of his Torah from his father, though the Talmud records him arguing with Rabbi Yehuda in halakhic discussions. He frequently disputes the older generation, including his father's colleagues and students of Rabbi Akiva such as Rabbi Elazar ben Shammua and Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai, as well as his contemporary Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi.

rabbi-yosei-b-yehuda-ish-kfar-habavli.canonical: Rabbi Yosei b. Yehuda ish Kfar haBavli
rabbi-yosei-b-yehuda-ish-kfar-habavli.canonicalHe: רבי יוסי בר יהודה איש כפר הבבלי
aliases: R. Yosei b. Yehuda ish Kfar haBavli
Rabbi Yose b. Yehuda ish Kfar haBavli
Rabbi Yosei ben Yehuda ish Kfar haBavli
Rabbi Yosei b. Yehuda of Kfar haBavli
Rabbi Yosei b. Judah ish Kfar haBavli
rabbi-yosei-b-yehuda-ish-kfar-habavli.bio: Rabbi Yosei b. Yehuda ish Kfar haBavli was a sage of the fifth generation of Tannaim. He is mentioned once in the Mishnah, in tractate Avot.

rabbi-yehudah-hanasi.bio: Rabbi Yehudah HaNasi, known as Rabbeinu HaKadosh and by the shortened name Rabbi, was a fifth-generation Tanna, Nasi of the Sanhedrin, and redactor of the Mishnah. A descendant of Hillel the Elder, he is regarded as the last of the Tannaim. Born between 135 and 137 CE during Hadrian's persecutions following the Bar Kokhba revolt, he studied under Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai, Rabbi Yehudah bar Ilai, Rabbi Elazar ben Shammua, and his father Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel. He lived in Tiberias and Tzippori, where he died around 220 CE, and was buried in Beit She'arim.

rabbi-plimo.bio: Rabbi Plimo was a Tanna of the fifth generation mentioned several times in baraitot in the Babylonian Talmud. Some scholars suggest that the name Plimo is a variant of the Hebrew words ploni or palmoni, meaning so-and-so, and that it does not refer to a specific individual.

rabbi-hama-bar-bisa.bio: Rabbi Hama bar Bisa was a sage of the transitional generation between the Tannaim and Amoraim. He was the father of Rabbi Oshaya.

rabbi-menachem-b-yosei.bio: Rabbi Menachem b. Yosei was a fifth-generation Tanna, one of five sons of Rabbi Yosei and brother of Rabbi Yishmael b. Rabbi Yosei. He appears only sparingly in baraitot. He is also called Rabbi Menachem-Verdimos. According to Aharon Heiman, he is the unnamed Rabbi Menachem cited in the Mishnah in tractate Yoma.

rabbi-yishmael-b-rabbi-yosei.bio: Rabbi Yishmael b. Rabbi Yosei was a fifth-generation Tanna. The son and preeminent student of Rabbi Yosei, he also studied under Rabbi Yehuda, and in his later years learned Torah from Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi and ate at his table. He was a colleague of Rabbi Hiyya and the teacher of Rabbi Hanina b. Hama. He had four brothers: Rabbi Eliezer, Rabbi Halafta, Rabbi Avtilus, and Rabbi Menachem. The Talmud records that when Avdan insulted him in Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi's study hall, he was severely punished from heaven. Rabbi Yishmael was forced by the king to serve as an officer catching thieves; Elijah the Prophet rebuked him for this and instructed him to flee to another city. A Midrash Tanchuma relates that he was given the first shearing, though because his father declared he was not a kohen, some

rabbi-efes.bio: Rabbi Efes was a first-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora (circa 225 CE). He served on the court of Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi, who appointed him to write a letter to Antoninus; some conclude he was the Nasi's scribe. He lived in the south of the Land of Israel and is referred to in the Jerusalem Talmud as 'our rabbis in the south.' Though Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi had designated Rabbi Chanina bar Chama as his successor, Chanina refused to assume leadership while Efes lived, since Efes was two and a half years his senior. Consequently, Rabbi Efes headed the yeshiva for at least twenty years until his death, after which Rabbi Chanina bar Chama succeeded him. Rabbi Oshaya mentions him frequently, and Levi bar Sisi was among his students.

rabbi-zakkai-of-kabul.bio: Rabbi Zakkai of Kabul was a first-generation Eretz-Yisraeli Amora active in Kabul in the Western Galilee during the 3rd century. The Jerusalem Talmud transmits a halakha in his name regarding the correction of errors in Torah reading in synagogue, which Rabbi Yirmeya and Rabbi Zeira discussed in connection with an incident in the synagogue of Sikhnin. Several baraitot mention a visit by Yehuda and Hillel, sons of Rabban Gamliel, to him, and relate that the people of Kabul honored them with food, drink, and the local bathhouse. Vayikra Rabbah cites him as an aggadist who expounded a verse from Ecclesiastes concerning a wedding feast in his city where a dignitary's son died of a snakebite while bringing wine to the guests. A cave near Kabul bears his name.

rabbi-yehuda-b-kenusa.bio: Rabbi Yehuda b. Kenusa was an Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the transitional generation from the Tannaim to the Amoraim, and of the first generation of Eretz Yisraeli Amoraim. He was a student of Rabbi Chiya. Rabbi Rafael Natan Neta Rabinovitz, followed by Rabbi Aharon Heiman, identified him with the Tanna Rabbi Yehuda b. Nekosa.

rabbi-yosei-b-shaul.bio: Rabbi Yosei b. Shaul was a first-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora.

rabbi-yonatan-b-elazar.bio: Rabbi Yonatan b. Elazar was a first-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora primarily known as an aggadist and authority in aggadah, honored with the title "Sar HaBirah." His principal teacher was Rabbi Shimon bar Yosei bar Lakunya, and he may have also studied under Rabbi Hiyya. He was a colleague of Rabbi Chanina bar Chama and Rabbi Yehoshua b. Levi, though younger than them, and a friend of Rabbi Yochanan, who was younger than he. His student Rabbi Shmuel bar Nachmani was likewise an aggadist. While the Jerusalem Talmud cites him simply as Rabbi Yonatan, the Babylonian Talmud adds his patronymic. Aharon Heiman suggests he was from Tiberias, whereas Yaakov Shmuel Zur identifies him with Rabbi Yonatan of Beit Guvrin, noting that his parables often draw on bathhouse imagery fitting that city's fam

rabbi-hezekiah-b-r-hiyya.bio: Rabbi Hezekiah b. R. Hiyya was an Amora of the transitional generation between the Tannaim and the Amoraim. He was the son of Rabbi Hiyya and Yehudit, and the twin brother of Judah.

rabbi-aftoriki.bio: Rabbi Aftoriki is an Amora mentioned only a few times in the Babylonian Talmud, and precise biographical details about him are difficult to ascertain. He appears to have been one of the Eretz Yisrael Amoraim rather than a Babylonian Amora, as he is cited in the Talmud with the title "Rabbi," customary for sages of Eretz Yisrael, rather than "Rav," the title used by Babylonian Amoraim.

rabbi-hama-bar-hanina.bio: Rabbi Hama bar Hanina was a first-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora. His father was likely Rabbi Hanina bar Hama, a student of Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi.

rabbi-yitzchak-of-magdala.bio: Rabbi Yitzchak of Magdala was a first-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora, also known as Magdalia or Magdalim. He interpreted the Mishnah in Bava Metzia regarding coins stacked like towers, which Maharatz Chayes suggested as the origin of his name, though Aharon Heiman considered this uncertain. In Niddah, conflicting traditions place him as either the teacher or student of Rabbi Shabbtai, and additional statements are cited in his name. Rabbi Simlai transmitted teachings in his name. He interpreted "lulvei gafanim" in Yoma as referring to vines that budded between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. Bereishit Rabbah records his teaching that pleasure exists even in flies, mosquitoes, and fleas, and his exposition of the verse about the consecrated brother's crown as "you are the chosen of your brot

rabbi-shabbetai.bio: Rabbi Shabbetai was an Amora of the first and second generations of Eretz Yisrael Amoraim. He was the teacher of Rav Assi.

rabbi-abba-bar-avina.bio: Rabbi Abba bar Avina was a second-generation Eretz-Yisrael Amora. A student of Rav, he transmitted halachic teachings in Rav's name. He served as a judge in his city. He is occasionally referred to as Rabbi Abba bar Bina or Rabbi Abba bar Mina.

rabbi-abba-bar-acha.bio: Rabbi Abba bar Acha was an Eretz Yisrael Amora who descended to Babylonia. He is frequently cited as "Rabbenu," and there is a dispute as to whether this refers to Rabbi or to Rav; accordingly, there is disagreement regarding whether he belonged to the first or second generation of Amoraim. Rabbi Elai transmits teachings in his name.

rabbi-abba-bar-zavda.bio: Rabbi Abba bar Zavda was a second- and third-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora. A student of Rav Ada bar Ahava, Rav Huna, and Rabbi Chanina, he is also known as Rabbi Abba bar Zavdi.

rabbi-pinchas-b-hama.bio: Rabbi Pinchas b. Hama was a third-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora. He appears in the Jerusalem Talmud, Babylonian Talmud, and Midrash Tehillim, though little is known of his life. The Jerusalem Talmud records that when a certain Issi offered to teach him the pronunciation of the Explicit Name, Rabbi Pinchas declined, saying he was unworthy because he ate tithes. In Midrash Tehillim he teaches that Moses established the order of prayers, while the Men of the Great Assembly re-established and fixed prayer in Israel. Seder HaDorot suggests his father may have been Rabbi Chama b. Abba and his brother Rabbi Hanina b. Chama.

aliases: Rabbi Nehunia ish Bik'at Beit Choratan
Rabbi Nehunia of the valley of Beit Choratan
rabbi-nehunia-of-havran.bio: Rabbi Nehunia of Havran was among the first generation of Amoraim in Eretz Yisrael. He is also known as Rabbi Chonia de-breit Havrin and Rabbi Nehunia ish Bik'at Beit Choratan. Some authorities regard him as a Tanna, as he lived during the transitional generation between the Tannaim and the Amoraim.

rabbi-abba-d-min-yaffo.bio: Rabbi Abba d'min Yaffo, also known as Rabbi Ada d'min Yaffo, was a third-generation Eretz-Yisraeli Amora who lived in Jaffa. He is best known for ruling that the names of Haman's sons must be read in a single breath during the Purim reading of Megillat Esther. The Jerusalem Talmud mentions his son Rabbi Chiyya, while a Midrash records that Rabbi Chiyya was his grandson.

rabbi-avitar.bio: Rabbi Avitar was a second-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora.

rabbi-avdimi-of-haifa.bio: Rabbi Avdimi of Haifa was an Eretz Yisrael Amora active during the second and third generations of Amoraim in Eretz Yisrael. Rabbinic sources cite him under variant names, including Rav Dimi of Haifa and Rabbi Avdia of Haifa.

rabbi-ilfa.bio: Rabbi Ilfa was a second-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora and a colleague of Rabbi Yochanan.

rabbi-oshaya-2.bio: Rabbi Oshaya (also Hoshaya) was an Amora of the second and third generations. Born in Babylonia, he moved to Eretz Yisrael in his youth. He was the brother of the Amora Rav Chanina.

rabbi-hanina-turtaya.bio: Rabbi Hanina Turtaya was a second-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora.

rabban-gamliel-iv.bio: Rabban Gamliel IV served as Nasi of the Sanhedrin among the second generation of Eretz Yisrael Amoraim. The son of Rabbi Yehuda Nesia and great-grandson of Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi, redactor of the Mishnah, he is almost unattested in the Babylonian Talmud. The Jerusalem Talmud relates that he tried to enforce the law of demai in Syria but was blocked by Rabbi Hoshaya. His rare appearances in the Talmuds relative to his father and son indicate that his presidency was short. His son, Rabbi Yehuda Nesia II, a disciple of Rabbi Yochanan, succeeded him.

rabbi-zeiri.bio: Rabbi Zeiri was a second-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora and one of the foremost students of Rabbi Chanina and Rabbi Yochanan. His statements and legal rulings are cited dozens of times throughout the Talmud.

rabbi-alexandri.bio: Rabbi Alexandri was an Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the second and third generations. A student of Rabbi Yohanan and Rabbi Yehoshua b. Levi, he was a renowned darshan, and all his statements in the Talmud are aggadic. Many aggadic teachings are cited in his name, most famously his practice of calling out, "Who wants to live?" and then showing the gathered crowd the verse, "Who is the man who desires life... guard your tongue from evil."

rabbi-hanina.bio: Rabbi Hanina was a second- and third-generation Amora born in Babylonia who moved to Eretz Yisrael in his youth. A student of Rabbi Yannai and Rabbi Yohanan, he was the brother of the famous Amora Rav Oshaya. Some scholars speculate that he is identical with Rabbi Hanina bar Hama, noting that his brother is referred to as Rabbi Oshaya bar Rabbi Hama.

rabbi-yehuda-nesiah-ii.bio: Rabbi Yehuda Nesiah II was the son of Rabban Gamliel IV and served as Nasi of the Sanhedrin in the second or third generation of Eretz Yisrael Amoraim. According to his lineage, he was the tenth generation from Hillel the Elder.

rabbi-yehudah-bar-peda.bio: Rabbi Yehudah bar Peda was a first-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora and colleague of Rabbi Yochanan. He was a nephew of bar Kapara. The Talmud often calls him simply bar Peda, whereas the Midrash knows him as Yehudah bar Pedaya. Among the earliest Amoraim, he is mentioned in the Talmud as engaging in halakhic discussion with Ilfa, who was older than Rabbi Yochanan.

rabbi-yehuda-b-nachmani.bio: Rabbi Yehuda b. Nachmani, also known as Rabbi Yehuda b. Nachman and Rabbi Yudan b. Nechemya, was an Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the second and third generations, active during the third and fourth centuries. His primary place of residence was Tiberias.

rabbi-yochanan-dekatzion.bio: Rabbi Yochanan deKatzion, also called deKartzion, was an Eretz Yisraeli Amora whose exact period is unknown, though he likely belonged to the second generation of Amoraim. Shlomo Sirilio, an early commentator on the Jerusalem Talmud, proposed that his name should be read as Rabbi Yochanan deKisaryon, indicating an origin in Caesarea.

rabbi-yehoshua-onya.bio: Rabbi Yehoshua Onya was an Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the second or third generation. His name indicates that he came from or resided in Ono in Judea. He is known for several baraitot transmitted in the Jerusalem Talmud. One baraita addresses the calculation of orlah years from the time of planting rather than regrafting. Another deals with liability for lashes for possessing even a small amount of chametz on Passover. He also transmitted an account of a violent clash between the students of Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel, in which the former attacked the latter with swords and spears over halakhic disputes.

rabbi-yosef-b-rabbi-yehoshua-b-levi.canonical: Rabbi Yosef b. Rabbi Yehoshua b. Levi
rabbi-yosef-b-rabbi-yehoshua-b-levi.canonicalHe: רב יוסף בריה דרבי יהושע בן לוי
aliases: R. Yosef b. R. Yehoshua b. Levi
Rabbi Yosef son of Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi
Rabbi Yosef son of R. Yehoshua b. Levi
Rabbi Yosef b. R. Yehoshua ben Levi
Rabbi Yosef b. Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi
rabbi-yosef-b-rabbi-yehoshua-b-levi.bio: Rabbi Yosef b. Rabbi Yehoshua b. Levi was a second-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora and the son of the renowned first-generation Amora Rabbi Yehoshua b. Levi. His father served as his teacher; the Talmud in tractate Chullin records that he asked his father a question concerning the laws of tereifot. His mother was likely the daughter of Rabbi Yosei b. Petras. He was known by the epithet "ben Ari" (son of a lion) in honor of his father, as cited in tractate Yoma, where Zeira b. Hama addressed him with this title while recounting his father's remarkable deeds.

rabbi-yissachar-of-kfar-mandi.bio: Rabbi Yissachar of Kfar Mandi, also known simply as Rabbi Yissachar, was an Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the second generation. A noted darshan, his sermons are cited in the Jerusalem Talmud and many midrashim. He was a contemporary and possibly a halakhic disputant of Rabbi Hama b. Rabbi Hanina II or Rabbi Hama b. Hanina I. He came from the ancient village of Kfar Mandi in the Lower Galilee. He is the only sage in the rabbinic period known by this name, aside from the disparaged Yissachar of Kfar Barkai.

rabbi-menahem.bio: Rabbi Menahem (also known simply as Menahem) was an Eretz Yisrael Amora of the second-third generation.

rabbi-shimon-b-pazzi-2.bio: Rabbi Shimon b. Pazzi was a second-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora. A student of Rabbi Yochanan and Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi, he was a colleague of Rabbi Elazar and Rabbi Zeira. He appears primarily in the Jerusalem Talmud and occasionally in the Babylonian Talmud. Tractate Shabbat relates that while he sat with Rabbi Elazar, Rabbi Yaakov bar Acha passed by; one suggested they stand because Rabbi Yaakov bar Acha was God-fearing, while the other cited his Torah scholarship, prompting a discussion about whether the latter description understated his piety. His son was Rabbi Yehuda bar Shimon, and his student was Rabbi Hilkiya.

rabbi-shimon-bar-abba.bio: Rabbi Shimon bar Abba was an Eretz Yisrael Amora and a student-colleague of Rabbi Yochanan. The Jerusalem Talmud describes him as meticulous in the observance of commandments and an expert in appraising diamonds, yet he remained poor and was known as a priest. A wealthy landowner named Eliposa once sought to give him tithes, but Rabbi Shimon hesitated to accept them because he doubted the man's reliability in matters of terumot and maasrot; he agreed only after Rabbi Yochanan affirmed Eliposa's trustworthiness. He may have been a relative or brother of Rabbi Chiyya bar Abba. Impoverished, he eventually left the Land of Israel to settle in Damascus, despite Rabbi Chiyya's unsuccessful attempt to dissuade him with a poignant appeal about the loss of a precious plant from the land.

rabbi-mani-bar-patish.bio: Rabbi Mani bar Patish was a second-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora and a student of Rabbi Yannai. According to Rabbi Aharon Heiman, he was the leading figure of his generation in the time of Rabbi Yochanan. In Ashkenazic tradition his name is voweled Mani (like Moni), but it is not related to the foreign name for monkey; therefore it is written in full spelling with a vav according to halachic rules.

rabbi-tanhum.bio: Rabbi Tanhum was a second-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora and a student of Rabbi Yehoshua b. Levi. Some identify him as Rabbi Tanhum b. Hanilai.

rabbi-shimon-chasida.bio: Rabbi Shimon Chasida was a second-generation Amora. His statements were transmitted by both Babylonian and Eretz Yisrael sages, leading some to conclude that his origin cannot be determined, while others held that he lived in Eretz Yisrael. His primary student who reported teachings in his name was Rav Chana bar Bizna.

rabbi-shimon-b-lakish-2.bio: Rabbi Shimon b. Lakish, known as Reish Lakish or Bar Lakish, was a leading second-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora. He was active in Tiberias during the second half of the third century CE. He was a student-colleague and central disputant (bar plugta) of his brother-in-law, Rabbi Yochanan.

rabbi-shmuel-bar-nachmani.bio: Rabbi Shmuel bar Nachmani was an Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the second and third generations, active in the 3rd and 4th centuries, who resided primarily in Lod. A kohen, he was among the greatest Jewish aggadists. He studied under Rabbi Yonatan ben Elazar and Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi, and also saw the Tanna Rabbi Shimon ben Elazar; his colleague was Rabbi Yaakov bar Idi. Though he engaged extensively in halakha and was considered a leading authority of his generation, he was especially renowned for aggadah, earning the title "ba'al aggadah." Many of his aggadic teachings were quoted by Rabbi Chelbo. His sons included the Amora Rabbi Hillel ben Rabbi Shmuel bar Nachmani and Nachman.

rabbi-shimon-b-elikim.bio: Rabbi Shimon b. Elikim was a second- to third-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora who studied at the Tiberias yeshiva. His precise standing is unclear: sources variously place him as a colleague or student of Rabbi Yochanan, or as a student of Rabbi Yochanan's students. Tractate Sanhedrin records that he worked to ordain Rabbi Yosi b. Rabbi Chanina, while Resh Lakish treats him as a student; the Jerusalem Talmud reverses this account, a version preferred by the author of Erech Tannaim veAmoraim. He appears in a halachic dispute in Chullin alongside Rabbi Yochanan and his circle. He debated halacha with Rabbi Elazar b. Pedat, was also his student, and many laws are cited in his name. He and Rabbi Elazar were uniquely known among Rabbi Yochanan's students for adjudicating cases of indirect damage

rabbi-abba-b-pappa.bio: Rabbi Abba b. Pappa was a third-generation Amora. He is described as a Babylonian Amora who later became an Eretz Yisraeli Amora.

rabbi-abba-of-akko.bio: Rabbi Abba of Akko, sometimes called Rabbi Abba d'Akko, was a third-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora. He lived in the cities of Akko and Caesarea.

rabbi-tanchum-bar-chanilai.bio: Rabbi Tanchum bar Chanilai was a second-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the early third century. Dozens of his statements appear in the Gemara. His primary teacher was Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi, an expert in aggadah, and he himself is portrayed as deeply occupied with aggadic matters. While he generally cited his teacher, he also reported teachings from other Amoraim. When Rabbi Chanina ben Agil asked why the word "good" is mentioned only in the second Tablets, he replied that he had not heard an answer from Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi, but Shmuel bar Nachum explained that it was because the first Tablets were eventually broken: "Heaven forbid that goodness should cease from Israel."

rabbi-abba-bar-kuryah.bio: Rabbi Abba bar Kuryah was a third-generation Amora. He debated with Rabbi Chiyya bar Abba.

rabbi-abba-of-kartigna.bio: Rabbi Abba of Kartigna was a third-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora from the city of Kartignin. He is known in Hebrew sources by variant spellings, including Kartigna and Kartinia.

rabbi-aha-b-hanina.bio: Rabbi Aha b. Hanina was a third-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the fourth century. He appears frequently in the Babylonian Talmud and occasionally in the Jerusalem Talmud.

rabbi-aibu.bio: Rabbi Aibu was a third-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora. He appears frequently in the Gemara and Midrashim, transmitting teachings of earlier sages. According to Toledot Tanna'im veAmora'im, he received traditions from the greatest Amoraim of the first and second generations and is found alongside the leading preachers of his era; many of his homilies are preserved in the Midrashim. Later Amoraim also cite rulings and statements in his name.

rabbi-ami.bio: Rabbi Ami b. Nathan, known as Rabbi Ami, was a third-generation Eretz-Yisraeli Amora. A student of Rabbi Yochanan, he was a colleague and bar plugta of Rabbi Assi. The Jerusalem Talmud occasionally calls him Rabbi Imi.

rabbi-assi-2.bio: Rabbi Assi was a third-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora. A student of Rabbi Yochanan, he was a colleague and frequent disputant of Rabbi Ami. He resided primarily in Tiberias.

rabbi-ila.bio: Rabbi Ila was a third-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora and among the younger students of Rabbi Yochanan. He is also known by several alternate spellings of his name, including Rabbi Elai, Rabbi Leia, and Rabbi Hila.

rabbi-binyamin-b-yefet.bio: Rabbi Binyamin b. Yefet was a third-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora (late 3rd century). He studied under Rabbi Yochanan and was also a student of Rabbi Elazar b. Pedat.

rabbi-ami-of-vardina.bio: Rabbi Ami of Vardina was a third-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora. Also known as Shafir Naah, he transmits teachings in the name of Rabbi Abbahu. He served as a sage in the house of the Nasi, where he was asked to rule on matters of forbidden and permitted and to inspect blemished firstborn animals. On Yom Tov, however, he would not inspect them.

rabbi-hama-b-ukva.bio: Rabbi Hama b. Ukva was a third-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora.

rabbi-hanina-b-agil.bio: Rabbi Hanina b. Agil, also known as Agol, was an Eretz Yisrael Amora of the third and fourth generations. He is mentioned only a few times in the Talmudim and Midrashim.

rabbi-hanina-bar-papi.bio: Rabbi Hanina bar Papi was an Amora of the third generation in Eretz Yisrael.

rav-chaga.bio: Rav Chaga was a third-generation Amora active in both Babylonia and Eretz Yisrael. The Jerusalem Talmud refers to him as Rabbi Chaggai or Rabbi Chagai.

rabbi-yudan-b-yishmael.bio: Rabbi Yudan b. Yishmael was a third-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora. Some scholars identify him with the Yudan b. Yishmael mentioned in an Aramaic inscription from the synagogue at Chorazin, which records that he donated its columns and steps from his own funds; if so, he was likely very wealthy.

rabbi-hanina-b-pazi.bio: Rabbi Hanina b. Pazi was an Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the third or fourth generation. He is also called Rabbi Hanin or Rabbi Hanan. He appears primarily in the Midrashim, and once in the Jerusalem Talmud under the name Rabbi Hananya. He is not mentioned at all in the Babylonian Talmud. According to certain versions, he may be mentioned once more in the Jerusalem Talmud, tractate Pesachim 3:1.

rabbi-yitzchak-bar-abba.bio: Rabbi Yitzchak bar Abba was a third-generation Babylonian Amora and colleague of Rabbi Zeira and Abaye. He likely came from Eretz Yisrael, hence his title "Rabbi," arriving in Babylonia when other Eretz Yisrael Amoraim migrated there. This accords with the version of his name as "Rabbi Yitzchak bar Abba." Yet other early sources refer to him as "Rav Yitzchak bar Abba," which would indicate he was a Babylonian Amora.

rabbi-yosei-ha-glila.bio: Rabbi Yosei ha-Glila was an Eretz Yisrael Amora of the third–fourth generation. He was likely a student of Rabbi Yosei bar Hanina and a colleague of Rabbi Yonah. The Babylonian Talmud recounts that he once carried a lamp on Shabbat in the presence of Rabbi Yosei bar Hanina, relying on the view of Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai, and Rabbi Yosei bar Hanina objected to this.

rabbi-avira.bio: Rabbi Avira was an Eretz Yisrael Amora of the third and fourth generations, a student of Rabbi Yochanan and a colleague of Rabbi Abbahu. He appears in the Talmud debating halakha with Rabbi Yochanan and alongside colleagues Rabbi Abbahu and Rabbi Chelbo. He frequently transmits teachings in the name of Rabbi Ami and Rabbi Assi. He is to be distinguished from the later Babylonian Amora Rav Avira, a disputant of Ravina who transmitted statements in the name of Rava.

rabbi-pedat.bio: Rabbi Pedat was an Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the third generation, and the son of Rabbi Elazar b. Pedat.

rabbi-yitzchak-atushia.bio: Rabbi Yitzchak Atushia was an Eretz-Yisraeli Amora of the third and fourth generations (late 3rd to early 4th century). His name derives from his hometown, the village of Atush, identified with Khirbat Atusha in the Lower Galilee. He learned from Reish Lakish and was a student of Rabbi Zeira; according to one opinion, he also studied under Rabbi Yirmeyah. He appears several times in Seder Nashim, transmitting teachings in the name of Rabbi Zeira. His son, Rabbi Chiyya, was also a Torah scholar.

rabbi-perida.bio: Rabbi Perida was a third-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora.

rabbi-anani-b-sasson.bio: Rabbi Anani b. Sasson (also called Inini) was an Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the third generation. He may be identified with Rabbi Ananiel b. Sasson, an Amora in whose name a homily is cited in Shemot Rabbah (Parashat Shemot, parasha 3).

rabbi-avina.bio: Rabbi Avina was a third-generation Amora, counted among both the Babylonian and the Eretz Yisrael Amoraim.

rabbi-shila-of-kfar-tamarta.bio: Rabbi Shila of Kfar Tamarta was a third-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora who lived in Kfar Tamarta in the Lower Galilee. A student of Rabbi Yochanan, he transmitted teachings in his name. He left a son named Rabbi Aha.

rav-shmuel-b-yehuda.bio: Rav Shmuel b. Yehuda was a third-generation Babylonian Amora who later immigrated to Eretz Yisrael.

rabbi-yosei-of-yokrat.bio: Rabbi Yosei of Yokrat was a third-generation Amora and teacher of Rabbi Yosei bar Avin. His hometown, Yokrat, is unidentified today. The Talmud recounts three stories about him: two depict extreme piety bordering on harshness toward his children—he cursed his son for ripening figs out of season, and told his beautiful daughter to "return to dust" so men would not sin, prompting his student Rabbi Yosei bar Avin to leave him for Rav Ashi; the third describes a donkey he rented out returning home with its wages. Some modern scholars argue he is not a historical figure, suggesting the tales symbolize the decline of the Eretz Yisrael center.

rabbi-shmuel-bar-rav-yitzchak.bio: Rabbi Shmuel bar Rav Yitzchak was a third-generation Amora born in Babylonia who ascended to Eretz Yisrael. He was considered, together with Rabbi Zeira, the greatest sage of his generation in Eretz Yisrael, and was known primarily as an aggadist.

rabbi-abba-b-kahana.bio: Rabbi Abba b. Kahana was a third-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora known as a preacher and aggadist. He spent time in Babylonia in the study hall of Rav Chiya b. Ashi, and many believe he was born there; he may have been the son of Rav Kahana. His primary activity was in Eretz Yisrael, where he was a colleague of Rabbi Levi and Rabbi Zeira.

rabbi-tachlifa-bar-ma-arava.bio: Rabbi Tachlifa bar Ma'arava was an Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the early third generation. He is recorded in Tractate Menachot as having recited baraitot before Rabbi Yochanan, though his primary study was before Rabbi Avahu. He traveled frequently to Babylonia. He is called "bar Ma'arava" ("son of the West," meaning Eretz Yisrael) in order to distinguish him from Rav Tachlifa the Babylonian.

rabbi-pharnakh.bio: Rabbi Pharnakh was a third-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora and a student of Rabbi Yochanan.

rabbi-abba-b-zavina.bio: Rabbi Abba b. Zavina, also called Abba b. Zamina or Zimuna, was a fourth-generation Amora born circa 305 CE. A student of Rav Zeira, he transmitted many teachings in his teacher's name. He worked as a tailor in Rome in the home of a gentile who, threatening him with death, tried to entice him to eat forbidden food; when he refused, the man declared that a Jew must remain a Jew to the end. A story in Midrash Tanchuma about a Jewish tailor in Rome who bought an expensive fish for Shabbat may also refer to him.

rabbi-avimi-b-tuvi.bio: Rabbi Avimi b. Tuvi was an Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the fourth generation and a student of Rabbi Abbahu. The Jerusalem Talmud relates that a certain non-Jewish woman once came to ask Rabbi Abbahu a question, and he sent Avimi b. Tuvi to answer her.

rabbi-abba-bar-pappai.bio: Rabbi Abba bar Pappai, also known as Rabbi Ba bar Pappai, was a fourth-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora who lived and was active in the 4th century. He died in 375 CE.

rabbi-abba-oshaya-ish-turya.bio: Rabbi Abba Oshaya ish Turya was a pious man who lived in Eretz Yisrael, likely during the Amoraic period.

rabbi-avimi-son-of-rabbi-abbahu.canonical: Rabbi Avimi son of Rabbi Abbahu
aliases: R. Avimi son of Rabbi Abbahu
Rabbi Avimi, son of Rabbi Abbahu
rabbi-avimi-son-of-rabbi-abbahu.bio: Rabbi Avimi son of Rabbi Abbahu was a fourth-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora who lived around 323 CE. A wealthy merchant, he traded with Babylonians and the people of Mechoza. The Babylonian Talmud relates that he once needed to send money there, and he traveled to Babylonia with other Eretz Yisraeli Amoraim to escape religious persecution. Scholars speculate that this is why no statements in his name appear in Eretz Yisraeli sources such as the Jerusalem Talmud and other midrashim; instead, traditions record teachings he delivered upon returning from Mechoza. He was renowned for honoring his father, and his father praised him as a model of filial piety.

rabbi-yehuda-bar-titas.bio: Rabbi Yehuda bar Titas was a fourth-generation Eretz-Yisraeli Amora. He appears several times in the Jerusalem Talmud. His name is recorded in variant spellings, including Titus, Titam, and Sotes.

rav-avya.bio: Rav Avya was a fourth-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora who studied under Rabbi Ami and Rabbi Yochanan before moving to Babylonia, where he studied under Rav Huna and was among his greatest students. He regularly attended Rav Yosef's Shabbat lectures before Musaf, and received traditions from him about healing on Shabbat. The Talmud relates that he once came to Rava's house with muddy feet and placed them on a bed; Rava questioned him on muktzeh due to filth, and he answered. He married the sister of Rami b. Papa. His sons included Rav Chiya, Rav Ada, Rava, Rav Chelkiya, Rav Huna, and Rav Acha son of Rav Avya, the latter a frequent disputant of Rav Ashi. Among his colleagues were Rabbah b. Rav Chanan and Rav Achadvoy b. Ami.

rabbi-hesa-of-eshtemoa.bio: Rabbi Hesa of Eshtemoa was an Eretz Yisrael Amora active in Eshtemoa at the end of the third century or beginning of the fourth century CE. He is mentioned only once in the Jerusalem Talmud, in connection with a visit to him by Rabbi Yasa.

rabbi-yudan-b-tarfon.bio: Rabbi Yudan b. Tarfon was an Eretz Yisrael Amora of the Byzantine period, active in the 4th–5th centuries CE. Scholars generally identify him as the sage named on a tombstone discovered in the ancient Jewish cemetery of Jaffa, now displayed at the University Museum in Oslo, Norway. According to this proposal, he is one of the few sages of the rabbinic era for whom direct archaeological evidence has been found.

rabbi-yehoshua-of-sakhnin.bio: Rabbi Yehoshua of Sakhnin was a fourth-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora who lived and was active in the fourth century CE. He resided in the village of Sakhnin in the Lower Galilee, where his grave and that of his wife are identified near the grave of Rabbi Shimon of Sakhnin. Over the generations, the site served as a place of pilgrimage for Jews and Arabs of the Galilee.

rabbi-yosei-tzidonia.bio: Rabbi Yosei Tzidonia was an Eretz Yisrael Amora of the fourth generation. He was called Tzidonia because he lived in Tzidon or Tel Beit Tzaida. He was a student of Rabbi Yirmeya and reviewed baraitot before him. He transmitted oral teachings in the name of Rabbi Yochanan.

rabbi-yehuda-b-shila.bio: Rabbi Yehuda b. Shila was a fourth-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora. He transmitted teachings in the name of Rabbi Assi, a disciple of Rabbi Yochanan, including an aggadic teaching enumerating six meritorious acts whose reward persists in the World to Come. He is credited with few halakhot, notably a foundational rule regarding secondary incest prohibitions (she'niyot), which Rava subsequently limited to cases involving a single marital bond. He also transmitted rulings concerning mikvah and muktzeh in the name of Rabbi Assi citing Rabbi Yochanan. Toward the end of his life he traveled to Babylonia.

rav-huna-son-of-rav-ika.canonical: Rav Huna son of Rav Ika
aliases: Rav Huna the son of Rav Ika
rav-huna-son-of-rav-ika.bio: Rav Huna son of Rav Ika was a fourth-generation Amora active in both Eretz Yisrael and Babylonia. He studied under Rabbi Abbahu in Eretz Yisrael and was renowned for transmitting his teacher's words with extreme precision; Rav Safra therefore favored his version of a ruling in a debate with Rav Kahana, another of Rabbi Abbahu's students. He later left Eretz Yisrael for Babylonia with Rav Kahana and Rav Safra, where he discussed halakha with Abaye and Rav Papa, who occasionally stayed at his home. His brother was the prominent Amora Rav Acha son of Rav Ika, frequently cited in the Talmud, often in debate with Rav Papa. He is also known as Rav Huna bar Manoach, and his brothers included Rav Idi and Rav Nahman (or Rav Hinena) son of Rav Ika.

rabbi-yosna.bio: Rabbi Yosna was a fourth-generation Amora of Eretz Yisrael. He transmitted teachings in the name of Rabbi Ami and Rabbi Acha. Rabbi Yosei beRabbi Bun ruled on his statements.

rabbi-yaakov-bar-zavdi.bio: Rabbi Yaakov bar Zavdi was a fourth-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora. He is mentioned frequently in the Jerusalem Talmud.

rabbi-yaakov-of-kfar-nevurya.bio: Rabbi Yaakov of Kfar Nevurya was an Eretz Yisraeli sage who lived at the end of the third generation and beginning of the fourth generation of Amoraim. A student of Rabbi Haggai, he taught halakha in Tyre and Caesarea, among other places. Some of his halakhic teachings provoked sharp opposition from his teacher.

rav-yitzchak-b-elishiv.bio: Rav Yitzchak b. Elishiv was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora who lived during the time of Abaye and Rava. He may have belonged to the wealthy and respected Bar Elishiv family. He appears only rarely in the Talmud and was known as a miracle worker. The Talmud relates that his student Rabbi Mani complained that his wealthy in-laws tormented him; Rav Yitzchak caused them to become poor, and when they then pestered him for money, restored their wealth. In another story, the same student complained that his wife was ugly; Rav Yitzchak made her beautiful, but when she became haughty, returned her to her former appearance. When two other students asked to be blessed with wisdom, he replied that he had lost the power to have his prayers answered.

rabbi-hilkiah.bio: Rabbi Hilkiah was an Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the fourth generation. A student of Rabbi Simon and colleague of Rabbi Pinchas, he was known as an aggadist, and most of his statements are aggadic. He is mentioned dozens of times in the Jerusalem Talmud and aggadic midrashim, and once in the Babylonian Talmud. He is noted for a statement against Christians. A sage named Rabbi Yitzchak b. Rabbi Hilkiah mentioned in midrash may have been his son.

rabban-gamliel-v.bio: Rabban Gamliel V served as Nasi of the Sanhedrin in Eretz Yisrael in the late 4th century, following his father Hillel the Nasi, who fixed the Hebrew calendar. Menahem Stern identified him as the Patriarch who corresponded with the rhetorician Libanius during 388–393. Libanius’s letters attest to Gamliel’s Greek education, close friendship with the rhetorician, and vast authority exceeding that of the provincial governor, including involvement in the removal of Governor Hilarius. Stern further links him to the Patriarch in a letter by Jerome from the 390s concerning the execution of a Roman official who had defrauded him of imperial documents. His son, Rabbi Yehuda Nesiah III, succeeded him.

rabbi-yusta-bar-shunem.bio: Rabbi Yusta bar Shunem was a fifth-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora cited in the Jerusalem Talmud and Midrashim. He came from Shunem, at the foot of Givat ha-Moreh (near present-day Sulam). He transmitted halakhot in the name of Rabbi Yehoshua of Sikhnin and studied under Rabbi Mana, whom he quoted in halakhic discussions. In a debate over the northern border of the Land of Israel for land-dependent commandments, he held that the boundary reached the Amana mountains, citing the verse "look from the top of Amana" as proof that the exiles would sing upon arriving there.

rabbi-hillel-ii.bio: Rabbi Hillel II, also called Hillel Nesiah and Hillel the Last, served as Nasi of the Sanhedrin in the fifth generation of Eretz Yisrael Amoraim in the fourth century CE. According to tradition, he was responsible for the transition to the calculated Hebrew calendar. Scholars identify him with the Patriarch 'Julos' mentioned in a letter of Emperor Julian.

rabbi-yehuda-b-shalom.bio: Rabbi Yehuda b. Shalom, also called Yudan, was a fifth-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora of the late fourth century and a student of Rabbi Yosei. He is mentioned in a halachic dispute with Rabbi Matunya, engaged in halacha, and likely served as a judge, though most of his statements are aggadic. Shlomo Buber identified him with Rabbi Yehuda HaLevi cited frequently in Midrash Tanchuma. Yechiel Heilprin suggested he lived in the time of Rabbi Abbahu but admitted lack of evidence; Buber attributed this to a corrupted text in Sefer Yuchasin. His son may have been named Yehuda as well, based on a mention of "Rabbi Yudan b. Yudan b. Shalom," though this may be a corrupt reading of his own name.

rabbi-yona-of-bostra.bio: Rabbi Yona of Bostra was a fifth-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora from Bostra in Hauran, cited in the Jerusalem Talmud and Midrash. His teachings address various halakhic topics, including plants exempt from tithing and the laws of vows. He engaged in halakhic discourse with Rabbi Mana of the Tiberias academy, frequently posing questions to him, and also corresponded with the Babylonian Rav Huna brei d'Rav Yehoshua, who permitted him to sell his books during chol hamoed (Moed Katan). He is named for his hometown, the major city of Bostra in Hauran, which maintained a Jewish community throughout the Talmudic period.

rabbi-ravin.bio: Rabbi Ravin, also called Rabbi Avin, Rabbi Bon, or Rabbi Avon, was a prominent fourth-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora. Some hold he was born in Babylonia but ascended to Eretz Yisrael, where he studied under Rabbi Abbahu, Rabbi Zeira, and Rabbi Elai. He regularly traveled between Eretz Yisrael and Babylonia, transmitting teachings from the sages of Eretz Yisrael to those in Babylonia, often cited in the Babylonian Talmud with the phrase 'when Ravin came.' Residing in Eretz Yisrael while making fixed visits to Babylonia, he deliberately linked the two centers to unify Torah across the Diaspora. Numerous halakhic and aggadic statements appear in his name throughout the Talmud and Midrashim. His son Bar Maryon is mentioned in the Talmud; according to Aharon Heiman, he is the famous Amora Rabbi

rabbi-matanya.bio: Rabbi Matanya was an Eretz Yisrael Amora of the fifth generation. He was the son of the Amora Rabbi Yudan and a colleague of Rabbi Abba Mari, Rabbi Elazar b. Rabbi Yossi, and Rabbi Shmuel b. Rabbi Yossi b. Rabbi Bun.

rav-eliezer-zeira.bio: Rav Eliezer Zeira, also called Elazar, was a first-generation Babylonian Amora of the generation of Shmuel. He is described as an "adam gadol" (a great man), and appears to have resided in Nehardea.

rabbi-shimon-b-karsana.bio: Rabbi Shimon b. Karsana was an Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the fifth generation. He appears only in the Jerusalem Talmud. In several places he is called Rabbi Shimon b. Barsana.

rabbi-yosei-berabbi-bun.bio: Rabbi Yosei beRabbi Bun (called Rabbi Yosei b. Avin in the Babylonian Talmud) was a fourth-generation Eretz Yisraeli Amora of the late fourth century CE. He appears primarily in the Jerusalem Talmud, where he is among the most frequently cited Amoraim, and only rarely in the Babylonian Talmud. He was a student of Rabbi Yosei of Yokrat, and according to Aharon Heiman his father was the Amora Rabbi Avin. In the Jerusalem Talmud he often debates halakha with Rabbi Mana and his students, and his statements typically conclude the sugya. He emended or explained traditions from Rabbi Yochanan and brought Babylonian traditions. He is traditionally credited with editing the Jerusalem Talmud, paralleling Rav Ashi's editing of the Babylonian Talmud. Later Eretz Yisraeli Amoraim are not mentioned by n

rabbi-yosei-bar-zevida.bio: Rabbi Yosei bar Zevida was a fourth-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora. He appears frequently in both Talmuds in disputes with his colleague Rabbi Yona, and in the Jerusalem Talmud he is sometimes called Rabbi Yasa. A student of Rabbi Ila and Rabbi Zeira, he taught first in Rabbi Ila's yeshiva and later in his own, and was one of the closers of the sugyot in tractate Nezikin in the Jerusalem Talmud. He lived in Tiberias; when decrees increased in Eretz Yisrael he briefly descended to Babylonia with Rabbi Yosei b. Rabbi Bun, then returned to become the leading sage of his generation. All fifth-generation Eretz Yisrael Amoraim were his students, foremost among them Rabbi Mana, and his son was Rabbi Elazar b. Rabbi Yosei.

rabbi-hananiah.bio: Rabbi Hananiah, also known as Rabbi Hananiah of Tzippori, was a fifth-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora. He shared a close collegial relationship with Rabbi Mana, expressed through numerous disputes. He initially served as head of the yeshiva in Tzippori, later ceding the position to Rabbi Mana and himself sitting as a student before him; for this self-effacement he was praised as one who relinquished honor in this world to inherit the World to Come. After some years he traveled to Babylonia, where he studied with Rav Ashi, and several disputes between them are recorded in the Babylonian Talmud.

rav-karna.bio: Rav Karna was among the first generation of Babylonian Amoraim in the period following the sealing of the Mishnah. A colleague of Shmuel and Rav, he is called Karna without title in the Babylonian Talmud and Rav Karna in the Jerusalem Talmud. He served as a judge, and the general title 'dayanei golah' in the Babylonian Talmud refers to him. He compiled a collection of baraitot on Seder Nezikin known as 'Nezikin deVei Karna,' and his livelihood came from testing the quality of wines.

rabbi-yitzchak-b-avdimi.bio: Rabbi Yitzchak b. Avdimi was a first- and second-generation Eretz Yisrael Amora. In his youth he studied under and served Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi. Sources indicate that he served as the tanna (reciter) in Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi's academy, playing a decisive role in the final formulation of the Mishnah; he taught most of the Mishnah to Rav, who transmitted it to Babylonia. In the Yerushalmi he is called Rabbi Yitzchak Ruba. He is to be distinguished from a later figure of the same name.

rav-yechezkel.bio: Rav Yechezkel was a first-generation Babylonian Amora mentioned rarely in the Babylonian Talmud. He is best known as the father of Rav Yehuda. While a Torah scholar, his primary greatness lay in mitzvot and good deeds, and Shmuel would stand before him. He had two sons who were Amoraim: the famous Rav Yehuda, who surpassed him in Torah but whom Shmuel instructed to rise before his father because the father was greater in good deeds; and Rami bar Yechezkel. The brothers studied separately under Shmuel and frequently contradicted each other, with Rami bar Yechezkel warning against certain general rules that his brother cited in Shmuel's name. The Jerusalem Talmud records a liturgical practice for rain that Rav Yehuda transmitted in his father's name, and the Babylonian Talmud cites a beraita

rav-shila.bio: Rav Shila was an Amora of the generation that bridged the Tannaim and Amoraim. He likely studied in Eretz Yisrael before relocating to Babylonia, where he headed the yeshiva (sidra) in Nehardea. Rav Chisda quotes him in the Talmud and calls him a "gavra raba" (Ketubot 75a). The Gemara records that he once erred in a halakhic ruling; Rav wished to excommunicate him, but Shmuel prevented this, clarified the issue with him, and after Rav Shila admitted his mistake he was not excommunicated. Rabbi Aharon Heiman distinguishes him from the Tanna Rabbi Shila, while Yaakov Neusner identifies them as one person.

rav-avimi.bio: Rav Avimi was an Amora of the second generation of Babylonian Amoraim. He was the primary teacher of Rav Chisda, and transmitted dozens of his rulings and statements.

rav-shmuel-bar-sheilat.bio: Rav Shmuel bar Sheilat was a first- and second-generation Babylonian Amora and a student of Rav. All halakhot he cites in the Talmud are transmitted in Rav's name. He was renowned as a dedicated teacher of young children; the Talmud records that he taught uninterrupted for thirteen years, earning Rav's praise with the verse from Daniel 12:3. Rav entrusted him with pedagogical guidance and requested that he deliver his eulogy so as to warm the hearts of listeners and bring them to tears. He was a wealthy man. A tradition identifies him as a descendant of Haman who studied Torah in Bnei Brak, though the Lubavitcher Rebbe concluded that his primary residence was Babylonia, with occasional study and teaching in Eretz Yisrael. His son was the Amora Rav Yehuda b. Rav Shmuel bar Sheilat.

rav-avin-naggara.bio: Rav Avin Naggara was a Babylonian Amora of the second or third generation, a contemporary of Rav Huna. He worked as a carpenter, from which his epithet derives. He was the father of Rav Idi b. Avin and Rav Chiyya b. Avin. The Talmud relates that he was exceedingly meticulous in observing the Shabbat candles. When Rav Huna saw his careful conduct, he declared that two righteous men would come from his house. Indeed, both Rav Idi, who became the teacher of Rav Papa, and Rav Chiyya were among the great sages of the fourth generation of Babylonian Amoraim.

rav-ada-b-ahavah.bio: Rav Ada b. Ahavah was a Babylonian Amora of the first and second generations of Amoraim. He was born eight days before the death of Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi and lived an exceptionally long life, spanning until the generation of Rav Nachman b. Yitzchak, though some maintain that there were two sages by this name. He was a preeminent student of Rav and transmitted many halakhot in his name. His students included Rav Nachman, Rav Malkiya, and others.

rav-ashian-b-nadvach.bio: Rav Ashian b. Nadvach was a Babylonian Amora of the second generation. He is mentioned in the Talmud only a few times.

rav-aha-b-dala.bio: Rav Aha b. Dala was a second-generation Babylonian Amora of the 3rd century and a student of Rav. The Talmud mentions him only as the recipient of instructions from his teacher: one may sleep outside a sukkah when flies disturb sleep inside; one who pounds salt on Yom Tov must tilt the pestle to do so unusually; and Rav used a small amount of saffron in his hand to demonstrate the law of ma'amad shelashtan. No independent statements of his appear in the Talmud, and nothing is known of his family.

rav-bruna.bio: Rav Bruna was a Babylonian Amora of the second generation. He was a preeminent student of Rav, and also transmitted teachings in the name of Shmuel.

rav-hamnuna-saba.bio: Rav Hamnuna Saba, also known as Rav Hamnuna the Elder, was a Babylonian Amora of the second generation.

rav-hamnuna.bio: Rav Hamnuna was a second-generation Babylonian Amora. A beloved student of Rav, he was called "my son" by his teacher. He transmitted numerous teachings in Rav's name, and the phrase amrei bei Rav is generally understood to refer to him. Among his students was Rav Yosef. He possessed a singular understanding of Rav's words, comprehending them even when others could not.

rav-hana-b-bizna.bio: Rav Hana b. Bizna was a Babylonian Amora of the second-third generation. He served as a judge in Pumbedita, but Rav Nachman reversed two of his rulings: one that orphans are sustained from their father's movable property, and another collecting a debt from slaves bequeathed to sons. He transmitted most of his teachings in the name of Rabbi Shimon Hasida, all of them concerning aggadah. He was so expert in aggadah that after defeating Rav Sheshet in a debate, Rav Sheshet conceded that Rav Hana surpassed him in that area.

rav-hilkiah-b-tovi.bio: Rav Hilkiah b. Tovi was a second-generation Babylonian Amora of the 3rd century. He appears infrequently in the Babylonian Talmud, where his statements are almost entirely halakhic in nature, and is mentioned once in the Jerusalem Talmud. He served as a judge alongside Rav Huna and Hiyya b. Rav, and was likely a student of Rav, as he is found in the company of Rav's disciples. Nothing is known of his hometown or family, though some speculate that Rav Minyomi b. Hilkiah was his son. He was a farmer who employed a unique method of crop rotation, sowing only half his field each year while plowing the other half.

rav-hiyya-bar-gamda.bio: Rav Hiyya bar Gamda was a second-generation Babylonian Amora. He is mentioned occasionally in the Jerusalem Talmud, where he is called Rabbi Hiyya bar Gamda.

rav-hinena-bar-shalmya.bio: Rav Hinena bar Shalmya was a second-generation Babylonian Amora of the 3rd century and a prominent disciple of Rav. His teachings appear in both Talmuds, all cited in Rav's name, and he frequently posed questions to his teacher. He served as a Mishnah instructor for Rav's son Hiyya. After Rav's death, he sent halakhic inquiries to Shmuel. In one incident, Rabbi Tavlah ruled according to Rav Hinena's view, prompting Rav Nachman to threaten: "Go retract, or I will produce Rav Hinena bar Shalmya from your ears" — meaning he would refute him using Rav Hinena's own words.

rav-hanan-bar-ami.bio: Rav Hanan bar Ami was a second-generation Babylonian Amora. He appears in the Talmud primarily transmitting halakhot in the name of his teachers Rav and Shmuel, including in Bava Metzia a ruling permitting one to rent out a poor person's pledge to reduce the debt if the item is durable and profitable to lease. In Beitzah he issues independent rulings regarding moving a ladder on a festival, restricting the dispute between Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel to either the public or private domain depending on the textual version. A statement of Rabbi Pedat in the name of Rabbi Yochanan cited elsewhere is attributed by some to a different Amora of the same name in Eretz Yisrael.

rav-chama-b-guria.bio: Rav Chama b. Guria was a second-generation Babylonian Amora of the 3rd century and a distinguished student of Rav. His statements in the Talmud are almost always cited in his own name, yet he also transmitted Rav's teachings to sages such as Rava b. Machsia, Rabbi Chelbo, and Avin b. Huna. Many of his sayings concern derekh eretz and proper personal conduct, while others treat medical questions on Shabbat. He is mentioned occasionally in the Yerushalmi. He once challenged Shmuel, who dismissed his objection sharply, and on another occasion advised a man to act quickly on a ruling of Rav before Shmuel could overturn it. Rabba b. Rav Huna calls him "our teachers who are expert in halakhah." The Talmud mentions Rav Nahman b. Guria, likely his brother, and Guria b. Chama, possibly his son.

rav-hana-bar-hanilai.bio: Rav Hana bar Hanilai was a Babylonian Amora of the second and third generations (third and fourth centuries). A distinguished student of Rav Huna, he lived in a suburb near Sura, though some identify his home as Mata Mehasia. He is called "a great man" and was deeply occupied with his studies; no halakhot or aggadot are cited in his name, though he raised a difficulty and asked a halakhic question of Rav Huna. He is remembered primarily for his extraordinary charity. The Talmud relates that Rav Hisda sighed upon seeing the ruins of Rav Hana's house, and described his remarkable charitable deeds. The Riaz describes him as a righteous man who performed many acts of kindness. He also participated uniquely in a charity collection organized by Rav Huna.

rav-tavla.bio: Rav Tavla was a Babylonian Amora of the second and third generations. He is mentioned mainly in the Babylonian Talmud, but also in the Jerusalem Talmud.

rav-yehuda-b-chaviva.bio: Rav Yehuda b. Chaviva was a second-generation Babylonian Amora of the third century and a student of Shmuel. His own statements in the Talmud are sparse; he generally transmits teachings in the names of other sages or baraitot. Little is known of his life, family, or place of residence, aside from the fact that his daughter married the son of Rav Yehuda, the famous Amora and leading student of Shmuel. The only statement he issued in his own name concerns combining the passage of tzitzit with the recitation of the Shema.

rav-yosef-b-abba.bio: Rav Yosef b. Abba was a Babylonian Amora who studied under Rabbi Yochanan, Shmuel, and Rav Yehuda. He is mentioned several times in the Talmud, including in a discussion on validating documents in the absence of a litigant, where he explains Rabbi Yochanan’s reasoning by deriving from the verse “and it be testified to its owner” that no legal judgment may be validated without the litigant’s presence. He was a contemporary of Rabba bar Yirmeya, and the Talmud records a joint discussion they held when Rabba bar Yirmeya came to his city.

rav-hananel.bio: Rav Hananel was a Babylonian Amora of the second generation. A preeminent student of Rav, he also studied under Rabbah bar bar Hana. He spent many years in Rav's study hall in Babylonia, where he acquired extensive Torah learning. A story is told that he and his study-hall colleague Rav Beruna ate a festive meal served by Rav Yeiva the Elder; after they decided to recite Birkat HaMazon and then requested a drink, Rav Yeiva cited Rav's teaching that such a decision ends the meal, so that any further food or drink would require a new blessing.

rav-yechiel.bio: Rav Yechiel was a Babylonian Amora of the second generation, during the 3rd century, and a colleague of Rav Yehuda. He is mentioned only rarely in the Talmud, and nothing is known of his place of activity, deeds, or teachers. Most of his statements are brief explanations of the Mishnah, often offering literal or quantitative definitions of its terms. For example, he explained that the "conquest of millstones" mentioned in the Mishnah means drilling a hole in the center of the millstone. Among his known teachings is the legal presumption that an agent performs his agency. A sage named Rabba b. Yonatan, otherwise unknown, transmitted in Rav Yechiel's name a medical saying that well-cooked barley is beneficial for the sick.

rav-yitzchak-b-shmuel-b-marta.canonical: Rav Yitzchak b. Shmuel b. Marta
rav-yitzchak-b-shmuel-b-marta.canonicalHe: רב יצחק בר שמואל בר מרתא
aliases: R. Yitzchak b. Shmuel b. Marta
Rav Yitzchak bar Shmuel bar Marta
R. Yitzchak bar Shmuel bar Marta
Rav Yitzchak b. Shmuel bar Marta
rav-yitzchak-b-shmuel-b-marta.bio: Rav Yitzchak b. Shmuel b. Marta was a Babylonian Amora of the second and third generations.

rav-yeva-the-father-in-law-of-ashian-b-n.canonical: Rav Yeva the father-in-law of Ashian b. Nadvach
aliases: R. Yeva the father-in-law of Ashian b. Nadvach
Rav Yeiva the father-in-law of Ashian b. Nadvach
Rav Yeva the father-in-law of Ashin b. Nadvach
rav-yeva-the-father-in-law-of-ashian-b-n.bio: Rav Yeva the father-in-law of Ashian b. Nadvach was a second-generation Babylonian Amora mentioned only sparingly in the Talmud. He appears in Tractate Bava Batra in connection with the laws of neighbor damages. There, he interprets in the name of Rav a baraita concerning a wall built alongside a neighbor's wall, ruling that it must be distanced so as not to block sunlight and that the required distance is the size of a window.

rav-yirmeyah-bar-abba.bio: Rav Yirmeyah bar Abba was a second-generation Babylonian Amora of the mid-3rd century CE. A student of the famous Amora Rav, he appears frequently in the Jerusalem Talmud, where he is cited simply as Rav Yirmeyah without his patronymic. Some say he was also called Rabbi Yirmeyah Rabbah (the Great).

rav-eina.bio: Rav Eina was a second-generation Babylonian Amora. A colleague and disputant of Rav Yehuda, together they were called in the Talmud the "Sabei dePumbedita" (Elders of Pumbedita). His eminent status is further evident from the fact that he would expound at the entrance of the Exilarch.

rav-amram.bio: Rav Amram was a Babylonian Amora of the second and third generations (3rd century) and a prominent disciple of Rav Sheshet. Most of his statements in the Talmud are cited in the name of other sages. He is also mentioned a few times in the Jerusalem Talmud.

rav-amram-chasida.bio: Rav Amram Chasida was a Babylonian Amora of the second generation, living in the third century. He was renowned for his piety and good deeds, which earned him the epithet Hasida. His statements in the Talmud are few.

rav-anan-b-tachlifa.bio: Rav Anan b. Tachlifa was a second-generation Babylonian Amora. He was a distinguished student of Shmuel, and transmitted many teachings in Shmuel's name, as well as in the name of Rav.

rav-yehuda-b-rav-shmuel-bar-sheilat.canonical: Rav Yehuda b. Rav Shmuel bar Sheilat
