rav-yehuda-b-rav-shmuel-bar-sheilat.canonicalHe: רב יהודה בן רב שמואל בר שילת
aliases: Rav Judah b. Rav Samuel bar Sheilat
Rav Yehuda ben Rav Shmuel bar Sheilat
R. Yehuda b. Rav Shmuel bar Sheilat
Rav Yehuda b. Rav Shmuel b. Sheilat
rav-yehuda-b-rav-shmuel-bar-sheilat.bio: Rav Yehuda b. Rav Shmuel bar Sheilat was a second-generation Babylonian Amora. He lived and was active in Babylonia.

rav-tachlifa-b-avdimi.bio: Rav Tachlifa b. Avdimi (also cited as b. Avimi) was a second-generation Amora and a student of Shmuel, following his rulings. Several statements in the Talmud are transmitted in his name. In Tractate Beitzah, Rav rebuked him for ruling leniently for the masses according to Shmuel's opinion, yet called him a tzurba m'rabbanan (Torah scholar). Most sources record his father's name as Avdimi, though some read Avimi.

rav-hana-of-baghdad.bio: Rav Hana of Baghdad was a second-generation Babylonian Amora who lived in Baghdad, Babylonia, during the third century. He was a student of Shmuel, and generally transmitted statements in his teacher's name.

rav-rabbah-b-avuha.bio: Rav Rabbah b. Avuha was a second-generation Babylonian Amora. A student of Rav, he was the principal teacher of Rav Nahman. He resided in Neharde'a, but after the city was destroyed by Papa b. Nazar he relocated to Shekhantziv, then to Shilhi, and finally to Mehuza, where he was appointed judge and city rabbi.

rav-rami-b-yechezkel.bio: Rav Rami b. Yechezkel, called Rabbi Ami in the Yerushalmi, was a second-generation Babylonian Amora, the younger brother of Rav Yehuda and son of Rav Yechezkel. He emigrated to Eretz Yisrael, visited Bnei Brak, and later spoke in praise of the land. He brought baraitot back from Eretz Yisrael, and the Talmud frequently cites them in his name. He sometimes disputed Rav Yehuda on halakhic matters, cautioning the study hall not to accept general rules that his brother formulated in the name of Shmuel or Rav. He also studied with Rav Amram, whom he honored with the title "Mar," likely Rav Amram the First, a student of Rav.

rav-avimi-b-papi.bio: Rav Avimi b. Papi was a third-generation Amora. He was a colleague of Rav Nachman and Ulla, and the Babylonian Talmud records that they sat together.

rav-abba-umna.bio: Rav Abba Umna was a professional bloodletter in Babylonia during the third generation of Amoraim. Although he was not counted among the Torah scholars sitting in the yeshiva, he was renowned for his righteousness and piety.

rav-rabbah-b-yirmeyah.bio: Rav Rabbah b. Yirmeyah was a second-generation Babylonian Amora of the third century and a student of Shmuel. He lived in Sura. His statements in the Babylonian Talmud are few, and he is cited in the Yerushalmi too. Most of his teachings were transmitted by Rabbi Zeira, who brought many traditions from Babylonia to Eretz Yisrael; the chain 'Rabbi Zeira said Rabbah b. Yirmeyah said Shmuel' is frequent. Rabbi Abba also cited him. Rabbi Zeira heard teachings from him in the name of Rav that were later found to have come via Rav Hama b. Guria, not directly. Some hold he was the son of Rav Yirmeyah b. Abba and that he may have lived into Rav's lifetime.

rav-dimi-b-yosef.bio: Rav Dimi b. Yosef was a third-generation Babylonian Amora. He may be identical to the Rav Dimi who appears throughout the Gemara without any additional appellation.

rav-dimi.bio: Rav Dimi was an Amora active during the transition between the third and fourth generations. Born in Eretz Yisrael, he journeyed to Babylonia as one of the Nehutei, where he shared traditions he had received from Rabbi Yochanan and from Ze'iri. He enjoyed a close friendship with Abaye, who addressed him in the direct, familiar language of a peer.

rav-acha-b-ada.bio: Rav Acha b. Ada was a Babylonian Amora of the second, third, and fourth generations. He studied under Rav, though his primary teacher was Rav Hamnuna. He briefly traveled to Eretz Yisrael, whence he transmitted testimonies to the sages of Babylonia, including a report about the division of a biblical verse into three verses there. He lived to an advanced age and his hand trembled. The Talmud relates that forgers once tried to fake his trembling signature on a document, but Rava uncovered the scheme because he remembered that he had never signed before Rav Acha out of respect for his elder status. Despite Rava's greater stature, Rav Acha directed his halakhic inquiries to him.

rav-huna-bar-hiyya.bio: Rav Huna bar Hiyya was a third-generation Babylonian Amora. Most of his Talmudic statements are cited in the name of his teachers, and he appears in the Jerusalem Talmud as well. He studied with Shmuel, Rav Kahana, Rav Nachman, and was among the younger students of Rav Yehuda. He married the daughter of Rav Yirmeyah bar Abba, from whom he received traditions; his son was Levi. After Rav Yehuda’s death, he served as head of the Pumbedita yeshiva when Rabbah declined the post, holding it until his death so that Torah study would not cease. He briefly served as a royal tax collector; upon hearing that the sages objected, he resigned. Rabbah ruled that he remained trustworthy, while Rav Yosef disagreed. He transmitted teachings in the name of Shmuel, and his son Levi was a colleague of Rava ba

rav-hiyya-b-ami.bio: Rav Hiyya b. Ami was a Babylonian Amora of the third and fourth generations.

rav-yitzchak-b-ashian.bio: Rav Yitzchak b. Ashian was a third-generation Babylonian Amora.

rav-hiyya-bar-rav.bio: Rav Hiyya bar Rav was a Babylonian Amora of the third generation. The son of Rav, he was likely named for his uncle Rabbi Hiyya. He studied under his father, under Rav Hanan bar Rava, and under Rav Hiyya bar Ashi; after Rav's death he studied under Shmuel. He is frequently recorded disputing Rav Huna, and in every such case the halakhah follows Rav Huna. He ruled on the laws of Keriat Shema and held that Musaf is recited only with a congregation, in accordance with Rabbi Yehuda. He transmitted a question and answer between Rabba bar Avuha and Rav, and Abaye cites a statement in his name. The Talmud records that Rav described him as sickly. His son, Shimi bar Hiyya, was a Torah scholar who appears often in the Talmud in discussions with his grandfather Rav. Rav Hiyya bar Rav is also mention

rav-yaakov-of-nehar-pakod.bio: Rav Yaakov of Nehar Pakod was a Babylonian Amora of the sixth generation and a student of Ravina. Originating from Nehar Pakod in Babylonia, few details of his family or life are known. In the Babylonian Talmud he is cited several times transmitting in Ravina's name comments on the Talmud's reasoning, questions, and resolutions. He is mentioned in Tractate Ketubot in the sugya of one who was married to three wives.

rav-yosef-b-hama.bio: Rav Yosef b. Hama was a Babylonian Amora of the third generation who lived in Mechoza. He was a student of Rav Sheshet, though some of his statements in the Talmud are also cited in the name of Rav Nachman bar Yaakov. His son was Rava, a leading Amora and head of the Pumbedita Yeshiva in Mechoza.

aliases: Rav Mari b. Rachel bat Shemuel
rav-mari-b-rachel.bio: Rav Mari b. Rachel, also called the son of the daughter of Shemuel, was a Babylonian Amora of the third and fourth generations. He appears dozens of times in the Babylonian Talmud and was close to Rava.

rav-sechora.bio: Rav Sechora was a Babylonian Amora of the third and fourth generations and a student of Rav Huna. He appears in the Talmud about ten times, always transmitting teachings in the name of Rav Huna. In every instance, his statements are cited by Rava in the fixed formula: "Rava said: Rav Sechora said: Rav Huna said."

rav-acha-bar-ovya.bio: Rav Acha bar Ovya was a Babylonian Amora of the third generation.

rav-ika-b-avin.bio: Rav Ika b. Avin was a Babylonian Amora of the third generation. He was a student of Rav Chananel and quoted his teachings by name. In Kiddushin 72b he states that "Chalzon Nihond" is "like the diaspora regarding lineage" (kegolah liyuchasin), meaning its people are fit to marry. Abaye objects that the statement carries no weight, as Rav Ika only says it because he needs to marry a yevamah from there, but Rav Ika replies that it was taught in the name of Rav Chananel, who confirms it.

rav-kahana-of-pum-nahara.bio: Rav Kahana of Pum Nahara was a Babylonian Amora of the third and fourth generations who headed the yeshiva in Pum Nahara. A kohen of Eli's family, he studied with Rav Yehuda and later with Rava, learning Sanhedrin alongside Rav Safra. He emigrated to Eretz Yisrael with Rav Safra and studied with Rabbi Zeira, Rabbi Abbahu, and Rabbi Chiya bar Abba, who prayed for his longevity. Returning to Babylonia, he established his yeshiva in Pum Nahara, where Rav Ashi became his outstanding student; Rav Ashi honored him beyond Amemar. He was a colleague of Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak, and together they rebuked arrogant wealthy men who interfered in judicial appointments. Though his academy was subordinate to Sura, after Rava's death the heads of Neharde'a frequently visited him, and he maintained ties wi

rava-b-machsiya.bio: Rava b. Machsiya was a Babylonian Amora of the third generation, sometimes called Rabbah. The Talmud records ten statements in Tractate Shabbat and one in Tractate Taanit in his name, which he transmitted in the name of Rav Chama b. Guria citing Rav. His preserved teachings are aggadic, including the duty to inform a recipient of a gift, the peril of parental favoritism, the ruin awaiting a city whose roofs rise above its synagogue, a list of sufferings that excludes certain ailments and a bad wife, and the boundless scope of government affairs.

rabbah-bar-nahmani.bio: Rabbah bar Nahmani, commonly known simply as Rabbah, was a Babylonian Amora of the third generation who served as head of the Pumbedita academy for twenty-two years. A kohen, he studied under Rav Huna and Rav Yehuda bar Yehezkel, and was among the most celebrated and widely accepted sages of his generation. In halakhic disputes between him and Rav Yosef, the law generally follows Rabbah, except in three instances where it follows Rav Yosef.

ravin-b-rav-ada.bio: Ravin b. Rav Ada was a Babylonian Amora of the third generation (c. 290–320 CE). A student of Rav Yehuda, he was likely the brother of Rava b. Rav Ada. He is cited primarily as a transmitter of traditions, notably passing on teachings of the Eretz Yisraeli Amora Rabbi Yitzchak Nafcha, whom he apparently heard while Rabbi Yitzchak visited Babylonia. He and his brother also transmitted statements in the name of their teacher Rav Yehuda. One of the few sources reflecting his own legal stance appears in the Jerusalem Talmud, where he did not protest against drawing human figures on mosaics despite its earlier association with idolatrous practice; scholars suggest this leniency may reflect the diminished idolatrous significance of such images in his time.

rav-rami-bar-tamrei.bio: Rav Rami bar Tamrei was a Babylonian Amora and a student of Rav Yehuda. Also known as Rami bar Dikulei, he is described in the Babylonian Talmud as a great sage and extremely sharp.

ravin-b-rav-nachman.bio: Ravin b. Rav Nachman, also known as Rav Avin, was a Babylonian Amora of the third generation. A devoted student of Rav Yehuda, he was so beloved that Rav Yehuda called him "Ravin my brother" and issued halachic rulings to him. The two owned neighboring fields whose tree branches overhung each other's property; according to the Rosh, they were close friends and did not object. Rav Yehuda advised him to purchase land far from the city to avoid the evil eye. He also studied with Rav Sheshet.

rav-rabbanai.bio: Rav Rabbanai, also known as Ravyanai, was a Babylonian Amora of the third generation. He was the brother of Rabbi Chiyya bar Abba.

rav-rami-bar-abba.bio: Rav Rami bar Abba was a Babylonian Amora of the third generation and a student of Rav Huna. He transmitted many enactments of Rabbi Yosei of Tzippori to his contemporaries, including a decree that a woman should not walk in the marketplace with her son following her, due to an incident in which licentious men abducted the child to lure her into a secluded place; and a decree that women should speak loudly in public latrines so that men would know they were present and not approach, for reasons of modesty and possible yichud. He frequently cited teachings of his teacher Rav Huna, and was extensively engaged in aggadah.

rav-avimi-b-rabbah.bio: Rav Avimi b. Rabbah was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora and a student of Rabbah. He was the son of Rabbah of Pumbedita and Choma, granddaughter of Rav Yehuda. He and his brother Ifa are called in the Talmud the "sharp ones of Pumbedita".

rav-sheizbi.bio: Rav Sheizbi was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora and student of Rav Chisda. Known as a great man, he was such that Rava warned that when he offered difficult teachings, one should examine them carefully and not dismiss them, for they were spoken by a great man. The Talmud relates that Rav Chisda once passed the home of Rav Sheizbi's father (or father-in-law), where many candles were lit, and predicted that a great man would emerge from that household. Although primarily a student of Rav Chisda, he also discussed halakha before Rabbah. Hyman conjectured that he briefly traveled to Eretz Yisrael and studied under Rabbi Yochanan. He is distinguished from a later Rav Sheizbi who was a student of Rava.

rav-abba-b-marta.bio: Rav Abba b. Marta, also known as Rav Abba b. Manyumi, was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora and student of Rav Yehuda. Some identify him with the Amoraim Abba son of Rav Minyamin b. Hiyya and Abba b. Binyamin. Though a student of Rav Yehuda, he also posed halakhic questions to Abaye, his contemporary.

rav-achdevoi-b-ami.bio: Rav Achdevoi b. Ami was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora. He is known primarily for his halakhic questions and for his complex relationship with Rav Sheshet.

rav-aha-b-yosef.bio: Rav Aha b. Yosef was a Babylonian Amora of the fourth generation. A leading student of Rav Huna, he also studied under Rav Chisda and was the uncle of Rav Nachman b. Yitzchak. Heiman, author of Seder HaDorot, argued that he was mistakenly conflated with Rav Acha brei d'Rav Yosef, a figure from a different period, while other scholars maintain that the two names refer to the same person.

rav-bibi.bio: Rav Bibi was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora. He is known from an aggadic account in which he plastered his daughter limb by limb with lime to enhance her beauty, a procedure Rav Nachman connected to his beer drinking. He disputed city leadership with the innkeeper of Rav Ada bar Ahava, a convert; Rav Yosef ruled that Rav Bibi would oversee religious matters while the convert managed municipal affairs. He was the uncle of Yitzchak the Exilarch. He discussed halakha with Rav Nachman, whose statements he also taught, and with Rav Yosef, the latter apparently as a colleague, and he transmitted teachings in the name of Rav Ada bar Ahava.

rav-huna-b-hinena.bio: Rav Huna b. Hinena was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora.

rav-acha-bar-huna.bio: Rav Acha bar Huna was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora. He studied under Rav Sheshet in his youth, and after Rav Sheshet’s death he likely studied alongside Rav Shmuel, son of Rabba bar bar Chana, while maintaining contact with Rav Yosef, to whom he sent halakhic inquiries. After Rabba’s death he joined Rava’s academy; Rava treated him as a disciple, assigning him tasks together with Rav Safra. On one occasion Rava sent Rav Acha bar Huna and Rav Safra to offer a sacrifice brought by Ifra Hormiz, mother of King Shapur, instructing them to find untouched ground, unused wood, and kindle fire from stones. He likely served as a judge.

rav-zevid.bio: Rav Zevid was a Babylonian Amora of the fourth and fifth generations. A student of Abaye and Rava, he was a frequent disputant of Rav Papa. Rav Kahana was among his students. According to the Iggeret of Rav Sherira Gaon, he served several years as head of the Pumbedita yeshiva. He married the mother of Rav Zutra bar Toviah but divorced her shortly thereafter.

rav-zevid-of-nehardea.bio: Rav Zevid of Nehardea was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora and a sage of the yeshiva of Nehardea. He was a colleague of Rav Dimi and the teacher of Amemar and Rav Kahana of Pum Nahara. Amemar, as a member of the yeshiva of Nehardea, favored Rav Zevid of Nehardea's opinions over those of other Amoraim, including Rav Kahana, Rav Papi, and Rav Zevid.

rav-yehuda-brother-of-rav-sela-the-hasid.canonical: Rav Yehuda, Brother of Rav Sela the Hasid
rav-yehuda-brother-of-rav-sela-the-hasid.canonicalHe: רב יהודה אחוה דרב סלא חסידא
aliases: Rav Judah, Brother of Rav Sela the Hasid
R. Yehuda, Brother of Rav Sela the Hasid
Rav Yehuda, the Brother of Rav Sela the Hasid
Rav Yehuda, Brother of Rav Sela HaHasid
Rav Yehuda Brother of Rav Sela the Hasid
rav-yehuda-brother-of-rav-sela-the-hasid.bio: Rav Yehuda, Brother of Rav Sela the Hasid, was a Babylonian Amora. He is mentioned several times in the Talmud, primarily in connection with aggadic teachings he heard from Elijah the Prophet.

rav-yeiva-saba.bio: Rav Yeiva Saba was a Babylonian Amora of the third and fourth generations.

rav-hiyya-bar-avin.bio: Rav Hiyya bar Avin was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora, the son of Rabbi Avin Naggara and brother of Rav Idi bar Avin. He studied under Rav Amram, Rav Huna, Rav Chisda, and Rav Sheshet, and was a preeminent student of Rav Huna; the Talmud relates that he once misunderstood Rav Huna's words as a dismissal, when Rav Huna had actually meant that his statement aligned with Rav Assi of Hutzal. He also studied under Rabbi Yochanan in Eretz Yisrael, transmitting teachings of the Land's sages. He cited many traditions in the names of Rav and Shmuel, though probably not from direct contact. In the Yerushalmi he is called Rabbi Hiyya bar Bun. His colleague was Rav Huna bar Chinena and his student was Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak.

rav-huna-b-yehuda.bio: Rav Huna b. Yehuda was a Babylonian Amora and student of Rav Yehuda and Rav Sheshet, cited often in the Talmud. He was a colleague of Rava; both studied under Rav Sheshet and debated his rulings. They disagreed, for example, regarding a rebellious wife: Rava held their teacher required gradual persuasion with a weekly deduction of seven dinars from her ketubah, whereas Rav Huna maintained that after four weeks she forfeits the entire sum. Rava sometimes stayed at his home; the Talmud relates that on one Shabbat evening Rava recited the blessing over spices before fire at Havdalah, citing Rabbi Yehuda's version of the dispute between Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel. Scholars such as Ze'ev Yavetz identify him with Rav Huna the son of Rav Yehuda.

rav-safra.bio: Rav Safra (c. 280–c. 338) was a prominent Babylonian Amora of the fourth generation.

mar-bereih-deravna.bio: Mar Bereih deRavna was a Babylonian Amora of the fourth generation, active in the time of Abaye and Rava. He is not to be confused with the son of the famous Amora Ravina, who lived later.

rav-yitzchak-b-yehuda.bio: Rav Yitzchak b. Yehuda was a Babylonian Amora of the third and fourth generations. He was a distinguished student of his father, Rav Yehuda, with whom he engaged extensively in halakhic discourse. His father held him in very high regard and would even transmit teachings in his name, a practice considered exceptional, as an Amora generally would not teach in the name of his son or student. At times, he would interpret his father Rav Yehuda's statements and transmit them in his father's name in the study hall.

rav-rabba-b-ulla.bio: Rav Rabba b. Ulla was a Babylonian Amora of the fourth and fifth generations. He engaged in halakhic discourse with Rav Kahana, Abaye, and Rava. The Talmud relates that at his son's wedding (or his daughter's, according to another version), an ancient custom was observed and a special oil was smeared on the students' heads. Though a contemporary of Abaye and Rava, he showed deference to Rav Beivai b. Abaye, addressing him as "Rabbi." His name is uncertain as "Rava" or "Rabba," though the latter is more common.

rav-eifa.bio: Rav Eifa, also called Iffa and referred to in the Yerushalmi as Chaifa, was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora. A student of Rabba, he was the son of Rechava of Pumbedita and Chuma, granddaughter of Rav Yehuda. He and his brother Avimi are called in the Talmud the 'sharp ones of Pumbedita'.

rav-ukva-bar-hama.bio: Rav Ukva bar Hama was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora. Unlike his brother Rami bar Hama, who died young before receiving the title Rav, Ukva was the elder brother and had reached the age of instruction, and is therefore usually called Rav Ukva bar Hama in the Talmud, though occasionally cited as Mar Ukva bar Hama. He had a business partnership with Rami, and the two married sisters, daughters of Rav Chisda. He engaged in halakhic discourse with Rav Nachman, likely his teacher, with his colleagues Abaye and Rava, and with the younger Rav Huna son of Rav Yehoshua. The Talmud preserves a dispute between the brothers over land gifted by their mother, adjudicated by Rav Sheshet and Rav Nachman, who invoked the principle of shuda d'dayanei to rule in Ukva's favor.

rav-rechava.bio: Rav Rechava, also referred to as Rechava of Pumbedita, was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora and a prominent disciple of Rav Yehuda.

rabbah-bar-rav-hanan.bio: Rabbah bar Rav Hanan was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora and son of Rav Hanan. He was a colleague of Abaye; both studied under Rabbah of Pumbedita in youth. A story tells how Rabbah tested them as children about where God dwells, foreseeing greatness. Abaye, younger, was his student-colleague. Rabbah esteemed him greatly and asked that he and Abaye eulogize him. He engaged in discourse before Rava, and Rav Avya was a colleague. He lived in Artivna near Pumbedita and made an eruv techumin to walk there on Shabbat, perhaps to hear Rav Yosef. He likely farmed date palms bordering Rav Yosef's orchard; when birds nesting there caused damage, he refused to uproot them but let Rav Yosef do so. No halakha is cited in his name in the Gemara; his many appearances are in Talmudic dialogue, raisi

rav-sa-uram.bio: Rav Sa'uram, also called Rav Sha'urim, was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora in the fourth century and the brother of Rava, a leading sage of that generation. No halachic or aggadic teachings of his are preserved, and his few mentions in the Talmud usually relate to his brother. Nevertheless, the Talmud portrays him as a first-rate sage and attributes to him a conversation with the Angel of Death and a posthumous dream appearance of Rava. In Bava Metzia 73b he rules that the biblical prohibition against enslaving a fellow Jew does not apply to Jews who misbehave, and he harnessed offenders to Rava's carriage; Rava approved and praised the act. This ruling was later codified in Shulchan Aruch, Yoreh De'ah 267:16.

rav-rechumi.bio: Rav Rechumi was a Babylonian Amora of the fourth generation. A student of both Abaye and Rava, he studied in Rava's yeshiva in Mechoza, where he was a preeminent disciple. He appears in Tractate Pesachim as a student of Abaye, posing a question about the essence of maror, and in Tractate Ketubot as a student of Rava.

rav-rafram-bar-papa.bio: Rav Rafram bar Papa was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora (fourth century). A student of Rav Chisda and a colleague of Rava, he was older than Rava and likely of the same generation as Rav Huna. Nearly all of his statements in the Talmud appear in his own name, spanning both halakha and aggadah. When Rava asked him to recount the good deeds of Rav Huna, he replied that he could only remember them from Rav Huna’s old age, not his youth. He was not the son of the later Rav Papa. A lesser-known sage named Rafram bar Papa of Sichra is also mentioned, likely to distinguish him from this figure.

rav-shimi-of-mechoza.bio: Rav Shimi of Mechoza was a Babylonian Amora who resided in the city of Mechoza. He was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora, contemporary with Abaye and Rava. He is mentioned in the Talmud only a few times.

rav-rami-bar-hama.bio: Rav Rami bar Hama was a fourth-generation Babylonian Amora. He studied under Rav Chisda alongside Rava. The Talmud records teachings he transmitted in the names of Rav Sheshet, Rav Nachman, and Rabbi Yitzchak. A sharp scholar, he answered questions through logical reasoning (misevara) rather than relying solely on earlier sources, unlike Rav Sheshet. This approach caused his student Rav Yitzchak, son of Rav Yehuda, to leave him for Rav Sheshet. He died young—some sources say at age twenty-seven—and due to his youth never formally received the title Rav.

rav-avimi-of-hagronia.bio: Rav Avimi of Hagronia was a fifth-generation Babylonian Amora and a student of Rava. He is frequently mentioned in the Talmud citing halakhot in the name of Rava, and once transmits a halakha in the name of Rav Huna.

rav-ada-b-abba.bio: Rav Ada b. Abba, also called Rav Ada b. Ahava II, was a fifth-generation Babylonian Amora. He was a distinguished student of Rava.

rav-acha-b-rav-ika.bio: Rav Acha b. Rav Ika was a fifth-generation Babylonian Amora. The son of Rav Ika and the sister of Rav Acha b. Yaakov, he lived in Pafonya with his uncle, Rav Acha b. Yaakov. He appears dozens of times in the Babylonian Talmud, engaging in discussions with his colleague Rav Papa, and Rav Huna b. Manoach frequently cites his statements. He may have been the brother of Rav Huna son of Rav Ika.

rav-dimi-of-neharde-a.bio: Rav Dimi of Neharde'a was a Babylonian Amora of the fifth generation of Babylonian Amoraim. He was among the leading sages of the yeshiva of Neharde'a.

rav-hama.bio: Rav Hama was a fifth-generation Babylonian Amora and one of the sages of the Yeshiva of Nehardea. He appears dozens of times in the Babylonian Talmud. He was so closely identified with Nehardea that he was known by the epithet "the Nehardean".

rav-amemar.bio: Rav Amemar was a Babylonian Amora of the fifth and sixth generations, and one of the leading sages of his generation. He was a head of the yeshiva in Nehardea, his city, though he later relocated to Mechoza and served there as a judge. His students included Rav Ashi, head of the Sura academy, who was like a student-colleague to him, and Rav Huna b. Natan, the Exilarch. In the Babylonian Talmud he is frequently mentioned alongside his colleagues Mar Zutra and Rav Ashi. He was the son of the daughter of Rami b. Chama, who was the son-in-law of Rav Chisda.

rav-beivai-bar-abaye.bio: Rav Beivai bar Abaye was a fifth-generation Babylonian Amora and the son of Abaye, head of the Pumbedita yeshiva. He debated halakhah with Rav Huna brei d'Rav Yehoshua and Mar Zutra. He once attempted to see demons through a spiritual technique and was injured, then healed by the sages' prayers. A descendant of Eli the Priest, he was subject to that family's curse of premature death; the sages linked flawed Torah reasoning to it and twice rebuked him with the saying "Since you are from a cut-off family, you say cut-off reasoning" — first by Rav Papi for claiming payment for spontaneous growth in a leased field, and again by Rav Huna brei d'Rav Yehoshua for misapplying the law of lifetime gifts. He was a friend of the Angel of Death and related an anecdote of a mistakenly collected soul. Hi

aliases: Rav Huna son of Rav Yehoshua
rav-huna-b-rav-yehoshua.bio: Rav Huna b. Rav Yehoshua was a fifth-generation Babylonian Amora and one of its most prominent figures. He studied under Abaye and Rava at the yeshiva of Pumbedita, and was a close colleague and disputant of Rav Papa. When Rav Papa founded the yeshiva in Naresh, Rav Huna joined him and served as rosh kallah, delivering lectures during the months of Adar and Elul. Early in his studies he supported himself from a small parcel of land; later he entered a business partnership with Rav Papa selling sesame. He was known for his humility and willingness to forgo his rights. Once, when he fell ill and Rav Papa prepared shrouds for him, he recovered and taught that because he had always been forgiving, God forgave him as well. He died around 411 CE according to some chronologies, though others sugg

rav-yosef-b-ami.bio: Rav Yosef b. Ami was a Babylonian Amora of the fifth generation. He is mentioned only twice in the Talmud, in one instance raising a contradiction between two statements of Rava. The author of Erech Tannaim veAmoraim infers from this that he belonged to the later generations, and hypothesizes that he may have been the brother of Rav Pinchas b. Ami; if so, he lived in the time of Rav Ashi.

rav-yehuda-b-merimar.bio: Rav Yehuda b. Merimar was a fifth-generation Babylonian Amora and student of Rava, transmitting halakha in his name. A colleague of Rav Papa and a man of great stature, he was asked to accompany Rav Papa to negotiate a dowry with Abba Sura. Fearing Abba Sura would be shamed into giving more than he wished, Yehuda initially refused; though he eventually attended, he remained silent. Abba Sura interpreted this as displeasure and kept increasing the dowry until he had given most of his assets. Yehuda then explained that even the original sum was unnecessary, but since Abba Sura had pledged it, he should not retract. Some identify him with the figure called Yehuda Mar b. Merimar, though scholars differ on whether the "Mar" belongs to him or to a later namesake, a colleague of Mar b. Rav Ashi.

rav-huna-b-natan.bio: Rav Huna b. Natan was a Babylonian Amora of the fifth and sixth generations who served as Exilarch and political head of the Jews in Babylonia. A scion of the Exilarch family descended from King David, he studied under Rav Papa and Amemar and sat before Rava in the yeshiva. His tenure as Exilarch coincided with Rav Ashi's leadership of the Sura academy. He enjoyed close ties with the Sassanian king Yazdegerd I, who ruled Babylonia and favored the Jews, and was a member of the royal household. Some scholars distinguish between two figures: Huna b. Natan of the sixth generation and Rav Huna b. Natan of the fourth and fifth generations.

rav-pinchas.bio: Rav Pinchas was a Babylonian Amora of the fifth generation. He frequently transmits teachings in the name of Rava. He is to be distinguished from the Eretz Yisrael Amora Rabbi Pinchas. The Babylonian Talmud mentions a Rabbi Pinchas whose identity is unknown. The Amora Rav Huna b. Rav Pinchas is also mentioned, and he may have been Rav Pinchas's son.

mar-yanuka-and-mar-kashisha-sons-of-rav-.canonical: Mar Yanuka and Mar Kashisha, sons of Rav Chisda
mar-yanuka-and-mar-kashisha-sons-of-rav-.canonicalHe: מר ינוקא ומר קשישא בני רב חסדא
aliases: Mar Yanuka and Mar Kashisha ben Rav Chisda
Mar Yanuka and Mar Kashisha, sons of Rav Hisda
Mar Yanuka and Mar Kashisha b. Rav Hisda
mar-yanuka-and-mar-kashisha-sons-of-rav-.bio: Mar Yanuka and Mar Kashisha, sons of Rav Chisda, were two Babylonian Amoraim of the fifth generation.

rav-mesharshiya.bio: Rav Mesharshiya was a Babylonian Amora of the fifth generation. He was a preeminent student of Rava, and the two are mentioned together dozens of times in the Talmud; he may also have studied under Abaye. He was a close colleague of Rav Papa, another student of Rava, and sent his own son to study before him. Rav Yosef, son of Rav Ila'i, was also among his friends. His father-in-law was Rav Kahana. Three of his sons—Rava, Rav Sama, and the honored Rav Yitzchak—were Amoraim who studied in the study house of Rav Ashi, whom Mesharshiya had encouraged them to attend. Upon his death, palm trees miraculously sprouted thorns instead of dates as a sign of mourning.

rav-papi.bio: Rav Papi was a Babylonian Amora of the fifth generation. He was a leading student of Rava, and a colleague and disputant of Rav Papa and Rav Huna b. Rav Yehoshua. Among his students were Rav Ashi and Rav Yirmeya of Difti.

rav-bar-sheva.bio: Rav bar Sheva was a Babylonian Amora of the fifth generation. He engaged in halakhic discourse with Rav Kahana of Pum Nahara, the teacher of Rav Ashi, and with Rav Papa.

rav-aharon.bio: Rav Aharon was a sixth-generation Babylonian Amora. He appears in two places in the Talmud, in both cases bringing a source in response to Ravina's uncertainty.

rav-shimi-bar-ashi.bio: Rav Shimi bar Ashi was a Babylonian Amora of the fifth or sixth generation. He was a member of Abaye's household and transmitted teachings that Abaye had shared with his sons, though he is not counted among the formal students of Abaye and Rava and often debated halakha with them, sometimes disagreeing. He was a colleague of Rav Papa, also of the circle of Abaye and Rava, and the two addressed each other as peers; nevertheless, Rav Shimi was more like a student to Rav Papa and frequently posed difficult questions, occasionally interrupting his public lectures. After once finding Rav Papa praying to be spared from his questions to avoid embarrassment, Rav Shimi ceased asking him in public. Rav Kahana of Pum Nahara transmitted his teachings, and Rav Shimi spent time in Pum Nahara at Rav Kaha

mar-shmuel-mar.bio: Mar Shmuel Mar was a fifth-generation Babylonian Amora. A student of Rava, he was close to Rav Papi, who stayed as his guest. In printed editions of the Babylonian Talmud he is called Mar Shmuel, which creates confusion with the renowned Amora Shmuel, who is sometimes called by that name as well.

rav-shemaya-b-ze-ira.bio: Rav Shemaya b. Ze'ira was a fifth-generation Babylonian Amora. A student of both Abaye and Rava, he served as an intermediary through whom Rava would send questions to Abaye. The Babylonian Talmud records only one statement of his, in which he explains that coins found in Jerusalem during festivals are presumed to be second-tithe money rather than secular coins, because the city's markets are swept daily; thus the coins must have been dropped that day, when most money in Jerusalem is second-tithe funds.

rav-acha-of-difti.bio: Rav Acha of Difti was a Babylonian Amora of the sixth generation. He was among the sages of the Sura yeshiva in Mata Mehasia. His statements are cited frequently in the Babylonian Talmud, where he often debates with his colleague Ravina.

rav-acha-b-abaye.bio: Rav Acha b. Abaye was a Babylonian Amora of the sixth generation. He engaged in halakhic discourse with Ravina. Rabbi Aharon Heiman maintained that he was not the son of the fourth-generation Amora Abaye, since he cites him merely as "Abaye" rather than as his father.

rav-gameda.bio: Rav Gameda was a Babylonian Amora and a student of Rava. His statements are cited throughout the Talmud. He was later counted among the leading scholars of the sixth generation of Babylonian Amoraim, alongside Amemar, Mar Zutra, and Rav Ashi. The Talmud (Nedarim) relates that he once gave sailors money to purchase something; when they could not find it, they bought him a monkey instead. The monkey escaped into a hole in the ground, and when they dug to retrieve it they discovered a trove of precious pearls, which they brought to him.

rav-acha-son-of-rav-avya.canonical: Rav Acha son of Rav Avya
aliases: R. Acha son of Rav Avya
rav-acha-son-of-rav-avya.bio: Rav Acha son of Rav Avya was a Babylonian Amora. He is generally identified as a sixth-generation amora and student-colleague of Rav Ashi. According to the author of Seder HaDorot, however, he is Rav Acha b. Avya of the third generation, a student of Rabbi Yochanan and Rabbi Assi, and a student-colleague of the earlier Rav Ashi.

rav-chavivi-of-mechoza.bio: Rav Chavivi of Mechoza was a sixth-generation Babylonian Amora who lived in Mechoza. He is mentioned several times in the Talmud as a student-colleague of Rav Ashi. He is sometimes referred to as Rav Chaviva.

mar-zutra.bio: Mar Zutra was a Babylonian Amora of the sixth generation. He was a close colleague of Amemar and Rav Ashi. According to the early work Seder Tana'im veAmora'im, he headed the yeshiva of Pumbedita before Rav Acha b. Rava. The French version of Rav Sherira Gaon's epistle does not list him as head of the yeshiva, though later sources do.

rav-yeimar.bio: Rav Yeimar was a Babylonian Amora of the sixth generation. A colleague of Rav Ashi, with whom he engaged extensively in halakhic discourse, he studied together with Rav Ashi under Rav Kahana of Pum Nahara. According to Rav Sherira Gaon's epistle, Rav Yeimar succeeded Rav Ashi as head of the Sura yeshiva for five years (427–432). Seder Tannaim veAmoraim relates that at his death a pillar of fire stood in the heavens for thirty days.

rav-huna-b-manoach.bio: Rav Huna b. Manoach was a Babylonian Amora of the sixth generation. After studying under Rava, he went together with Rav Shmuel b. Idi and Rav Chiyya of Vestaniya to study under Rav Papa, who headed the yeshiva. He and his colleagues were dissatisfied with Rav Papa's study method; when his teachings displeased them they would hint to each other, offending Rav Papa. Rav Papa dreamed a heavenly warning that severe punishment might befall these three students, alluding to the verse "And I will cut off the three shepherds." Following the dream, Rav Papa asked them to leave his beit midrash, and they departed. Rav Huna b. Manoach transmits many statements in the name of Rav Acha b. Rav Ika.

rava-b-rabbah.bio: Rava b. Rabbah was a Babylonian Amora of the fourth generation.

aliases: Rav Nehemiah son of Rav Yosef
R. Nehemiah son of Rav Yosef
rav-nehemiah-b-rav-yosef.bio: Rav Nehemiah b. Rav Yosef was a sixth-generation Babylonian Amora and the son of Rav Yosef, head of the Pumbedita academy. Born late in his father's life, few statements are cited in his name. He was an expert in examining tereifot, particularly lung adhesions (sircha), and developed independent methods for their inspection. A distinguished student of Rava, he learned from both his revealed and hidden conduct, including a ruling about sharpening a knife on a festival with a change (shinui) and a lesson that one should prioritize guarding tefillin even at slight cost to their honor. Later Amoraim labored to understand his rulings. He served as head of the court in his city and was a colleague of Rav Papa, Rav Papi, and Rav Huna b. Rav Yehoshua.

rav-rafram.bio: Rav Rafram was a sixth-generation Babylonian Amora. He headed the yeshiva in Pumbedita during the years 388–395. He was a colleague of Rav Ashi.

rav-mareimar.bio: Rav Mareimar was a Babylonian Amora of the sixth generation. Sefer HaKabbalah states that he headed the yeshiva in Sura. Many of his statements in the Talmud are introduced with "derash," reflecting homilies he delivered in the yeshiva. He was a close colleague of Mar Zutra; the two built synagogues, led their community together, and are frequently mentioned side by side in the Talmud. He was also a friend of Ravina, with whom he engaged in extensive halakhic discourse, and he transmitted traditions from Ravina's father. The Talmud relates that Rav Acha once sent a questioner back to ask Mareimar after disagreeing with a ruling. Later Amoraim derived several laws from his conduct, particularly concerning Havdalah. His son, Yehuda Mar bar Mareimar, was also an Amora whose rulings appear in

rav-nachman-b-rav-huna.bio: Rav Nachman b. Rav Huna was one of the last Babylonian Amoraim and head of the Sura academy. According to Rav Sherira Gaon's letter, he succeeded Rav Idi b. Avin as head of Sura in 451 CE and led the academy for three years until his death in 454 CE. He is not mentioned in the Talmud by his full name; some scholars identify him with a Rav Nachman who debated with Rav Ashi and Ravina, though Shalom Albeck maintains that these passages require emendation and that he is absent from the Talmud entirely. He was not the son of the earlier Amora Rav Huna, and no connection between them is known. A tradition in Shibolei ha-Leket links his days to the institution of embedding the Shema within Kedushah to evade a Persian decree, though some emend the text to place that decree after his death.

rav-avahu.bio: Rav Avahu was a Babylonian Amora of the seventh generation, on the eve of the Savoraic period. He was among the sages of the Pumbedita academy and a student of Rav Ashi. His son, Rav Shmuel, was one of the first Savoraim, who transmitted halakha in Rav Avahu's name in the Babylonian Talmud.

rav-hanina-of-sura.bio: Rav Hanina of Sura was an amora who lived during the seventh generation of Babylonian amoraim, and was among the sages of Sura. He is frequently mistakenly referred to as "Rabbi" in many sources.

rav-aha-b-rava.bio: Rav Aha b. Rava was a prominent Babylonian Amora of the seventh generation. Born in Babylonia, he was the son of Rava, a kohen; some identify his father with the famous Rava, colleague of Abaye, while others suggest it was a different Rava, since the famous one was not a kohen. He studied under Rav Kahana of Pum Nahara, Rav Ashi, Amemar, and Mar Zutra, and was a colleague of Ravina. After Mar Zutra's death, he succeeded him as head of the Pumbedita yeshiva.

rav-amemar-b-mar-yanuka.bio: Rav Amemar b. Mar Yanuka was a Babylonian Amora of the seventh and eighth generations and a student of Rav Hiyya b. Rav Avya. He was among the dignitaries of the Jewish community in his generation, near the end of the Amoraic period, when many troubles befell the Jews of Babylonia. He was arrested together with two other Jewish dignitaries and executed during the reign of Peroz I, king of the Sasanian Empire.

rav-yosef-b-rava.bio: Rav Yosef b. Rava was a seventh-generation Babylonian Amora, the son of Rava, and head of the Pumbedita yeshiva. He studied under Rav Yosef for six years. It is related that after his engagement he went to study with Rav Yosef; when he returned home early to see his wife, his father Rava came out toward him with weapons, enraged that he remembered his wife instead of being immersed in Torah, and their quarrel caused them both to miss the pre-Yom Kippur meal. His father helped him understand difficult teachings of Rav Yosef, such as reconciling conflicting opinions on how many incidents establish a chazakah. He reported his father's stringencies, including never walking without tzitzit, and assisted Rava in complex rulings, notably technical examinations in the laws of treifot.

rav-rechumi-ii.bio: Rav Rechumi II was a Babylonian Amora of the seventh generation. He served as head of the Pumbedita academy after Rafram from 449 to 493. He was a student of Ravina, colleague of Rav Ashi, and reports customs of his teacher Ravina in Tractate Yoma. Near the end of his life, King Yazdegerd decreed a persecution upon the Jews of Babylonia.

mar-b-rav-ashi.bio: Mar b. Rav Ashi, who signed with the nickname Tavyomi, was a Babylonian Amora of the seventh generation. The son of Rav Ashi and his preeminent student, he transmitted halakhot in his father's name. He headed the yeshiva in Mata Mehasia, a suburb of Sura, from 455 CE for some thirteen years until his death on Motzaei Yom Kippur in 467 CE, having been appointed twenty-eight years after his father's death. He continued his father's great enterprise of editing the Babylonian Talmud.

rav-sama-b-rava.bio: Rav Sama b. Rava was a Babylonian Amora of the seventh generation. Upon Rav Rachumi's death in 456 CE, he was appointed head of the Pumbedita academy, the same post held by his father Rava (distinct from the earlier Amora of that name), serving until his death. He is cited in the Talmud only twice, once regarding kashrut and once regarding Jewish civil law.

rav-achai.bio: Rav Achai, also known as Rav Acha, was a Savora of the second generation, on the border of the first generation. He is the Savora most frequently mentioned in the Babylonian Talmud, where his questions are regularly introduced with the formula "Parich Rav Achai" ("Rav Achai objected"). The sages of Eretz Yisrael said of him: "Be careful with our teacher Achai, for he illuminates the eyes of the Diaspora." Various hypotheses have been proposed regarding his exact identity and period.

rav-avina-bar-rav-huna.bio: Rav Avina bar Rav Huna, known as Ravina the Last, was a Babylonian Amora of the eighth and final generation. He succeeded his teacher Rabba Tosfa'ah as head of the yeshiva in Mata Mehasia, a suburb of Sura. He completed the editing of the Babylonian Talmud begun by Rav Ashi and Ravina the First, sealing the teachings of earlier generations shortly before his death in 499. His death marked the end of the Amoraic period of hora'ah. The Talmudic statement "Rav Ashi and Ravina are the end of hora'ah" (Bava Metzia 86a) was applied by Rav Sherira Gaon to Rav Ashi and to him as the one who sealed the Talmud. Subsequent sages, including some of his students, were known as the Savoraim, who explained the Gemara.

rav-huna-gaon.bio: Rav Huna Gaon was among the first heads of the Sura yeshiva. He served as Gaon following the death of Rav Chanina Gaon in 640 for about ten years. Some opinions regard him as one of the Savoraim. Some of his statements appear in the Babylonian Talmud, and he is credited by some with composing the first sugya in Tractate Kiddushin. He died in 650 and was succeeded by Rav Sheshna Gaon.

aliases: Mar Zutra son of Mar Zutra
mar-zutra-b-mar-zutra.bio: Mar Zutra b. Mar Zutra, also called Mar Zutra III, was the only son of the Exilarch Mar Zutra II in Babylonia at the beginning of the Savoraic period. His father rebelled against Sasanian rule and was hanged on the bridge of Mahoza after King Kavadh I suppressed the revolt. According to Seder Olam Zuta, Mar Zutra was born on the day of his father's execution and named after him. He ascended to Eretz Yisrael in 520 CE and headed the court of sages there, while Savoraic sages were active in Babylonia.

rav-hanina-gaon.bio: Rav Hanina Gaon was one of the heads of the third generation of Savoraim in the seventh century and served as head of the Sura Yeshiva in Babylonia during the late Sasanian and early Arab periods. According to another view, based on the epistle of Rav Sherira Gaon, he is considered one of the heads of the first generation of Geonim in Sura.

rav-rabbah-tosfaah.bio: Rav Rabbah Tosfaah was a Babylonian Amora of the eighth generation, active at the end of the Amoraic period. According to Rav Sherira Gaon, he headed the Sura academy for some six years after Mar bar Rav Ashi, from 467 to 474 CE, and is numbered among the editors of the Talmud. Regarding his epithet 'Tosfaah', one view holds that he added to the Talmud during its final redaction; another attributes it to his expertise in beraitot and the Tosefta. Epstein, however, contended that these explanations are unfounded and that the name refers to his place of origin.

rav-yitzchak-of-peroz-shapur.bio: Rav Yitzchak of Peroz Shapur was the final head of the Yeshiva of Peroz Shapur in Babylonia during the fourth generation of the Savoraim in the seventh century. The yeshiva had served for several generations as a third Babylonian academy alongside Pumbedita and Sura. When the Muslims conquered Babylonia under Ali ibn Abi Talib, Rav Yitzchak went out to greet him with a large Jewish congregation. Some regard his generation as the transitional period between the Savoraim and the Geonim.

rav-hanan-of-ishkiya.bio: Rav Hanan of Ishkiya (also called Ishka) was the head of the Pumbedita yeshiva in the third generation of the Savoraim during the 6th century. In 589 he re-established the yeshiva in Pumbedita after it had been closed. Some scholars view this act as marking the beginning of the Geonic period, and according to that opinion he is considered the first of the Geonim. His teachers were the sages of the second and third generations of the Savoraim.

mar-b-rav-huna.bio: Mar b. Rav Huna (died c. 614 or 620) was a leading figure of the third generation of Savoraim in the seventh century and head of the Sura Academy in Babylonia during the late Sasanian Empire. He reopened the Sura academy after it had been inactive for over fifty years due to persecutions and pogroms against the Jews of Babylonia in the Savoraic period. According to another view, based on the Iggeret of Rav Sherira Gaon, he is regarded as the first of the Geonim of Sura.

rabbah-yosei.bio: Rabbah Yosei was among the last Babylonian Amoraim and the first generation of Savoraim in the fifth century. He headed the Pumbedita academy from 476 until his death in 514. He assisted Ravina in completing the first stage of the Babylonian Talmud's redaction, and under his leadership the Savoraic editing of the Talmud began, consisting mainly of explanations, completions, and halakhic rulings.

rav-simuna.bio: Rav Simuna was a leading Savora of the second generation in Babylonia and head of the Pumbedita academy. He belonged to the Savoraim, the Jewish sages active in Babylonia following the Amoraic period. In his era, Rav Ena headed the Sura academy. He died in 540 CE.

rav-ravai-of-rov.bio: Rav Ravai of Rov was a leading Savora in Babylonia of the second-third generation. He became head of the Pumbedita Yeshiva after Rav Simuna died in 540 CE. A native of Rov, near Nehardea, he led the academy during persecutions that forced the sages and their students to relocate from Pumbedita to Firuz Shapur, also near Nehardea. Like other Savoraim, little is known of him, as their Talmudic additions were mostly anonymous; most information derives from Rav Sherira Gaon's epistle.

ravina-b-amotzia.bio: Ravina b. Amotzia was a Savora of the second generation, bordering on the first. According to Iggeret Rav Sherira Gaon, he died in Adar of 507. In Sefer HaKabbalah, Rabbi Abraham ibn Daud lists him among second-generation Savoraim as Ravina of Umtza, stating that he headed the Pumbedita academy for one year after Rav Shmuel b. Rava and died in 519; he was succeeded by Rav Simuna. Some scholars, however, understand from Iggeret Rav Sherira Gaon that he belonged to the first generation, the era of Rabbah Yosei, and was not head of the academy.

rav-shmuel-b-rav-avahu.bio: Rav Shmuel b. Rav Avahu was a Jewish sage of the second generation of Savoraim, on the border of the first generation. He was the son of Rav Avahu, a sage of Pumbedita in the late Amoraic period. His colleague and disputant was Rav Achai, and they appear together in two sugyot in the Babylonian Talmud.

rav-sheshena-gaon.bio: Rav Sheshena Gaon, who signed his name as Mesharsheya b. Tahlifa, was the head of the Sura Yeshiva in the seventh century. He belonged to the fifth and final generation of the Savoraim. Some scholars view him as an intermediary between the Savoraim and the Geonim, while others see him as part of a transitional period between the two eras.

rav-rechumi-iii.bio: Rav Rechumi III was an early Savora who edited and completed the Babylonian Talmud after the Amoraic period. According to Rav Sherira Gaon, he died in Nisan 506. He was a student of Rabbah Tosfa'ah and engaged in halakhic discourse with Rabbah Yosei, head of the Pumbedita yeshiva; one of their disputes concerning the meaning of "petchei shima'i" is cited twice in the Talmud, in Eruvin and Menachot. He is called "the Third" because two earlier Amoraim also bore the name Rav Rechumi. Some scholars identify him with the Talmudic figure Rav Nechumi (or Rav Nechumai), who argues with Rabbah about courtyard eruv, or with Rabbi Nechunya, who inquires about treifot in Eretz Yisrael.
